questions.stem: Synchronized cardioversion is used in an attempt to terminate certain cardiac dysrhythmias. At which part of the ECG is the shock delivered when synchronization occurs successfully?
questions.explanation: The R wave avoids the shock being delivered on the T wave inducing VF. Although any of the QRS would be suitable to avoid delivering the shock during the relative refractory period of the ventricle, the R wave is chosen as this is usually the largest signal and hence can be discriminated from the T wave.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents for review. His past medical history includes a parathyroidectomy and a recent gastroscopy for dyspepsia which shows peptic ulcer disease.What is the most likely inherited genetic mutation?
questions.explanation: This is a classic presentation of Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1. The clinical diagnosis of MEN1 is based upon the occurrence of two or more primary MEN1 tumor types (parathyroid gland, anterior pituitary, and enteropancreatic). Dyspepsia in a young person is the clue.

questions.stem: Which immunoglobulin causes haemolytic transfusion reactions?
questions.explanation: Immune-mediated hemolytic transfusion reactions caused by immunoglobulin M (IgM) anti-A, anti-B, or anti-A,B typically result in severe, potentially fatal complementmediated intravascular hemolysis. IgG mediated reactions can also occur, but these are less common.

questions.stem: A liver biopsy of a patient shows "globular hyaline material within hepatocytes".What is the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Alcohol-associated liver disease includes macrovesicular steatosis that is characterized by a single large vacuole or fat droplet occupying the entire hepatocyte and thereby pushing the nucleus to the edge of the cell. Hepatocytes may also contain MalloryDenk hyalines surrounded by neutrophils (termed Mallory-Denk bodies), which are more characteristic of alcohol-associated steatohepatitis than NASH. - In NAFLD there is hepatic steatosis characterized by fat droplets in the hepatocytes in association with ballooning degeneration and lobular inflammation. - Autoimmune hepatitis is characterised by lobular inflammation and portal inflammation with many plasma cells. Histologic features of hereditary hemochromatosis include excess iron deposition, and in some patients, uniformly micronodular cirrhosis that is demonstrated by hypocellular fibrous bands. - In primary sclerosing cholangitis the most specific histologic finding is fibrous obliteration of small bile ducts, with concentric replacement by connective tissue in an "onion skin" pattern.

questions.stem: Regarding the steady state of a drug, which of the following is incorrect

options.text: It takes five half lives to reach a steady state of 97%
Steady state is achieved when dose rate is equal to elimination rate
Steady state is affected by loading dose and volume of distribution
A loading dose achieves a steady state immediately
Concentration of a drug at steady state is mostly determined by the drugs half life

questions.explanation: Steady state is achieved when the rate of drug input is equal to the rate of elimination, so plasma concentrations are the same for each drug interval. Time to reach steady state is dependent on half-life of the drug. 97% steady state is seen after 5 half-lives. - Dose rate = Clearance (Cl) × Steady State Drug Concentration (Css) Loading dose = Vd × Css ­ so it is affected by loading dose and volume of distribution. - A loading dose rapidly achieves the peak concentration necessary to compete with clearance, so that the desired constant rate is achieved and maintained sooner but not immediately.

questions.stem: In the pathogenesis of type 1 diabetes, which antigen is beta cell specific?
questions.explanation: Insulin is the only self-antigen specific for -cells, and several lines of evidence indicate that it may play a major role in driving autoimmune -cell destruction. Anti-GAD and anti-IA2 are seen in type 1 diabetes but not specific for beta cell autoimmunity. Transglutaminase antigen is seen in coeliac disease.

questions.stem: Regarding the normal physiology of melatonin, which one of the following statements is incorrect?

options.text: Plasma concentrations ascend after the onset of darkness and peak between 11pm and 3am in most individuals
Melatonin's MT1 and MT2 receptors are highly susceptible to desensitization, with their activity decreasing markedly after exposure to high melatonin concentrations
Night time peak serum melatonin levels drop to 25% or less in people aged 70 or over

Norepinephrine activates serotonin-N-acetyltransferase in pinealocytes converting
stored serotonin to melatonin
Melatonin is largely albumin- bound and is non lipid soluble

questions.explanation: Melatonin is albumin bound but is highly lipid soluble meaning it can freely cross cell membranes and the blood brain barrier. All the other statements regarding melatonin are correct.

questions.stem: Which translocation on cytogenetic analysis would be diagnostic of follicular lymphoma?
questions.explanation: Although no single chromosomal finding is diagnostic, the majority of follicular lymphoma is driven by translocation of heavy chain Ig on Chr14 and BCL-2 on Chr18.

questions.stem: What is the underlying mechanism for the most prevalent inherited thrombophilia?
questions.explanation: Factor V Leiden is the most common inherited thrombophilia. This is caused by a mutation in factor V, resulting in resistance to inactivation of protein C. - Activated protein S resistance and antithrombin III deficiency cause thrombophilia but are not as common as Factor V Leiden. - Von Willebrand factor deficiency is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. - ADAMTS13 is an enzyme that cleaves vWF allowing controlled binding of platelets to damaged endothelium. ADAMTS13 mutation results in thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP).

questions.stem: A medical student presents to his GP feeling fatigued and passing dark urine after eating at a vegan restaurant the night before. He looks pale. Bloods are remarkable for hemoglobin 101 g/L and haptoglobin 0.1 g/L (0.7-3.2). What mechanism best describes the cause of the low serum haptoglobin?

options.text: There is increased breakdown of haptoglobin in haemolysis

questions.explanation: Haptoglobin binds to free hemoglobin. Free hemoglobin is increased due to haemolysis in this patient with undiagnosed G6PD deficiency, who ate fava beans. - Haemopexin binds to free haem. - Transferrin binds free iron. - Increased breakdown and reduced production of haptoglobin are incorrect.

questions.stem: Which of the following is not an inhibitory signal for hunger?
questions.explanation: Ghrelin is a hormone which activates the growth hormone secretagogue receptor (GHS-R). Ghrelin's functions are its stimulatory effects on food intake, fat deposition and growth hormone release. All the other options listed are examples of inhibitory signals for hunger and food intake.

questions.stem: What is the lifetime risk of type 1 diabetes in a child born to a mother diagnosed with type 1 diabetes in her teenage years?
questions.explanation: Risk of a child developing type 1 diabetes from a parent diagnosed with type 1 diabetes at 16 years is 5-7%.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of the monoclonal antibody denosumab?

options.text: Competitively binds to RANK ligand on osteoclasts inhibiting their growth
Binds to RANK ligand on osteoblasts stimulating new bone growth
Inhibit osteoclasts via inhibiting the enzyme FFP synthase
Causes osteoclasts apoptosis by modifying ATP

questions.explanation: The monoclonal antibody binds the cytokine RANKL (receptor activator of NFB ligand), an essential factor initiating bone turnover. RANKL inhibition blocks osteoclast maturation, function and survival, thus reducing bone resorption.

questions.stem: Which of the following has not been associated with increased risk of developing rheumatoid arthritis?
questions.explanation: STAT4 polymorphisms, not STAT1 have been shown to be associated with increased risk of rheumatoid arthritis.

questions.stem:
Which cytokine is the target for a monoclonal antibody found to have benefit in relapsing

giant cell arteritis?
questions.explanation: Tocilizumab targets IL-6 and benefit was seen in the GiACTA trial in relapsing giant cell arteritis.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is incorrect, with regard to renal physiology in pregnancy?

options.text: Renal blood flow is increased by over 50%
There is a lower threshold for vasopressin release

questions.explanation: Glycosuria in pregnancy is increased and is part of normal physiology in pregnancy.

questions.stem: Vincristine is commonly used in chemotherapy regimes for high risk Hodgkin's lymphoma. At what stage in the cell cycle does this cytotoxic agent act?
questions.explanation: Metaphase is the phase of the cell cycle when the chromosomes align at the middle of the cell and then begin to separate. Vincristine disrupts this process by binding to the tubulin protein, preventing the formation of microtubules and preventing the initiation of chromosome separation. The cell will then undergo apoptosis.

questions.stem: In early breast cancer, patients identified through breast screening have an associated reduction in mortality as a result of earlier detection. This is an example of which type of bias?
questions.explanation: Lead time bias in screening programmes occurs often. It occurs when comparing survival of patients detected through screening versus patients detected from clinical findings.

questions.stem: Which gene mutation is seen in medullary thyroid cancer?
questions.explanation: C-RET oncogene mutations are commonly seen in medullary thyroid cancer.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of aciclovir?
questions.explanation: Aciclovir is a guanosine analog, phosphorylated by thymidine kinase to aciclovir monophosphate and subsequently by cellular enzymes to aciclovir triphosphate, which in turn inhibits the viral DNA polymerase.

questions.stem: In familial adenomatous polyposis, the recommended management in mutation positive and at-risk family members should include all of the following, except:

options.text: Notification of at risk family members

questions.explanation:
FAP has 100% risk of colorectal cancer if left untreated. Recommended screening includes annual sigmoidoscopy from age 12 years and two yearly gastroscopy from 25 years as gastric and duodenal polyposis is a recognised extra-colonic feature (and potential cause of mortality post-colectomy).
Notification of at risk family members, genetic counselling, and advising afflicted families to enrol in a family cancer register is also recommended.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman on neoadjuvant chemotherapy and anti-HER2 therapy for locally invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast presents with progressive exertional dyspnea. Examination is consistent with left ventricular failure. Trastuzumab-related cardiotoxicity is suspected. Which of the following statements is correct regarding trastuzumab?

options.text: Adjuvant treatment with radiotherapy increases the risk of cardiac toxicity

Trastuzumab- related cardiotoxicity is usually reversible and most patients can
tolerate a continued use or a rechallenge
Trastuzumab- related cardiotoxicity is rarely reversible

Trastuzumab- related cardiotoxicity is often not reversible and a repeat trial is not
recommended

In the adjuvant setting a baseline assessment of LVEF prior to commencement is only
recommended in patients with established cardiac disease

questions.explanation: Trastuzumab-related cardiotoxicity is reversible in many patients and responds to standard treatment for heart failure. Many patients tolerate continued treatment or rechallenge with trastuzumab. Previous or concurrent anthracycline use and age greater than 50 years are the strongest risk factors for development of trastuzumab-related cardiotoxicity. - Concurrent treatment with radiotherapy does not increase the risk. - In the adjuvant setting, a baseline assessment prior to starting trastuzumab and serial LVEF monitoring (at 3, 6, and 12 months after initiating trastuzumab and 18 months after initiating an anthracycline or other chemotherapy) are appropriate to screen for cardiac dysfunction.

questions.stem: Which of the following antibodies is associated with immune mediated necrotising myopathies?
questions.explanation: HMG CoA reductase antibodies are rare, but when they occur are often triggered by treatment with statins. A severe necrotising myopathy can occur, with profound weakness, muscle pain and dramatically elevated CK. The antibody often persists for months or years after the statin is discontinued. - Anti-Jo1 is associated with Polymyositis, especially within the context of the antisynthetase syndrome. - Anti-Scl-70 is associated with diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis - Anti-sm is associated with with renal and neuropsychiatric Lupus - Anti-GBM is associated with with Goodpasture's Disease.

questions.stem: Which serum antibodies are associated with the Miller-Fisher Syndrome?
questions.explanation: Anti-GQ1b. Miller-Fisher syndrome (MFS), is a variant of Guillain Barre Syndrome and constitutes about 5% of presentations. An cute onset of external ophthalmoplegia is the cardinal feature. Ataxia, mild limb weakness, ptosis, facial palsy, and bulbar palsy may be seen. - Anti-MOG is associated with Anti-MOG syndrome. - Voltage gated calcium channel (VGCC) antibodies are associated with Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome. - Acetylcholine receptor antibodies are seen in Myasthenia Gravis.

questions.stem: Which of the following options most accurately describes the primary reason for avoiding morphine in patients with advanced chronic kidney disease?

options.text: Morphine is metabolised to the active metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide in the kidney
Morphine is metabolised to the active metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide which is renally excreted
Morphine is predominantly excreted by the liver

Morphine is metabolised to the inactive metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide by the
kidney
Morphine causes acute tubular necrosis in chronic kidney disease

questions.explanation: Morphine is metabolised to the active metabolite morphine-6-glucuronide which is renally excreted.

questions.stem: Which pharmacodynamic property of clindamycin is the most important in determining its efficacy? *Note: MIC = Minimum Inhibitory Concentration

options.text: Time the concentration is above the MIC

questions.explanation: Clindamycin is lipophilic and reaches most tissues in a similar concentration. The time the concentration is above the minimum inhibitory concentration of the drug has been shown to be the most important factor in determining efficacy in both beta lactams and lincosamide classes. Free drug levels should be greater than the MIC in >50% of the drug dosing interval. In contrast, aminoglycosides and quinolones need a maximum plasma drug concentration. Their effect is concentration dependent.

questions.stem: Ipilimumab is a targeted therapy that is used for metastatic melanoma. Which of the following best describes the drug's mechanism of action?
questions.explanation: CTLA-4 inhibitor. CTLA-4 (cytotoxic T-lymphocyte-associated protein 4) is a protein receptor that functions as an immune checkpoint and downregulates immune responses. CTLA-4 inhibitors "turns off" this inhibitory mechanism thus increasing the immune response against cancer cells.

questions.stem: What is the approximate bioequivalence of a 40mg IV dose of furosemide compared to an oral dose?
questions.explanation: 2.0. In fasting samples form healthy volunteers, the mean bioavailability of furosemide when taken orally is 64% of that observed after an intravenous injection of the drug.

questions.stem: The herpes virus Epstein Barr establishes latent infection in which immune cell?
questions.explanation: EBV establishes latent infection in B lymphocytes and epithelial cells. Under circumstances like immunosuppression there may be reactivation of the virus inducing lymphoproliferative disorders, especially Burkitt's lymphoma.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old patient with severe depression is brought in by her partner. She was found in their flat semiconscious and overdose is suspected. Which ECG changes are most consistent with nortriptyline taken in overdose?
questions.explanation: Tricyclic antidepressants in overdose cause ECG changing including sinus tachycardia, widening of the QRS and prolongation of the QT interval.

questions.stem:
A serological marker for a cancer screening has been through a preliminary trial, the results are as follows: Disease present Disease absent Test Positive 120 40 Test negative 60 700 Which of the following is the highest?

Test positive Test negative

Disease present 120 60

Disease absent 40 700

Which of the following is the highest?
questions.explanation:
Specificity
Cancer present Test + 120 TP
- 60 FN 180

Cancer absent

40 FP

160

200 TN

260

240

Sensitivity = TP/condition positive = 120/180 = 2/3 Specificity = TN/condition negative = 200/240 = 5/6 PPV = TP/test positive = 120/160 = 3/4 NPV = TN/test negative = 200/260 = 10/13 = 5/6.5

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with end stage COPD presents to a local hospital in his holiday destination due to worsening dyspnea and sputum production over the past few days. He is kept in an observation area overnight because the cruise ship refused him entry back on board. The following day his sputum sample shows heavy growth of a gram-negative cocci. What is the likely pathogen?
questions.explanation: Morexella catarrhalis is a gram-negative cocci that commonly causes respiratory infections in those with underlying lung disease. - Haemophilus influenzae, Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Klebsiella pneumoniae are gramnegative rods. - Streptococcus pneumoniae is a gram-positive cocci.

questions.stem: In Parkinson's disease excessive amounts of which protein accumulates in the central nervous system?
questions.explanation: Alpha synuclein is seen in Parkinson's disease. - Tau protein is seen in Alzheimer's and forms the typical Neurofibrillary tangles. - Amyloid deposition is seen in various conditions including Alzheimer's along with Tau. - Prions are responsible for the plaques that drive Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease. - Albumin is the main protein found in serum.

questions.stem: The main determinants of hepatic clearance of a drug include the hepatic extraction ratio and which other parameter?
questions.explanation: The two main determinants of hepatic clearance are hepatic extraction ratio and hepatic blood flow. Hepatic extraction ratio is the fraction of the drug entering the liver in the blood which is irreversibly extracted during a pass through the liver. The hepatic extraction ratio is determined largely by the free (unbound) fraction of the drug.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with sudden onset lower back pain which came on whilst lifting a box from her car boot. She complains of pain in her right leg. Positive neurological findings on lower limb examination include mild weakness in plantar flexion, reduced right ankle reflex, decreased sensation to the posterolateral aspect of the right leg and sole of the foot. Sciatic nerve stretch test is positive. At which nerve root is the prolapsed disc causing compression?
questions.explanation: Compression at the nerve root of S1 can cause sensory loss over the posterolateral aspect of the leg and lateral aspect of the foot and reduced ankle reflexes. Motor weakness is noted in plantar flexion (S1 `S1nks' your foot to the floor). - L2 nerve root compression causes pain radiating to anterior thigh through the posterior pelvis and buttock, there can be weakness on hip flexion due to weakness of iliopsoas. - L3 and L4 - sensory loss anterior thigh, weak quadriceps, reduced knee reflex. - L5 - sensory loss dorsum of foot, weakness in foot and hallux dorsiflexion.

questions.stem: Which of the following pathogens is associated with the development of adult T-cell leukaemia?
questions.explanation: HTLV-1 is associated with adult T-cell leukaemia. - Helicobacter pylori is associated with gastric lymphoma. - Malaria is associated with Burkitt's lymphoma. - HIV-1 is associated with B cell lymphoma. - EBV is associated with Hodgkin's lymphoma, Burkitt's lymphoma, and nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

questions.stem: Which one of the following statements regarding chronic lead poisoning is true?

options.text: Lead levels are never elevated to toxic levels from gunshot wounds alone
Beau's lines may be seen in the nails clinical
Blood film abnormalities are common including microcytosis and basophilic stippling

questions.explanation: Blood film abnormalities in chronic lead poisoning include microcytosis with basophilic stippling, clover leaf morphology, and uncleared red blood cells. - Beau's lines are indentations in the nail seen in chronic prolonged illness or injury. `Lead lines' may be seen on the gum in lead toxicity. - Gunshot wounds are a common cause of elevated lead levels when projectile fragments are retained in the body. They can cause toxicity - specially if multiple. - Desferrioxamine is used to treat iron toxicity. Lead toxicity is treated with chelating agents including D-penicillamine (no longer easily available) and EDTA. Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant, predominantly used to treat hypercholesterolaemia (used rarely now statins are freely available).

questions.stem: George and Hank are brothers and both have haemophilia A. What is the likelihood their sister's child will develop haemophilia A?
questions.explanation: Haemophilia A is X-linked recessive. George and Hank's mother is highly likely to be a carrier, as two affected sons with hemophilia A. - The chance of their sister being a carrier is ½. - The chance of the sister's daughter being a carrier is ½ × ½ which is ¼. - Only male pregnancies will develop hemophilia A, so the chance the pregnancy is male and inherits the mutation is ¼ × ½ = .

questions.stem:
This natural log scale illustration shows the concentration and effect of three drugs
Which statement is incorrect regarding Drug A, B and C?

options.text: The potency of Drug A is greater than Drug B
The potency of Drug C is greater than Drug B
The efficacy of Drug C is greater than Drug B
The efficacy of Drug A is greater than Drug C

questions.explanation:
Potency is expressed in terms of ED50, which is the plasma concentration required to give 50% max response of the drug. Potency is expressed in terms of the ED50 (gradient

and lateral position of the curve). In this question A and B have the same ED50 (see diagram below). C will be greater then A and B Potency A=B >C Efficacy is defined by Emax. E max = the maximal effect when all the receptors are occupied by a drug. In this question A and C have the highest E max. Efficacy A = C>B

questions.stem: Which cytokine is predominantly associated with pro-inflammatory Th1 response and drives the rise in CRP?
questions.explanation: IL-6 is the main driver of the CRP response. Elevated IL-6 levels are seen in Giant cell arteritis, and Juvenile Idiopathic Systemic Arthritis as well as in infections. It is part of the cytokine storm response seen in Covid-19 severe infections. Sustained high levels are associated with amyloidosis. Tocilizumab is a monoclonal antibody to IL-6 which reduces CRP predictably.

questions.stem: A fit elderly female (76 years old) who lives independently with no known medical problems is found to have a serum calcium of 2.9 mmol/L on routine testing, subsequently PTH is tested and is 35 ng/L (10-65). She is asymptomatic on questioning and has no history of renal calculi. Urine calcium:creat ratio is in the normal range. There is no previous fracture history. What is the next step in management?
questions.explanation:
This patient has primary hyperparathyroidism (high calcium and non-suppressed PTH) which is asymptomatic.
This question is focused on the indications for surgery. A diagnostic sestamibi scan should only be ordered if surgical excision of an active parathyroid gland is planned. Indications for surgery include symptomatic hypercalcaemia, renal calculi, or osteoporosis.
It would be reasonable to do a DEXA scan next as if osteoporosis was found this would be an indication to consider surgery in an otherwise well, elderly patient.

questions.stem: Dulaglutide is a GLP-1 agonist injected once weekly for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. If used with metformin alone, which of the following is NOT a recognised adverse effect of this medication?
questions.explanation: Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists bind to and activate the GLP-1 receptor to increase insulin secretion in the presence of glucose, suppressing glucagon secretion and slowing gastric emptying. They do not cause hypoglycemia.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old publican describes a long history of retrosternal burning pain after eating. A trial of lansoprazole did not help his symptoms. Severe oesophagitis is found on upper gastrointestinal endoscopy. A biopsy is taken of a concerning lesion in the lower portion of the oesophagus, histology confirms squamous cell carcinoma. Which of the following is the biggest risk factor for this pathology?
questions.explanation: Smoking carries the highest risk for oesophageal SSC followed by alcoholism. Family history is not a risk factor. GORD and obesity increase the risk of adenocarcinoma.

questions.stem:
A 75-year-old man with a history of stable angina and provoked DVTs is admitted to high dependency unit due to severe community acquired pneumonia. His medication list includes, aspirin, augmentin, clarithromycin, prophylactic clexane and he has TED stockings. His full blood count on day 7 of admission is shown:

Hb Platelets WCC

14.1 g/L 21 *10^9/L 13.2 *10^9/L

What is the most likely cause of this man's thrombocytopenia?
questions.explanation:
Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) is immune mediated, where antibodies form against complexes of platelet factor 4 and heparin.

Unlike other conditions caused by enhanced consumption, impaired production, or destruction of platelets leading to bleeding complications, HIT results in a paradoxical prothrombotic state. Patients who receive unfractionated heparin for 7 to 10 days are at the highest risk; with an incidence 1 to 3% after cardiac surgery. Thromboembolic complications develop in approximately 50% of patients with HIT.

In one study, Platelets factor 4 antibodies were found in 9.1% of patients receiving unfractionated heparin and 2.8% of patients receiving LMWH group after 5­7 days of treatment.

questions.stem:
A 50 year old man presents following his first ever seizure to the Emergency Department in a confused state, hemodynamically stabile but with a high fever of 39 degrees. There are no lateralizing neurological signs. He has multiple cutananeous petechiae on his limbs. Urgent blood tests show:

Hemoglobin MCV WCC Platelets Blood film: Haptoglobin: Creat ALP ALT GGT Bilirubin

81 g/L

(115-165)

100fL

(80-99)

12.1

(4-11)

(150-450)

Schistocytes: helmet cells, fragmented red cells 11 mg/dL (50-220) 128 mmol/L (45-90)

37 U/L

(30-150)

15 U/L

(0-30)

18 U/L

(10-35)

39 umol/L (2-20)

What is the most appropriate initial investigation or management?
questions.explanation: This patient has thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) which is a medical emergency and is treated with plasma exchange. - Platelet transfusions are not recommended in TPP treatment. - The ADAMTS-13 antibody test will confirm the diagnosis but takes several days and you cannot wait for the result before treatment is commenced. - Rituximab is an effective treatment but may take several days or weeks to take effect. Therefore, plasma exchange is life saving in the initial treatment regime.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to her GP with fatigue and pruritus. Examination reveals mild hepatomegaly and clubbing. Her history is positive only for Hashimoto's thyroiditis for which she takes levothyroxine. Serology shows mild hepatic pattern derangement of liver enzymes. A non-invasive liver screen is positive for anti-mitochondrial antibodies. What treatment has been shown to delay the progression of this disease?
questions.explanation: This woman has a history of autoimmune disease and her presentation and serology suggest this is primary biliary cirrhosis. Treatment with UDCA has been associated with improved outcomes in patients with PBC.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old shearer presents with a 2-year history of intermittent dark, foamy urine. He has been noted to be hypertensive. Tests reveal he has stage 5 chronic kidney disease? What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: IgA nephropathy is the leading cause of glomerulonephritis requiring renal replacement therapy.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old woman has a 20-year history of Type 2 Diabetes mellitus. She has been noted to have persistent proteinuria over the last 4 years and was started on an ACE inhibitor, with initial reduction in proteinuria. However, her urine protein has now started to rise exponentially over the last 9 months. Which of the following interventions DO NOT delay progression of renal failure in patients with proteinuria?

options.text: Bicarbonate therapy if evidence of metabolic acidosis

questions.explanation: The combination of an ACE inhibitor and an ARB reduces proteinuria, but has not been shown to delay progression to end stage renal failure.

questions.stem: A patient is admitted to the intensive care unit following cholecystitis complicated by a perforated gallbladder and septic shock. She requires urgent dialysis for anuric renal failure and refractory hyperkalaemia. Which of the following parameters would favour continuous renal replacement therapy over intermittent haemodialysis?
questions.explanation: Continuous renal replacement minimises the large fluid shifts that are seen with intermittent dialysis.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with sudden onset of profound expressive dysphasia without confusion, preceded by a headache. She has no previous history of note and no family history. On examination she has livedo reticularis and mild right upper limb weakness. A CT scan shows an acute infarct in the left middle cerebral artery territory. Which investigation is most likely to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This patient is most likely to have antiphospholipid syndrome, with livedo reticularis being one clue, and her age and gender strongly suggesting this also. Von Willebrand disease is a bleeding disorder. - ANA may be negative. - Anticardiolipin antibody is often positive although Lupus anticoagulant positivity carries the highest risk for arterial thrombosis. - Beta-2 glycoprotein 1 is a rare antibody usually only tested if other antibodies are negative.

questions.stem:
A 32-year-old woman is found to have hypertension on an ambulatory monitor with average BP 164/95. She is not established on any medications. There is no family history of hypertension at a young age. Examination is non-contributory for secondary causes of hypertension.

Plasma Renin Plasma Aldosterone Serum potassium Urinary metanephrines

<40 fmol/L (130-2350) 360 pmol/L (80-1040) 3.9 mmol/L (3.5-5.5) 1.3 umol/24hr (<1.5)

Which of the following tests is most likely to reveal the underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation: She has a raised aldosterone:renin ratio and therefore the diagnosis is primary hyperaldosteronism.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old woman presents with a 12-month history of worsening breathlessness and a dry cough. She has no other symptoms, and no occupational exposure. Examination reveals fine crepitation and reduced air entry but no other signs. An HRCT scan is performed. What does this show?
questions.explanation:
The honeycomb lung appearance is typical of UiP. This may be idiopathic or associated with many connective tissue disorders, including scleroderma, rheumatoid arthritis, and antisynthetase syndrome. - Asbestos exposure is possible but less likely - there is no history of asbestos exposure, although this cannot be ruled out on history alone. However, the changes on the HRCT are not typical for asbestosis, as pleural plaques/calcification are commonly seen. - PJP is associated with a ground glass appearance and there is nothing in the history to suggest she is

immunosuppressed. - Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency would cause emphysema and bronchietasis with liver involvement also.

questions.stem: A 56 year old Samoan male with Type 2 Diabetes melitus and a BMI of 35 has an Hb A1C of 56 despite treatment with Insulin daily and metformin. He is commenced on empagliflozin. Which of the following is INCORRECT?

options.text: Empagliflozin significantly reduces hospitalisation for heart failure in patients with high CV risk
Empagliflozin reduces incident or worsening nephropathy but not incident albuminuria

The concurrent insulin doses should be reduced when initiating as Empagliflozin an additional treatment
for glycaemic control
Urinary tract and genital infections are a relative contraindication for use

questions.explanation: It is unnecessary to reduce the dose of insulin in those with elevated persistently blood glucose or HbAIc. The pivotal study showed that although the higher dose (25mg) of Empagliflozin added to insulin resulted in an 8% difference increase in hypoglycemia compared to placebo, the lower dose (10mg) was similar to placebo. The average drop in mmol/l during the first 18 weeks where no dose reduction was made was <1 mmol.

questions.stem:
A 76-year-old man presents with a 24 month history of aching and pain around his hips and low back, especially at night. His CRP is <1, his ALP is 285. He has a bone scan as malignancy is felt likely - the results are shown below:
Which of the following is NOT an indication for treatment?
questions.explanation: This is Paget's disease. Raised serum ALP is common in Paget's but is not an indication for therapy. All the other options are indications for treatment. Heart failure is due to high output failure in Paget's disease.

questions.stem:
A 68-year-old woman is 12 hours post uncomplicated total hip joint replacement following a neck of femur fracture. She feels well, denying all symptoms asked.

Na+

126 mmol/L (135-145)

K+

4.4 mmol/L (3.5-5.2)

Urea

7.0 mmol/L (3.2-7.7)

Creatinine 88 umol/L (45-90) Serum osmol 260 mosm/L (275-295)
questions.explanation: Low serum osmolality and low serum sodium suggest SIADH. Fluid status and urinary sodium is required for definitive diagnosis. - Dehydration may cause a low sodium however the serum osmolality would more likely be normal or high. - Lithium can cause nephrogenic diabetes insipidus which results in raised serum osmolality and sodium. - Addisonian crisis would demonstrate hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia and we would expect her to be clinically unwell. - NSAIDS are associated with decreased free water and sodium excretion but would rarely be solely responsible for hypotonic hyponatremia.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man with hypertension, hyperlipidaemia and established type 2 diabetes suffers recurrent attacks of gout. His eGFR is 25 ml/min. His serum urate is 0.56mmol/L. Which of the following is the best treatment combination?

options.text: Commence allopurinol 300mg daily and voltaren 50mg BD
Commence allopurinol 50mg daily with colchicine 0.5mg daily
Commence probencid 500mg BD with ural satchets
Commence Prednisone 40mg daily with allopruinol 100mg daily

questions.explanation: This patient requires urate lowering therapy. Allopurinol is associated with DRESS syndrome if commenced at the usual dose in patients with significant renal impairment (especially at eGFR below 25ml/min). Start low (25-50mg daily) and titrate upwards to reduce the UA to <0.36mmol/L. On initiation gout flares are more likely. Low dose colchicine is safe even at this level of renal impairment. - Prednisone at high dose will lead to the usual GC complications. - Probenecid is ineffective below an eCGFR of 35ml/min.

questions.stem: An 84-year-old who lives in a rest home facility develops diarrhoea two days into treatment with broad spectrum antibiotics for a lower respiratory tract infection. She has been febrile since presentation. Faecal PCR is negative for other pathogens but positive for C. diff Antigen. C. diff toxin assay is negative. Which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?
questions.explanation: C. diff antigen demonstrates the presence of bacteria but not the pathological variance. Toxin assay demonstrates the presence of the pathological variant. As her PCR demonstrates no infective organisms and the toxin assay is negative, no specific change in antibiotic is necessary although it is reasonable in an attempt to stop the diarrhoea. Supportive care ensuring fluid replacement is all that is required. - Erythromycin may be considered for campylobacter and salmonella. - Metronidazole is the first choice for C. Diff with vancomycin indicated with metro failure or severe colitis. - Loperamide may be considered but the risk of toxic megacolon with age and comorbidity limits its use.

questions.stem: An alcoholic man is admitted to the urology service after transurethral resection of the prostate complicated by clot retention. The following morning he becomes agitated, combative, and aggressive towards staff. You suspect he has acute alcohol withdrawal. Which of the following agents would be most appropriate to use in this setting?
questions.explanation: Alcohol withdrawal is managed acutely using a benzodiazepine. A longer acting BZP with active metabolites is preferred over shorter acting, therefore midazolam (although effective) is not preferred over diazepam. Haloperidol, Quetiapine, and Ethanol reduce the frequency of minor symptoms but there is insufficient evidence supporting their control of seizure and delirium tremens.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman complains of discomfort and paraesthesia requiring her to stand or pace in the evenings. She has difficulty sleeping due to these symptoms. She has treated hypertension and dyslipidemia, glucose intolerance and obesity with a BMI of 38. She has no history of substance abuse. Which of the following is most likely to be deficient?
questions.explanation: This patient is displaying typical symptoms of restless leg syndrome which is associated with iron deficiency.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old patient with Parkinson's disease has severe and persistent nausea with associated weight loss. He has Rheumatoid arthritis and the nausea is worst the day after he takes Methotrexate, which is essential to control his inflammatory arthritis. Which is the best treatment to control his nausea?
questions.explanation: Domperidone is a dopamine receptor antagonist that minimally crosses the blood brain barrier thus has little effect on the extrapyramidal symptoms of Parkinson's. - Ondansetron is a serotonin receptor antagonist and may be used in patients with Parkinson's disease however constipation is a limiting side effect for its use in most. - Metoclopramide is a dopamine antagonist crossing the blood brain barrier and thus carries a risk of worsening Parkinson's symptoms. - Haloperidol is an example of a first generation antipsychotic and blocks dopamine potentially worsening Parkinsonian features.

questions.stem: A 92-year-old man presents with pneumonia. His past medical history includes treated hypertension and dyslipidemia. He develops rapidly conducted atrial fibrillation at a rate of 140 bpm and blood pressure is 90/60. He is asymptomatic. Electrolytes are normal and Troponin T is 30. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial therapy?
questions.explanation: This patient has atrial fibrillation with haemodynamic compromise. A 500ml fluid bolus may be all that is needed to raise the blood pressure and control the rate and is the first choice in this scenario. No intervention is clearly inappropriate. - Electrolyte replacement may be advised however biochemistry should guide that. - Oral beta-blockade carries a risk of acute deterioration in heart function. That is a significant risk in an elderly man with haemodynamic compromise. - Amiodarone is perhaps appropriate should initial management and electrolyte correction fail.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old lady with a BMI of 32 and treated hypertension presents with symptomatic palpitations for 3 days. Her current medications include a Metoprolol 23.75mg and an Enalapril 10mg. Her 12 lead ECG confirms atrial fibrillation at a rate of 90 bpm. Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy?

options.text: Flecainide given as a PRN "Pill in pocket"
Transoephageal echocardiogram prior to consideration of cardioversion

questions.explanation: AF of onset >48 hours requires anticoagulation for at least 3 weeks or no thrombus on TOE precardioversion. As she is symptomatic she should proceed to cardioversion. - Flecainide is an appropriate approach in the context of PAF however she requires an ECHO demonstrating a structurally normal heart before Flecainide can be used. - Increasing beta-blockade is less likely to help given her rate is well controlled and she remains symptomatic. - Amiodarone is the most effective antiarrhythmic however carries significant side effects that make it less favourable. - Straight to DC cardioversion carries a high risk of embolisation and is not recommended.

questions.stem:
A 73-year-old woman is referred to hospital with an acute kidney injury. She is day 3 of community management for a urinary tract infection. Her bloods show the following results:

Sodium Potassium Urea Creatinine Osmolality

138 mmol/L 4.8 mmol/L 6.4 mmol/L 165 umol/L 288 mosm/L

(135-145) (3.5-5.2) (3.2-7.7) (45-90) (275-295)

What is the most likely cause for the above results?
questions.explanation: Trimethoprim induces a reversible and rapid increase in serum creatinine and decrease in creatinine clearance independent of GFR. This is mediated through inhibition of tubular creatinine secretion. - Hypotension, dehydration, and NSAID would increase creatinine following an acute kidney injury however we would expect a similar rise in urea. - Metformin clearance is reduced and can lead to the development of lactic acidosis in acute deterioration.

questions.stem:
A 26 year old presents with multiple upper and lower respiratory tract infections. He has no family history of cystic fibrosis. He is otherwise well and takes no regular medications. He has normal FBC, renal and liver function tests.

Immunoglobulin profile is shown below

IgG < 1.41g/l IgA 0.25 g/l IgM 0.7g/l IgE < 2 IU/ml

(8-17) (0.85-4.9) (0.5-3.7) (< 120)

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Chronic variable immunodeficiency is characterised by this patient's presentation and immunoglobulin profile. - Bruton's X-Linked agammaglobulinemia typically present in the first decade of life and typically have low immunoglobulins across the board. - CLL may affect all immunoglobulins production but the normal WCC argues against this. - HIV would be expected to increase IgG early in its course and reduced IgA, IgG and IgM as it advances. - Kartagener's syndrome (with situs inversus), is associated with chronic sinusitis, and bronchiectasis in the context of primary ciliary dyskinesia. Immunodeficiency is not a characteristic.

questions.stem:
A 69-year-old female has longstanding rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed 20 years ago with elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP. She has been treated with multiple disease modifying drugs over the years ­ including Methotrexate, Hydroxychloroquine, Leflunomide, and Sulphasalazine. She is currently treated with Etanercept by weekly subcutaneous injections. Her blood pressure is 135/85 and her fasting glucose is 7.4 mmol/L.

Bloods demonstrate:

Creatinine Bilirubin GGT ALP ALT AST

121 umol/L (45-90) 23 umol/L (2-20) 440 U/L (10-35) 110 U/L (30-150) 74 U/L (0-30) 230 U/L (10-50)

Albumin 28 g/L (32-48) Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Alcohol hepatitis is characterised by an AST:ALT ratio >2 which is rarely seen in any other cause of hepatitis. - DMARD's may induce hepatitis typically >2× the UL but the ratio is <2. Etanercept does not cause hepatotoxicity. - Amyloidosis is not suspected in the history although it can be associated with hepatic involvement. The most common finding is a raised ALP and increased bilirubin is unlikely. - Steatohepatitis can lead to a similar picture as this however the AST:ALT ratio is <2. - Hepatitis B is part of pre-treatment screening prior to initiating biologic therapies and hence would have been picked up earlier. It would not affect the AST:ALT ratio in this way.

questions.stem: An 83-year-old man with cognitive impairment is about to have a gastroscopy following an episode of melena. What is the most important aspect of assessing competence to consent for the procedure?

options.text: Ability to state the risks and benefits of the procedure to you
Understanding the reason for having the procedure and acknowledging the risks and benefits
Recalling the events leading up to the procedure
Ability to read the consent form
Ability to name next-of-kin to assist with consent

questions.explanation: Capacity to make a decision is fundamental to the protection of Autonomy. Law and ethics have settled on four decision-making abilities that constitute capacity: 1. Understanding 2. Expressing a choice 3. Appreciation 4. Reasoning

questions.stem:
A 64-year-old female has routine blood tests as part of an annual health check. She is well and takes no regular medications.

Na+ K+ Urea Creatinine Albumin Adj Calcium Phosphate

141 mmol/L 4.6 mmol/L 5.4 mmol/L 81 umol/L 39 g/L 2.7 mmol/L 0.9 mmol/L

(135-145) (3.5 - 5.2) (3.2-7.7) (45-90) (32-48) (2.2 -2.6) (0.8-1.5)

Which of the following most likely explains the above tests?
questions.explanation: Primary hyperparathyroidism is by far the most likely cause of mild hypercalcaemia on routine screening. Increased production of PTH by an adenomatous parathyroid drives calcium up via increased bone resorption, intestinal absorption, and decreased renal excretion. Phosphate is typically low or low normal. - Her calcium is elevated which by definition is not normal. - Vitamin D deficiency would cause hypocalcaemia. - Hypercalcaemia is not diagnostic of sarcoidosis. Although it may be associated with hypercalcaemia, in the absence of other symptoms it is unlikely. - Secondary hyperparathyroidism is characterised by hypocalcemia.

questions.stem:
A 54 year old male with no background medical history presents with six months of progressive exertional dyspnea, myalgia without weakness, arthralgia, and thickened, cracked, fissured skin on his hands. Serology demonstrates:

CK

3400

ANA

1:320

ANCA

negative

Anti-dsDNA negative

Anti-Scl 70 negative

Anti-Jo 1 positive

Anti-RNP negative

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Anti-synthetase syndrome is charactersied by skin changes ("mechanics hands"), myalgia and sometimes myositis, Pulmonary interstitial fibrosis, and the presence of Anti-Jo-1 antibodies. - Diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis is associated with sclerodactyly not mechanics hands. Pulmonary fibrosis can be associated as well as a low grade myositis, but the antibody associated with this condition is Anti-Scl-70. - Dermatomyositis shares many features but is associated with proximal muscle weakness and characteristic heliotrope rash and Gottron's papules. Interstitial Pulmonary fibrosis is not usually a feature. The antibody most associated is Anti-Mi-2 antibody. - Statin-induced necrotising myopathy is not associated with skin or lung involvement.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old male presents with a painful red eye and photophobia. Past history is significant for unexplained arthralgia and abdominal pain. He had diffuse conjunctival hyperaemia and Inflammatory cells are seen on slit lamp examination. Which of the following tests most likely to be useful?
questions.explanation:
The patient has acute anterior uveitis (AAU). His presentation suggests IBD with extra-intestinal manifestations. The HLA B27 gene is independently associated with AAU even in the absence of features of spondyloarthritis spectrum conditions such as: Ankylosing Spondylitis, Reactive RA, and IBD.
In children with Oligoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis AAU is often asymptomatic and is associated with ANA, but this is not the right age group. Scleritis is seen in patients with ANCA associated vasculitis not AAU.

questions.stem:
A 56-year-old male builder presents with increasing exertional dyspnoea. His BMI is 26. He is an exsmoker of longstanding and is otherwise well. Chest x-ray is remarkable for increased interstitial markings. He has fine inspiratory crepitations in both mid to low zones. Heart rate, blood pressure and saturations at rest were

normal. Pro-BNP was not elevated. Pulmonary function tests demonstrate:

FEV1 (L) FVC (L) TLC DLCO

1.52 1.14 3.04 (62% predicted) 9.84 (48% predicted)

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: He has a restrictive deficit and reduced diffusion capacity characteristic of a fibrosing restrictive lung disease. - Obstructive airway disease is characterised by fixed obstruction with a ratio of <70% and a reduced FEV1. There may be increased residual volumes from gas trapping. - Metabolic acidosis is not identified on PFT. - While pulmonary hypertension can lead to this picture of PFT, PFT are not diagnostic for PH. The crepitations are a sign suggesting fibrosis and the x-ray is in keeping with fibrosis. - While left ventricular failure could reproduce a restrictive pattern on spirometry, the normal pro-BNP argues strongly against the presence of significant heart failure.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old lady with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents with confusion, fever, and photophobia. All reflexes are brisk and following admission she has a seizure. A pustular rash is noted in the external auditory meatus on the left. Which of the following medications used to treat Rheumatoid arthritis is MOST likely to have predisposed her to this complication?
questions.explanation:
This patient has encephalitis with lesions of Herpes Zoster in the EAM as a likely cause. Shingles reactivation is more common following treatment with many immunosuppressants, especially steroids, but

severe systemic Zoster and encephalitis is very rare with most biologics and conventional DMARDs. Upadacitinib is a JAK inhibitor and like many drugs in this group is particularly associated with herpes reactivation. Prior vaccination is recommended.

questions.stem:
A 34-year-old female presents with a two-week history of anterior neck pain, anxious disposition, and tremor. She has a fine tremor and is tachycardic on examination.

Bloods demonstrate:

WCC 14.6 x10^9/L (4.0-11.0)

Neut 12 x 10^9/L (1.9-7.5)

CRP 85 mg/L

(<5)

TSH <0.01mIU/L (0.4-4.0)

fT4 47 pmol/L (10-24)

fT3 28 pmol/L (2.5-6.0)

Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
questions.explanation: This patient has subacute thyroiditis therefore supportive management with simple analgesics such as NSAID is all that is required as initial management. - Radioiodine is used in chronic forms of hyperthyroidism such as Grave's, Toxic multinodular goitre, and certain adenoma and carcinoma. - Carbimazole blocks hormonogenesis by inhibiting the action of thyroid peroxidase. As the hyperthyroid phase of thyroiditis is caused by breakdown of follicles releasing large volumes of colloid into the bloodstream and not hormonogenesis, it is not indicated. - She is thyrotoxic, Thyroxine is not indicated.

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old male on an opiate substitution programme presents with weakness, lethargy, low mood and weight gain. The following blood tests are performed:

Hb

140 g/L

(115-155)

Na+

140 mmol/L (135-145)

K+

4.8 mmol/L (3.5-5.2)

Urea

4.3 mmol/L (3.2-7.7)

Creatinine 76 umol/L (45-90)

TSH fT4 8am cortisol Testosterone Prolactin

4.6 mIU/L 8.2 pmol/L 644 nmol/L 4 nmol/ 360 mU/L

(0.4-4.0) (10-24)

What is the most likely explanation?
questions.explanation: Methadone commonly disrupts the HPG axis, specifically causing hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, leading to testosterone deficiency. In men this carries significant morbidity. - Adrenal insufficiency would be expected to have an abnormal morning cortisol. - Panhypopituitarism would lead to low TSH and free hormone. - A prolactinoma would result in a raised prolactin.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man with obesity, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia presents with an inflamed right big toe which is exquisitely tender on examination. He recalls no trauma to the area. He is afebrile. Current medications include felodipine, and atorvastatin. His WCC is 15, CRP is 84 and uric acid level is 0.60. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
questions.explanation:
A short acting potent NSAID such as Naproxen is the first line in managing attacks of gout. - Prednisone is best reserved for patients in whom NSAIDs are contraindicated (renal impairment), where a lower dose of Prednisone 35-40mg daily is recommended. - Antibiotics (Cefuroxime) are not indicated as there is low suspicion of infection in this presentation. - Allopurinol is reserved for recurrent attacks of gout and may be the appropriate next step in management,

although no history of previous attacks is given here. It should not be given alone in an acute attack, but could be started concurrently.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old female is evaluated for dysmenorrhea, hirsutism and acne. She has a BMI of 36 and an HbA1c of 44. Thyroid function tests, prolactin and beta-HCG are negative. What is the most useful medication to initiate in order to assist with her presenting symptoms?
questions.explanation: She presents with common symptoms of polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Combined oral contraceptive improves these symptoms by its oestrogenic effect. - Metformin is indicated in the treatment of insulin resistance associated with PCOS but it is not recommended as a first line in PCOS management. - Spironolactone is an antiandrogen and may benefit patients with regard to hirsutism but is second in line to the combined oral contraceptive. - SGLT2 inhibitors are not indicated. - Goserelin may be effective at managing the androgenic effects of PCOS but is reserved for resistant cases due to its complexity requiring add-back oestrogen therapy.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate in the management of suspected pulmonary embolism in a pregnant 26 year old at 20 weeks gestation with no haemodynamic instability?

options.text: Therapeutic LMWH for at least 12 weeks
Unfractionated heparin + warfarin until INR therapeutic
Organise CTPA and only treat if PE is confirmed

questions.explanation:
Therapeutic LMWH for at least 12 weeks is most appropriate. - UFH is not unreasonable but is inferior to LMWH. Warfarin is contraindicated in pregnancy, particularly in the first trimester due to teratogenicity and the last month due to risk of neonatal coagulopathy. - CTPA should be avoided if possible, and can potentially be avoided if the Pregnancy-Adapted YEARS

Algorithm is used. - Prophylactic dose of LMWH is not indicated as she needs treatment dose. - DOACs (Rivaroxaban) are not safe in pregnancy.
EMQs (Neurology) For each of the following questions, select the most likely answer from the following list
A. Vascular dementia B. Creutzfeld-Jakob disease C. Delirium D. Alzheimer's dementia E. Mood disorder F. Dementia with Lewy-body G. Frontotemporal dementia H. Encephalopathy I. Parkinson's disease-associated dementia

questions.stem: A 38 year old male is evaluated for infertility. He has been attempting to conceive a child for the past two years without success. He was referred to endocrinology after a semen analysis showed no sperm. He is otherwise healthy and does not take any medications. On physical examination his vital signs are normal. He is tall and has small testes, gynecomastia and minimal facial hair and auxiliary hair. Chromosomal analysis confirms 47, XXY. Which of the following statements is true?

options.text: Androgen supplementation is not clinically indicated
He is not at increased risk of breast tumours
Increased plasma concentrations of oestrogen is present
Most cases are diagnosed before puberty

questions.explanation: This patient has Klinefelter syndrome which is a chromosomal disorder (47,XXY) with the primary feature of gonadal failure. Because of gonadal failure testosterone is low and luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle stimulating hormone (FSH) are increased in an attempt to increase testosterone production in the sex hormone feedback loop. Increased oestrogen is often present because of chronic Leydig cell stimulation by LH and aromatisation of androstenedione by adipose tissue. The lower testosterone to oestrogen ratio results in mild feminisation with gynecomastia. Features of low testosterone are small testes and eunuchoid proportions with long legs and incomplete virilisation. Severe cases can be diagnoses prior to puberty ­ however 75% of cases are not diagnosed until adulthood and the frequency in the general population is 1 in 1000. Patients with Klinefelter's are at increased risk of breast tumours, thromboembolic diseases, learning di>iculties, obesity, diabetes mellitus and varicose veins.

questions.stem:
You are the after hours registrar who receives a call from the laboratory about a 64 year old females abnormal blood results. Her triglyceride levels are 62 mmol/L.

You telephone the patient and she describes severe epigastric pain and you recommend she presents to the emergency department. She is diagnosed with acute pancreatitis. In addition to supportive care how else should this patient be managed?

options.text: Dietician involvement for low fat and calorie diet

questions.explanation: High fasting triglycerides are associated with poorly controlled diabetes, high alcohol intake or secondary causes such as thyroid disorders, liver disease, ketogenic or restrictive diets. It can also be associated with medications such as glucocorticoids, thiazide diuretics, isotretinoin, non-selective beta blockers and oral oestrogens. If triglycerides are higher than 50 mmol/L acute medical assessment for an insulin infusion or plasmapheresis is indicated to prevent acute pancreatitis. If the patient has pancreatitis this is the treatment of choice. A statin is less e>ective on hypertriglyceridemia especially if levels are >5 mmol/L. They will often be combined with a fibrate at a high dose. Glucocorticoids would likely cause a further elevation in TGL and would not be the treatment of choice. While lifestyle advice is important and should be addressed on discharge and while an inpatient it would not treat the acute pancreatitis.

questions.stem: A 63 year old man presents to his general practitioner for a routine review. His past medical history include gout, obesity and he is a current smoker with a 40 pack year history. His only medication is allopurinol and he has no drug allergies. His examination is normal and his blood pressure is 120/80. A routine ECG shows new atrial fibrillation. What would be the most appropriate management given this finding based on the CHADsVASc score?
questions.explanation: The need for anticoagulation can be calculated using the CHA2DS2-VASc score; females with a score of 2 and males with a score of 1 are likely to benefit from an anticoagulant to reduce their risk of stroke. Aspirin should not be used to prevent stroke in atrial fibrillation as it is minimally e>ective, not safe or cost e>ective. This patients CHADsVASc score is 0 which would be low risk and can be managed with observation and lifestyle management alone.

questions.stem:
A 46 year old male ultra endurance athlete presents to the emergency department following palpitations and a presyncopal episode while running. On his fitness watch he noted his heart rate to be 250 beats per minute and irregular. His symptoms lasted five minutes and resolved. He has no cardiovascular risk factors and takes no regular medications or supplements. He has no family history of cardiovascular disease. His initial ECG is below. His examination, troponin, laboratory studies and observations are within normal limits.

He was admitted for telemetry and given a beta blocker. His ECHO was normal. When walking his telemetry showed the below rhythm. The patient described lightheadedness and palpitations during the event which self terminated.
What is the best next investigation?
questions.explanation: This is a fit and well endurance athlete who presents with palpitations and was found to have a narrow complex irregular rhythm. Given his investigations and ECHO are normal the next best test would be an electrophysiology study to ensure he does not have an concealed accessory pathway leading to pre-excited atrial fibrillation that was unmasked by the beta blocker. No further investigation is incorrect because this patient is symptomatic and has an abnormal rhythm. A stress ECHO is incorrect because this test would be looking for regional wall motion abnormalities which is not the suspicion with normal troponins and a nonischemic ECG. A cardiac MRI would be useful if the suspicion was a collapse and a wide complex rhythm consistent with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

questions.stem:
A 65 year old patient presents to the emergency department with chest pain and was found to have raised troponin and an ECG with ischemic changes.
A diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction was made and the patient was taken for early percutaneous angiography. They were found to have obstructive coronary artery disease in one vessel of >50% and a fractional flow reserve of <0.80. Their echocardiogram showed a preserved ejection fraction of 50% following revascularisation.
The patient was discharged on a high intensity statin, aspirin, a P2Y12 receptor blocker and an angiotensin-converting- enzyme inhibitor.
What will be the likely outcome of NOT prescribing a long term beta blocker on discharge?

options.text: Decreased risk of hospitalisation for heart failure
No increased risk of death or new myocardial infarction
Increased risk of new myocardial infarction

questions.explanation:
A new study from the New England journal of medicine randomised patients with preserved ejection fraction and early percutaneous intervention into beta blocker vs non beta blocker treatment. Older trials which showed good e>icacy of beta blockers predated early percutaneous intervention, biomarker based diagnosis, antithrombotic agents, high intensity statins and RAAS antagonists and were often done on patients

with large myocardial infarctions with reduced EF. This updated study found long-term beta-blocker treatment did not lead to a lower risk of the composite primary end point of death from any cause or new myocardial infarction than no beta-blocker use. https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2401479

questions.stem:
A 43 year old female presents with progressive shortness of breath on minor exertion and light-headedness over the last few months. She has presented to the emergency department following a syncopal episode at work. She is normally well with no significant past medical history or regular medications.
Her ECG shows right axis deviation. You organise an echocardiogram which shows an elevated peak tricuspid regurgitation velocity of 3.6. There are no signs of pulmonary valve stenosis. She proceeds to right heart catheterisation which shows a mPAP 32mmHg, PAWP of 10 mmHg and a positive response to inhaled nitric oxide. What medication should this patient be started on?
questions.explanation:
This patient presents with group one pulmonary hypertension which is related to an underlying pulmonary vasculopathy. This is more prevalent in young women and presents with shortness of breath on minimal exertion eventually progressing to right heart failure. Suspicion should be raised with hypoxia, an ECG showing right axis deviation, RVH or p pulmonale. The next step is an ECHO that will demonstrate high right sided pressure (high RVSP, RV dilation, RA dilation, raised peak tricuspid

regurgitation volume. The gold standard test is a right heart catheterisation. In Group One PAH the diagnostic criteria is a mean PA pressure greater than 20 mm Hg, PVR greater than or equal to 3.0 Wood units, PCWP less than or equal to 15 mm Hg. If there is a response to nitric oxide the initial treatment is with calcium channel blockers. If there is no response then treatment tailored to severity is started. This is most often combination therapy with an endothelin receptor antagonist and phosphodiesterase inhibitor.

questions.stem: Which subtype of mesothelioma has the best prognosis?
questions.explanation: The most common and best prognosis of mesothelioma is epithelioid. The worst prognosis is associated with sarcomatoid. Malignant mesothelioma is an aggressive tumour arising from the serosal outer linings of the lungs, heart, abdomen and testes. The most important risk factor is asbestos. Five year survival continues to be 5-10%. Treatment is guided by staging, histology and ECOG. Systemic therapy for non epithelioid includes nivolumab plus ipilimumab and for epithelioid is pemetrexed plus cisplatin with or without bevacizumab.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements regarding long term complications with advanced testicular germ cell tumours is true?

options.text: Cisplatin chemotherapy does not contribute to late toxicity
There is an increased risk of developing acute lymphoid leukaemia with etoposide
There is an increased risk of cardiovascular disease as a result of chemotherapy

questions.explanation: Most patients with testicular germ cell tumours will have good long term survival. Long term survivors are at increased risk of cardiovascular disease and secondary malignancies. Patients treated with cisplatin chemotherapy have increased risk of hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, metabolic syndrome and cardiovascular events. Patients treated with high cumulative doses of etoposide- such as those who relapse and require salvage treatment- have a 1-2% risk of developing acute myeloid leukaemia usually 2-3 years after completion of treatment. Patients treated with radiation therapy, cisplatin based chemotherapy or both have an increased risk of secondary solid malignancies.

questions.stem: An elderly patient has recently been diagnosed with mild cognitive impairment. Which of the following predicts the progression to Alzheimer's disease?
questions.explanation: A measurable cognitive problem that does not seriously disrupt daily activities is often referred to as mild cognitive impairment. Factors that predict progression to Alzheimers disease include a prominent memory deficit, family history of dementia, presence of apolipoprotein 4 allele, small hippocampal volumes, low cerebrospinal A, and elevated tau or brain amyloid on PET scan. Alpha synuclein inclusions are found in Lewy body dementia. SPECT and PET scanning show a temporal- parietal hypoperfusion in AD dementia and frontotemporal deficits in frontotemporal dementia.

questions.stem: A 28 year old female presents to her GP with a two week history of spontaneous bruising, fatigue, shortness of breath and presyncope. Her complete blood count shows a haemoglobin of 76 and a platelet count of 25. A blood film demonstrates circulating myeloblasts with Auer rods. A bone marrow biopsy shows 35% blasts with Auer rods. The patient would like to have a family in the future. Which of the following is the most appropriate to preserve fertility?

options.text: Delay treatment to allow for egg retrieval
Commence a selective oestrogen receptor modulator
Administer a gonadotropin releasing hormone agonist

questions.explanation:
A GnRH agonist such as zoladex can help preserve fertility by temporarily suppressing ovarian function. While ovaries are less active they may be less susceptible to damage from chemotherapy which harms rapidly dividing cells. Delaying treatment to allow for fertility preservation is not an option in those who present with acute myeloid leukaemia. Egg harvesting will take at least two weeks and a delay in treatment may be fatal. Progesterone therapy can be a fertility sparing option for those with early stage endometrial cancer who still want to have a child rather than the standard treatment of a hysterectomy. Progesterone does not have a role in cancers treated with chemotherapy. A selective oestrogen receptor modulator is a hormone treatment that is

used to treat some types of breast cancer and osteoporosis. It can also be used to stimulate ovulation in fertility treatment- not for fertility preservation.

questions.stem: A post allogeneic stem cell transplant patient is admitted with VZV pneumonitis. Which is the most appropriate form of isolation?
questions.explanation: Hospitalized patients with varicella, disseminated zoster, and immunocompromised patients with dermatomal zoster should be placed on airborne (negative pressure room) and contact isolation precautions.

questions.stem: What is the best way of reducing the risk of transfusion associated Graft versus Host Disease?

options.text: Selection of CMV-antibody negative blood components
Testing for more antigens which may potentially be incompatible

questions.explanation: Transfusion-associated graft-versus-host-disease (TA-GvHD) is a rare but usually fatal complication of transfusion. The risk associated with an individual transfusion depends on the number and viability of contaminating lymphocytes, the susceptibility of the patient's immune system to donor lymphocyte engraftment (primarily related to the degree of T-cell immunosuppression) and the degree of immunological incompatibility between donor and patient. Cellular components, with the exception of thawed haematopoietic progenitor cells (HPC) intended for transplantation, must be irradiated to prevent TA-GvHD in at-risk patients. https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/transfusionmedicine/transfusion-medicine-handbook/4-blood-components/4-6-irradiation/

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT needed to calculate the 4T score for Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia?

options.text: Number of days post heparin exposure that thrombocytopenia took place
Looking into other causes of thrombocytopenia

questions.explanation: All the other options are part of the 4T score. The last "T" is Thrombosis (new thrombosis, skin necrosis).

questions.stem:
A 52 year old woman presents with fevers, malaise and a new painful, erythematous rash on her face, back and limbs following treatment with trimethoprim one week prior for a UTI. Laboratory results reveal elevated inflammatory markers and neutrophilia. A skin biopsy shows dense infiltration of neutrophils in the subcutaneous fat without evidence of vasculitis. An image of her rash is shown below:

What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The clinical presentation, recent antibiotic use and biopsy findings of neutrophilic infiltration without vasculitis are consistent with Sweet's syndrome. Erythema nodosum also involves tender nodules but is typically located on the lower legs and shows a di>erent histological pattern with septal panniculitis. Cellulitis usually presents with localized infection and lacks systemic neutrophilic dermatosis findings. Fixed drug eruptions present di>erently and generally show di>erent biopsy results.

questions.stem: A 68 year old woman with chronic lymphocytic leukaemia has been under a watch-andwait approach for the past year. During her follow-up, several clinical and laboratory findings are noted. Which of the following factors would be an indication to initiate treatment?

options.text: Absolute lymphocyte count of 60 x 10^9/L with no symptoms
Stable haemoglobin level of 120g/L and platelet count of 150 x 10^9/L
New onset of significant fatigue a>ecting daily activities
Lymphocyte doubling of more than 6 months, with no B symptoms

questions.explanation:
Indications for initiating treatment in CLL include:
 Autoimmune complications (e.g. AIHA, ITP not responsive to corticosteroids) LDT of <6 months OR increase >50% increase in ALC in 2 months Evidence of bone marrow failure (Hb <10g/dL, Plt <100 x 10^9) Disease related symptoms (Significant B symptoms such as significant fatigue
impacting daily life, night sweats, weight loss) Massive/progressive lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly

questions.stem: Which of the following groups of people who have hepatitis B are NOT in the target groups for Hepatocellular Carcinoma screening?
questions.explanation: Current guidelines would suggest screening for Asian men >40years, Asian women >50 years, African people >20 years, Aboriginal/Torres Strait Islander >50 years, anyone with cirrhosis, anyone with family hx of HCC. https://testingportal.ashm.org.au/hepatitis-b-related-hepatocellularcarcinoma/#:~:text=HCC%20surveillance%20by%206%2Dmonthly,to%20curative%20t herapy%20and%20prognosis.

questions.stem:
A 29 year old woman at 34 weeks' gestation presents with intense pruritus' especially of her palms and soles. She has no rash. Laboratory results show the following:
 Total bile acids: 24umol/L (elevated) AST: 110 U/L (mildly elevated) AST: 105 U/L (mildly elevated)

 Total bilirubin: 18 umol/L (mildly elevated) Haemoglobin: 128g/L (normal) Platelet count: 230,000 /uL (normal) Serum creatinine: 70 umol/L (normal) Blood glucose: 4.8mmol/L (normal) What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Pruritic urticarial papules and plaques of pregnancy (PUPPP)

questions.explanation: ICP is most likely given elevated bile acids, mild liver enzyme abnormalities and pruritus without rash. These findings are typical for ICP which is characterized by high bile acids and pruritus especially of the hands and feet. AFLP usually presents with more severe liver dysfunction, coagulopathy, elevated creatinine, hypoglycaemia and sometimes low platelets. It would often be associated with systemic symptoms which are not seen here. HELLP syndrome often includes elevated liver enzymes, thrombocytopenia and haemolysis. This patient's haemoglobin and platelet levels are within normal range making HELLP less likely. PURPP typically presents with an erythematous rash, especially of the abdomen which is not present in this case.

questions.stem: A 62 year old woman with metastatic lung cancer, currently treated with pembrolizumab, develops a widespread, pruritic erythematous rash covering approximately 40% of her body surface area two weeks after her last dose. The rash is worsening and she reports discomfort and di>iculty sleeping. What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Continue pembrolizumab and apply topical corticosteroids
Discontinue pembrolizumab and initiate high dose oral corticosteroids
Administer oral antihistamines and monitor closely
Treat with IV corticosteroids and re-challenge with pembrolizumab in one week

questions.explanation: For a severe immune-mediated rash involving >30% BSA, the initial management typically involves stopping pembrolizumab and starting high dose corticosteroids (e.g. prednisone 1mg/kg/day). Option A is insu>icient for a widespread rash. Option C is suitable for mild rashes but not for severe cases. Option D is inappropriate as rechallenging should only be considered after FULL resolution of the rash.

questions.stem: What Parkinson's plus syndrome is the MRI "hot cross bun sign" typically associated with?
questions.explanation: Hot cross bun sign in MSA is due to atrophy in the pons. PSP typically causes midbrain atrophy the "hummingbird sign"

questions.stem:
A 58 year old man with a history of cirrhosis secondary to EtOH use presents to ED with haematemesis and melena. After treatment for an upper gastrointestinal bleed, he receives several units of blood transfusion. Two days later, he develops worsening renal function with the following laboratory results:
 Serum creatinine 250umol/L (baseline 100umol/L) Serum bilirubin 50umol/L Serum albumin 30g/L INR 2.0 Urinalysis shows:
 Urine protein 30mg/dL (trace)

 Urine sodium 15mmol/L Urine osmolality 500mOmso/kg No casts present What is the most likely diagnosis for this patients acute kidney injury
questions.explanation: This patient's clinical presentation is most consistent with HRS, characterized by AKI in context of advanced liver disease. The significant rise in creatinine following an acute UGIB coupled with low urine sodium (<20mmol/L) and absence of urinary casts, strongly suggests HRS where renal impairment is primarily functional rather than due to intrinsic damage. Option A may be considered given volume depletion from UGIB however the rapid increase in creatinine following resuscitation supports HRS. Option B is less likely given the lack of casts typically seen in ATN and option C would require evidence of an allergic reaction or recent drug exposure neither of which are indicated in this scenario.

questions.stem: A 45 year old woman with a history of systemic sclerosis presents with sudden onset of severe hypertension, headache, oliguria, and rising creatinine. She recently began taking ibuprofen for joint pain. On examination, blood pressure is 210/120mmHg and there are signs of volume overload. Laboratory tests reveal a rapidly declining renal function consistent with scleroderma renal crisis. Which of the following is the most appropriate medication to initiate as part of her treatment plan?
questions.explanation: Scleroderma renal crisis is a life-threatening complication of systemic sclerosis often triggered by factors like NSAIDs. ACE inhibitors are the mainstay of treatment as they e>ectively reduce blood pressure and slow the progression of renal failure by dilating the a>erent arterioles and reducing glomerular pressure. Early use of ACEi is crucial in managing scleroderma renal crisis as it improves both blood pressure control and renal outcomes.

questions.stem: A 55 year old woman is diagnosed with rheumatoid arthritis. You are assessing her for prognostic indicators that may suggest a more favourable disease course. Which of the following factors is associated with a better prognosis in RA?

options.text: High rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-CCP titers

questions.explanation: Older age at onset of RA is generally associated with a milder disease course, as it often presents with fewer erosive changes and a slower rate of progression. In contrast, high RF/anti-CCP titres are typically linked to a more severe disease course, female sex is often associated with higher disease activity and worse outcomes in RA, early erosive changes on imaging correlate with aggressive disease and increased joint damage. Question 63 William, a 78 year old man in the hospital, is found to have a pressure injury. On examination, there appears to be full thickness skin loss with adipose tissue visible at the base of the wound. What stage of pressure injury is present? A. Stage 1 B. Stage 2 C. Stage 3 D. Stage 4 Answer = C

questions.stem:
Ben, a 30 year old man with a known history of systemic lupus erythematosus, presents with shortness of breath and chest pain. On examination, he has a new heart murmur.
