questions.stem: A 28-year-old patient with depression wants to be commenced on medication. A routine ECG is performed prior to initiation of treatment which shows a QTc of 480ms. Which antidepressant is most likely to prolong QTc further?
questions.explanation:
Studies have shown that escitalopram and citalopram are most likely of the SSRIs to cause prolongation of QTc. Recent study showed that QTc prolongation was negligible when using agents such as paroxetine, sertraline, and fluoxetine.
A Comparison of the Risk of QT Prolongation Among SSRIs - Kylee A. Funk, Jolene R. Bostwick, 2013 (sagepub.com)

questions.stem:
A 29-year-old female presents for assessment of painful, red patches over bilateral anterior lower legs, fevers and sore and swollen joints of the hands, knees and shoulder. Her ankles appear as below. Further workup including a CXR is performed as shown.


Which of the following is the next most appropriate step in management?

options.text: Endobronchial ultrasound guided needle aspirate of an enlarged hilar lymph node

questions.explanation: The diagnosis is Lofgren's syndrome, observed in <5-10% of patients with sarcoidosis. This is the combination of erythema nodosum, hilar adenopathy, migratory polyarthrlagia and fever seen primarily in women. The presence of all features has a 95% diagnostic specificity for sarcoidosis allowing a clinical diagnosis to be made without biopsy. Diagnosis is typically made on the presence of the classic triad of symptoms and is supported by imaging. Treatment with NSAIDs is usually adequate to control symptoms, but occasional patients will need additional therapy such as corticosteroids, immunomodulatory agents, colchicine.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a characteristic radiological finding of the spondyloarthropathies?
questions.explanation: Romanus lesion ­ characteristic erosions of the anterior and posterior vertebral endplates. Healing of the erosion results in sclerotic increase in density resulting in the shiny corner sign. Most easily diagnosed on MRI.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old gives a history of recurrent morning myoclonus and absences after sleep deprivation. The most likely epilepsy syndrome is:
questions.explanation: JME ­ reasonably common form of generalised epilepsy. If short on sleep may have brief myoclonus in the morning in the hands, as well as brief absence seizures. BRE typically characterised by focal sensory or motor symptoms on one side of the face, starts in childhood and typically seizures remit by adulthood/mid-adolescence. BME usually starts in infancy, not as typical to see absence seizures.

questions.stem:
A 49-year-old woman with metastatic melanoma commences on immunotherapy with ipilimumab. She has baseline symptoms of dry coughing and right pleural chest pain. At her first restaging scans after completing four doses of ipilimumab three weekly, some of her lung metastatses are reported as being larger in size than compared to baseline by 20%. Her symptoms are currently stable with satisfactory biochemistry and haematology.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Transient worsening of disease can be seen with ipilimumab before tumour regression. Given stable symptoms, she should be closely monitored until the next scan
Transient worsening of disease can be seen with immunotherapy before tumour regression. Given stable symptoms, she should proceed with further doses of ipilimumab
Worsening of disease on radiology is consistent with disease progression. She should be considered for nivolumab as the second-line therapy
Worsening of disease on radiology is consistent with disease progression. She should be considered for chemotherapy

questions.explanation: Pseudoprogression is a phenomenon where immune checkpoint inhibitors cause an initial, temporary increase in tumor size or new lesions before the tumor eventually shrinks and responds to the therapy. This is caused by an influx of immune cells into the tumor site, leading to inflammation and swelling that can mimic true disease progression on scans. Differentiating pseudoprogression from true progression is a challenge, leading to the development of specific criteria like the immune-related response criteria (irRC) and iRECIST, which require a follow-up scan to confirm progression.

questions.stem: A 22 year old female university student with moderately severe Crohn disease including perianal involvement presents with a further flare despite current treatment with azathioprine in conjunction with a reducing dose of steroid, which of the following is the treatment of choice?

options.text: Continue with current dose of azathioprine, and increased dose of steroid

questions.explanation: The need to increase therapy is clear here, with a further flare despite current treatment with a thiopurine and steroids. Perianal involvement signifies a high-risk patient, as does a diagnosis before the age of thirty years. Other factors resulting in high risk are: raised CRP/faecal calprotectin levels; deep ulcers on colonoscopy; tobacco use; extra-intestinal manifestations, such as arthritis, uveitis and psoriasis; and long segments of small and/or large bowel involvement.

questions.stem: Which one of the following features of encephalopathy in thiamine deficiency reverses most quickly with thiamine replacement therapy?
questions.explanation: The feature of encephalopathy in thiamine deficiency that reverses most quickly with treatment is ocular abnormalities, specifically the nystagmus and ophthalmopelgia.

questions.stem: Which herniation causes an ipsilateral dilated pupil?
questions.explanation:
Ipsilateral dilated pupil is associated with Uncal herniation. This is due to compression of CN III which can result in the combination of CN III palsy + dilated pupil + papilledema. This is often an earlier sign prior to increased lethargy coma and death. Causes include expanding mass lesions, trauma ­ resulting in bleeds or obstructive hydrocephalus.
Transcalvarial results from either an open skull fracture or procedurally ­ no specific symptoms associated with this.
Subfalcine is the most common form form of intracranial herniation where the frontal lobe herniates under the falx cerebri. This occurs in setting of midline shift in setting of bleeds/mass lesion.
Tonsillar herniation or "coning" is where the is herniation of the cerebellum into the formen magnum. This is associated with Cushing's triad and high mortality without intervention. Would expect low level consciousness, blood pressure instability and bradycardia.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old lady is travelling to Angola as part of a missionary group. She has a diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica and is on prednisone 10 mg daily. She had a yellow fever vaccination 15 years ago and still has the vaccination certificate. Which of the statements is correct with regards to further yellow fever vaccination for this patient:

options.text: Yellow fever vaccination is required as she is travelling to a high risk country and it is greater than 10 years since her last vaccination
Yellow fever vaccination is contraindicated due to concurrent prednisone use
Yellow fever vaccination as a single dose is protective
Yellow fever vaccination is not required as it has been eradicated from Africa

questions.explanation: One dose enough for lifetime Prednisolone dose not high enough to be contraindicated

questions.stem: A 21 yo male presents to outpatient clinic with asthenia, myalgias and general muscle weakness. He had been noted by his GP to have hypokalaemia (2.5mmol/L) which he has been supplemented for. He was admitted for further investigation and was found despite supplementation to have a potassium of 2.9 mmmol/L. Na and CL were within normal limits. Magnesium was 0.58 mmol/L. He was also noted to have a metabolic alkalosis with a pH of 7.48, pCO2 40 and HCO3 of 32. A Renin: aldosterone level is performed which is noted to be elevated. He denies using any medications or illicit drug use. What is the likely cause of his presentation?
questions.explanation: This presentation is classically in keeping with Gitelman syndrome which presents similar to a patient on thiazide therapy. Bartters present with similar physiology however presents in early childhood where Gitelmans presents in late adolescence to early adulthood. Liddles can be differentiated due to the raised Renin:aldosterone level as this would expected to be reduced in this disorder. Frusemide abuse could present with a similar metabolic picture however no concerns for medication abuse.

questions.stem: A left adrenal lesion measuring 10 x 12mm is incidentally detected in a 62-year-old woman on CT abdomen. The lesion is homogenous, has clear demarcations and has Hounsfield units of 12. The patients has a history of longstanding hypertension, well controlled on ramipril 10 mg and amlodipine 5 mg. She is obese with a BMI of 32 and appears to have mild facial plethora and an interscapular fat pad, but does not have proximal myopathy or striae. You decide to perform a series of investigations to further evaluate the adrenal mass. The following investigations are all likely to aid in diagnosis EXCEPT for:

options.text: High dose (8 mg) dexamethasone suppression test

questions.explanation: Normal early morning cortisol does not exclude Cushing's -- all the other tests are recognised screening tests.

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old accountant experiences breathlessness after 20 minutes of semi-competitive football. During a CPET, the following are noted after 5 minutes of exercise in association with severe breathlessness.
HR 145 RR 38 SpO2 88% RA


Workload 50% predicted maximal Which of the following is true?

options.text: A subclinical myopathy may explain the exercise limitation
This level of desaturation is not uncommon during exercise after age 35
He has achieved maximum expected heart rate excluding any cardiac limitation to exercise
A disorder of V/Q matching is probable

questions.explanation: Causes of hypoxaemia: hypoventilation, V/Q mismatch, right to left shunt, diffusion limitation V/Q mismatch ­ characterised by increased A-a gradient, common causes include obstructive airway disease, pulmonary vascular diseases, ILDs

questions.stem: Age-related changes in pharmacokinetics can lead to increased risk of adverse drug reactions. The oral bioavailability of a drug is the fraction of the drug reaching the systemic circulation after oral ingestion. Which age-related change has the greatest impact on the increase in oral bioavailability in older people?
questions.explanation:
PK and ageing
- Absorption: nil significant change - Distribution: decreased 1st pass metabolism, decreased lean body mass with increase in fat
stores therefore lipid soluble drugs such as diazepam have increased apparent Vd with prolonged T1/2, whilst water soluble drugs such as digoxin have reduced Vd and higher concentration. Decreased protein binding due to decreased (albumin) -- increases potential for free drug ­ especially important for drugs with a narrow therapeutic range - Clearance: liver 10% reduction in hepatic blod flow/unit volume, minor phase 1 decrease in metabolism; renal ­ reduced renal excretion due to decreased GFR & decreased tubular secretion - Gastric acid production increases with ageing which correlated with the age-related increase in ulcer incidence in Western countries (even when accounting for the higher incidence of atrophic gastritis & achlorhydria in the elderly) - Decreased intestinal motility with ageing ­ not going to affect PD

questions.stem: A 58-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset palpitations and is found to be in atrial fibrillation. Decision is made to attempt pharmacological cardioversion. The patient is prescribed flecainide and metoprolol. What is the purpose of metoprolol in this case?

options.text: Synergistically increase change of revision to sinus rhythm.
Prevent 1:1 conduction if atrial flutter occurs.
Reduce risk of flecainide inducted ventricular arrhythmias.

questions.explanation: A known risk of flecainide is that he can cause AF to go into 1:1 atrial flutter. This is more likely in the setting of higher doses that are used in setting of pharmacological cardioversions. This can be prevented by using a beta blocker concurrently.

questions.stem: What is the most likely explanation for persisting malabsorptive symptoms and villous atrophy in a patient with proven coeliac disease?
questions.explanation:
The above scenario is most in keeping with ongoing ingestion of gluten. In some instances, this can be in the form of cross contamination of products and the patient may not be aware. Collagenous atrophy can present similarly and also results in villous atrophy however serological testing for Coeliacs disease will be negative and there will be no response to gluten free diet. There will also be notable collagen deposition on biopsy.
Pancreatic malabsorption would be typically seen in patient with chronic pancreatic insufficiency in setting of pancreatitis. This would not show villous atrophy and testing with faecal elastase would assist in establishing this diagnosis.
Intestinal Crohn's disease again would present with other macroscopic findings namely crypts and ulceration.

questions.stem: Stem cell mobilisation for the purpose of autologous stem cell transplant:

options.text: Requires the administration of myelosuppressive chemotherapy

questions.explanation: Stem cell mobilisation does not always require myelosuppressive chemotherapy, as it can be achieved with growth factors like G-CSF alone. However, combining chemotherapy with growth factors (chemomobilisation) can result in higher stem cell yields and may be used to reduce tumour burden, although it carries more side effects. In cases where mobilisation is poor with growth factors alone or with a combination of CTX and growth factors, other agents like plerixafor may be used to improve the mobilisation of stem cells.

questions.stem: In interstitial lung disease, which of the following is true?

options.text: Bronchoalveolar lavage demonstrates a lymphocytosis in hypersensitivity pneumonitis
The restrictive defect associated with interstitial lung disease can be diagnosed on spirometry
Smoking is a risk factor for hypersensitivity pneumonitis

Pirfenidone has been shown to be as effective in slowing the progress of lung function decline in
patients with non-IPF fibrotic lung diseases when compared with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis

questions.explanation: BAL often shows lymphocytosis in hypersensitivity pneumonitis with a lymphocyte percentage over 30% considered supportive of the diagnosis. This combined with typical findings from a high-resolution CT scan and identified exposure to an inciting antigen can increase diagnostic confidence. Spirometry can suggest a restriction associated with ILD by showing a disproportionate reduction in both FEV1 and FVC but cannot definitively diagnose ILD because it does not measure total lung capacity, which is the gold standard for confirming a restrictive ventilatory defect. HP is an inflammatory condition triggered by an allergic reaction to inhaled organic dusts, not smoking; smoking appears to be protective against developing the disease but can be a risk factor for developing fibrosis in those who already have HP. While studies show promise, the level of evidence for pirfenidone's efficacy in some non-IPF fibrotic diseases is not as strong as for IPF and further research is needed.

questions.stem: Regarding the treatment of PD peritonitis, which of the following is true?

options.text: Empirical therapy does not require gram negative cover
Any episode of PD peritonitis should prompt a review of the patient's technique
Antifungal agents are only required if fungal infection is confirmed
Polymicrobial infection is usually related to skin contamination

questions.explanation: PD peritonitis commonly results from poor or incorrect handling of the PD catheter by the patient. Empirical therapy for PD peritonitis should include gram pos and gram neg cover, as although offending organisms are usually skin commensals, gut-derived organisms are not uncommon. Polymicrobial infection indicates GIT pathology and should prompt investigation for a bowel source of infection. The strongest risk factor for fungal PD peritonitis is recent antibiotic use, so antifungal prophylaxis should be given whenever antibiotics are administered.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with a 60-pack year history of smoking presents to hospital with signs of SVC syndrome. She had a large mass in her central chest with an enlarged subclavian node's. She is Pemberton's positive and has distended anterior chest veins. She is to undergo urgent biopsy. Which histology would make you choose urgent up-front chemotherapy to treat her SVC obstruction?
questions.explanation:
Small cell lung cancer although aggressive can be highly responsive to high dose chemotherapy. Therefore, out of the answers above this would be the most correct. Typical first line therapy is a combination of Carboplatin, Etoposide and Atezolizumab. Lymphoma would be another lesion in the chest that you would consider urgent chemotherapy.
For slower growing tumours steroids may provide some benefit along with radiotherapy. Very rarely stenting of the blocked SVC can be considered however this is considered in certain situations.

questions.stem: You have a CKD patient on EPO treatment with Hb 110 g/L, ferritin 140 micrograms/L and transferrin saturation 15%. Regarding their management, you should:
questions.explanation: Relative/functional iron deficiency (TSAT <25%) Hb is at target (110-120) so no need to change EPO dose ­ note that Hb should be monitored as an increase is likely once iron replete.

questions.stem:
A 57-year-old woman with acromegaly still has elevations of GH and IGF-1 after transsphenoidal surgery. As a complication of her acromegaly she has difficult to control diabetes.
Which of the following adjunctive therapies is most likely to worsen her diabetes?
questions.explanation:
Pasireotide causes new onset diabetes in 60% by binding to SST5 somatostatin receptor on pancreas beta cells, can also exacerbate pre-existing diabetes. If diabetes from acromegaly or other, avoid pasireotide.
EXTENDED MATCHING QUESTIONS (Q46-47)
Match the following genetic cancer syndromes from the list of options:
A. MEN1 B. RET C. VHL D. SDHA E. SDHB F. HPNCC G. PTEN H. NF1

questions.stem:
A 55 year old woman with a history of metastatic breast cancer who has recently completed her first round of chemotherapy presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath. Her observations include BP 100/60 HR 130Bpm with an ECG showing sinus tachycardia. Her chest x-ray is shown below.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
The CXR clearly shows a large globular heart consistent with a pericardial effusion. The clinical


presentation is also suggestive of this. Namely the relative hypotension with tachycardia (always be weary of the patient with hypotension and tachycardia and consider tamponade/effusion as possible differential). Her background of underlying malignancy increased this risk and this is likely malignant in origin.
Other differentials would include; 1. Idiopathic 2. Infections (viral/bacterial)

questions.stem:
A 42-year-old music teacher undergoes breast conserving surgery and radiotherapy for a node negative, oestrogen receptor positive, Her-2 negative breast cancer. She comes back after 6 weeks of treatment with adjuvant tamoxifen. She complains of persistent hot flushes, which are very debilitating. She is keen to persevere with tamoxifen, however would like to try something to help with vasomotor symptoms.
Which of the following is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.text: Hormone replacement therapy with low dose oestradiol

questions.explanation: The safest treatments for vasomotor menopausal symptoms with a history of breast cancer include nonhormonal medications like SSRIs. Both venlafaxine and paroxetine can be effective for vasomotor menopausal symptoms, but studies suggest paroxetine may offer a greater overall reduction in hot flashes, while venlafaxine may provide faster symptom relief but with more side effects. Hormone therapy generally not recommended due to the risk of stimulating any remaining cancer cells, especially in oestrogen-receptor-positive breast cancers.

questions.stem: What regards to the use of trikafta (elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor) which of the following is true?

options.text: Only benefits patients with the F508del mutation
Increases the mean sweat chloride concentration

questions.explanation:
The triple drug combination of elexacaftor-tezacaftor-ivacaftor (ETI) is an important therapy for people with CF (PwCF) and at least one F508del mutation and for those with any other CFTR gene mutation that is responsive, based on in vitro and/or clinical trial data. Trikafta is taken orally and should be taken with fat-containing foods to improve absorption. Trikafta's three drugs work together to target defects in the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) protein. Elexacaftor and tezacaftor act as "correctors," improving the protein's processing and transport to the cell surface. Ivacaftor acts as a "potentiator," binding to the CFTR protein at the cell surface and opening the chloride channel to improve its function. The combined


effect is increased quantity and function of the CFTR protein, which helps regulate the flow of chloride ions and water in the body. Trikafta significantly decreases sweat chloride concentrations in people with cystic fibrosis (CF), as it helps correct the underlying defect in the CFTR protein. Clinical trials have shown that mean sweat chloride levels can decrease by approximately 41­58 mmol/L after 24 weeks of treatment, often falling to about half of the initial levels.


MEDICAL SCIENCES 35 QUESTIONS

questions.stem: A large-scale study compared the incidence of lung cancer between 2500 coal miners who worked above ground and 2500 coal miners who worked below ground who were matched in terms of their age and duration of occupation. Those who worked below ground were found to have an increased risk of cancer and was inferred as a causal factor. It was later discovered however that those miners who worked underground were also more likely to smoke. This phenomenon can be described as;
questions.explanation: A confounder is a variable that is associated with study exposure that can influence the study outcome. In this study it is unclear if the underground mining or increased smoking exposure or both have contributed to the outcome of the study. Selection bias appears less likely as there is two well matched groups. Measurement and misclassification bias also less likely as there is a clear exposure and documented outcome.

questions.stem: The diagram above represents an action potential at the sino-atrial node. The drug ivabradine has been found to benefit patients with chronic heart failure. Which of the following best describes its mode of action?

options.text: Action at the iCa(T) channel blocking flux of calcium leading to a reduction in the action potential slope
Action at the iCa(T) channel blocking efflux of calcium leading to an increase in the action potential slope
Action at the If channel blocking influx of sodium leading to a reduction in the action potential slope
Action at the If channel blocking efflux of sodium leading to an increase in the action potential slope

questions.explanation: The `funny' (pacemaker, I(f)) current is a mixed sodium/potassium inward current. Ivabradine inhibits the hyperpolarization-activated cyclic nucleotide-gated (HCN) channels, which are responsible for the If current in the SA node.

questions.stem: On polysomnography testing, which of the following is characteristic of REM sleep?
questions.explanation: REM sleep is defined by minimal skeletal muscle activity during which is shown as low amplitude levels on EMG during PSG. Myotonia is the not a characteristic of sleep disorders however poor sleep is a major problem for patients with myotonic dystrophy etc. Periodic limb movements can be seen during sleep however not during REM. If this is to degree that this wakes the patients, then it may be that the restless leg syndrome. Opsoclonus or "dancing eyes" is where a patients eyes make rapid and irregular movements. This is associated with toxidromes most commonly such as serotonin toxicity.

questions.stem: Which of the following coagulation tests is most sensitive to the presence of dabigatran?
questions.explanation: The thrombin time is the most sensitive routine coagulation assay to detect the presence of dabigatran. A normal TT excludes the presence of dabigatran, however low drug levels may significantly prolong the TT, therefore the assay cannot be used to estimate plasma levels. Some labs may perform a drug level assay (dilute thrombin clotting time assay).

questions.stem: What finding on EMG is most consistent with axonal injury?
questions.explanation: The most characteristic feature on EMG is that of decreased amplitude. A = a! You would expect reduced velocity in the setting of demyelinating disease. Both axonal and demyelinating can have prolonged F wave latency however this is more typical in demyelinating disease.

questions.stem: What is the cause of ACE Inhibitor induced angioedema?
questions.explanation: ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema is a rare but potentially fatal adverse reaction to ACE inhibitor medications, causing sudden swelling of the face, lips, or tongue due to the accumulation of bradykinin. It is not a histamine-mediated allergic reaction and can occur at any time, even years after starting treatment.

questions.stem: Myasthenia Gravis is characterised by impaired neuromuscular transmission. What change is seen in the post-synaptic miniature end-plate potentials in a patient with Myasthenia Gravis?
questions.explanation: Reduced numbers of ACh receptors

questions.stem: In a patient taking warfarin, co-administration of which of the following drugs is most likely to lead to a reduction in INR?
questions.explanation:
Rifampicin is an aromatic hydroxylated cytochrome P-450 isoenzyme drug that reduces the half-life of warfarin and accelerates its clearance rate (clearance rate of R-warfarin triples while that of S-warfarin


doubles), thereby reducing the anticoagulant effect of warfarin and leading to increased risk of embolism

questions.stem: Which of the following diseases is least likely to be associated with recurrence of renal disease following renal transplantation?
questions.explanation: Alport syndrome is a genetic disorder that typically does not recur in a transplanted kidney. Diseases that do commonly recur after a transplant include IgA nephropathy, FSGS, MPGN.

questions.stem: A 25 year-old female presents with intellectual disability and epilepsy. You organise genetic testing with a chromosome microarray. Which of these will be detected by the chromosome microarray?

options.text: Splice-site variant in the PTEN gene
Nonsense variant in the ARID1B gene

questions.explanation: Chromosome microarrays detect a gain (microduplication) or loss (microdeletion) in genetic information within a chromosome. Microarrays do not sequence the DNA code of the gene and hence cannot pick up single nucleotide changes eg. missense variant/nonsense variant/frameshift variant etc. Microarrays also cannot detect triplet repeat expansion disorders like Fragile X syndrome, Huntington's disease, myotonic dystrophy etc.

questions.stem: What cell is primarily affected in leucocyte adhesion deficiency?
questions.explanation: The primary cell affected is the neutrophil. Patient will typically present with an elevated WBC in the absence of infection. Leucocyte adhesion deficiency is a rare inherited immunodeficiency which results in immune cells not being able to reach sites of tissue injury or inflammation. There are a number of subtypes but all effect different steps of immune phagocytes attachment to the endothelium allowing for migration into the tissues.

questions.stem: The following statement regarding confidence intervals is false:

options.text: The intervals are larger with smaller sample size
They indicate the presence or otherwise of a statistical difference between two group
A 95% confidence interval means that 95% of all observed values fall within that interval

In an odds ratio, if the 95% confidence interval includes unity then no significant difference may
apply

questions.explanation: A 95% confidence interval does not mean that 95% of all observed values fall within that specific interval. A confidence interval is a range of values used to estimate an unknown population parameter (like the mean or proportion). For any single, specific interval that has been constructed, you can be 95% confident that it contains the true population parameter. The observed data points (the values in your sample) are used to calculate the interval itself. The interval is about estimating the true underlying population value, not about the spread of individual data points within your sample.

questions.stem: Which of the following can cause a frameshift mutation?

options.text: Substitution of one nucleotide for another
Insertion of two nucleotides into the DNA sequence
A point mutation that does not change the amino acid.
Substitution of one nucleotide resulting in a stop codon.

questions.explanation: A frameshift mutation occurs when 1 or more codons are inserted or deleted from the genetic code resulting in altered reading frame. Disorders associated with these include CF and Tay-Sachs. The other answers are not correct as these do not alter the reading frame. A. Describes either possibly a missense mutation where a different amino acid is produced due to the alteration or possible a silent mutation if the same amino acid is produced. C. Describes a silent mutation where despite change there is no change to protein that is coded. D. This describes a nonsense mutation whereby alteration results in a stop codon resulting premature termination of the protein.

questions.stem: Which of the following is MOST closely associated with the pathology of the Lewy body in Parkinson's disease?
questions.explanation: Alpha-synuclein is a highly soluble unfolded protein that accumulates in Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites in Parkinson disease and other synucleinopathies. Mutations in the gene encoding alpha-synuclein (SNCA) are linked to familial Parkinson disease. Like other amyloids, alpha-synuclein acquires a cross-Bsheet structure in the seeded nucleation process. Tau and amyloid are associated with Alzheimers. Presenilin associated with familial AD. Mutations in the Parkin gene are known to cause a familial form of Parkinson's disease.

questions.stem: Which of the following nucleos(t)ide analogues is associated with the highest incidence of antiviral resistance in Hepatitis B?
questions.explanation: Lamivudine is associated with the highest incidence of antiviral resistance in Hepatitis B, with rates reaching up to 80% after 5 years of use. Newer analogues like entecavir and tenofovir have a much lower rate of resistance, making them preferred first-line treatments.

questions.stem: Which organism causes neurocysticerocosis?
questions.explanation:
Neurocysterocosis is caused by Taenia Solium. It is tapeworm that is found in contaminated food. It will initial attach to the intestinal wall but can travel to brain, muscle, eyes and skin. It can present with a number of symptoms including seizure, focal deficits, and intracranial hypertension. Below is a an image showing it typical radiological features.
Schistosoma haematobium is a blood fluke that causes is one of the major causes of schistosomiasis worldwide. Toxoplasma gondii is another parasite associated with toxoplasmosis. Common source of infection is cat faeces. In immunocompetent individuals this is typically cleared or remains latent without symptoms. However, can re-emerge in immunocompromised such as patient with HIV causing significant infection. Naefleria.fowleri is a brain eating amoeba which can be contracted from exposure to contaminated water in the nasal cavity. Where it accesses the brain through the cruciform plate. This disease has a significantly high mortality rate.

questions.stem: Assuuming a history of relevant drug exposure, which clinical feature most suggests serotonin toxicity?
questions.explanation: Hunter criteria are considered the most accurate diagnostic criteria for serotonin syndrome which requires a patient to have a history of exposure to a serotonergic drug and at least one of the following signs: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus plus agitation or diaphoresis, ocular clonus plus agitation or diaphoresis, tremor and hyperreflexia, and hypertonia + temp over 38 degrees Celsius with ocular or inducible clonus.

questions.stem: The primary purpose of irradiating red blood cells is to:

options.text: Reduce the rate of febrile non-haemolytic transfusion reactions by inactivating leucocytes
Prevent transfusion associated graft versus host disease

questions.explanation: The primary purpose of irradiating red blood cells is to prevent transfusion-associated graft-versus-host disease (TA-GVHD), a rare but often fatal complication that can occur when transfused donor Tlymphocytes attack the patient's cells. Irradiation inactivates these lymphocytes, preventing them from multiplying and causing the disease. This is crucial for immunocompromised patients who may not be able to reject the donor cells.

questions.stem: Which of the following processes DO NOT occur in germinal centres of lymph nodes?

options.text: Presentation of protein antigen by dendritic cells to naive T-cells

questions.explanation: B ­ phagocytosis by neutrophils

questions.stem: Teriparatide is used in the management of osteoporosis. Which of the following best describes its mechanism of action?

options.text: Competitive antagonist of the parathyroid hormone receptor

questions.explanation:
Teriparatide's mechanism of action (MOA) is that of an anabolic agent that stimulates new bone formation by preferentially stimulating osteoblasts (bone-building cells) over osteoclasts (boneresorbing cells). This action is similar to endogenous parathyroid hormone (PTH) but, with intermittent use, leads to a net increase in bone mass, density, and strength, which reduces fracture risk. It also influences calcium and phosphate homeostasis, causing a transient increase in serum calcium and a transient decrease in serum phosphate. In disease states associated with excess parathyroid hormone (PTH) in the blood, such as primary hyperparathyroidism, increased osteoclast activity, and accelerated bone resorption it may seem paradoxical that parathyroid hormone or its analogues can be a treatment for osteoporosis, a disease associated with accelerated bone loss. Ultimately, the difference in PTH effects on the bone stems from differences in the dose and pattern of exposure of bone to PTH. When under continuous exposure to PTH (eg, hyperparathyroidism), bone undergoes resorption more than formation, while intermittent exposure to low-dose PTH (like daily teriparatide administration) induces bone formation more than resorption. Although the underlying molecular mechanisms undergirding this variation in effect (ie,


catabolic effect with sustained exposure vs anabolic effect with intermittent exposure) are largely unknown, some involved molecular signalling mechanisms have been elucidated.

questions.stem: Which of the following ECG signs would make a rhythm more likely to be VT rather than SVT?

options.text: Concordance throughout the precordial leads (either positive or negative)

questions.explanation: Concordance throughout the precordial leads is a highly specific (and easily-recognised) sign of VT.

questions.stem:
A 54-year-old woman of Macedonian descent presents with 15 kg weight loss, abdominal pain diarrhoea, and cramping in her hands and feet. On examination, she looks unwell, hypovoaemic and cachectic, with a BMI 15 kg/m2. She has abdominal distension without organomegaly or ascites. Trousseau's sign is positive.
Her haemoglobin is 82 g/l [NR: 115-159], MCV 101 fL [80-96], platelets 628 x 109/L [140400], white cell count 4.9 x 109/L [4-10] with a normal differential. Ferritin is 10 g/ml [15200], folic acid 3.5 nmol/L [6-38] and vitamin B12 100 pmol/L [150-600]. Her serum sodium is 128 mmol/L [135-145] potassium 2.4 mmol/L [3.5-5.5], renal function is normal. Her serum calcium is 0.84 mmol/L [2.1-2.6], corrected calcium 1.34, magnesium 0.36 mmol/L [0.7-1.3] and phosphate 0.31 mmol/L [0.8-1.5]. Her albumin is 15 g/L [35-50], alkaline phosphatase 340 IU/L [0-120], and INR 6.1 [0.8-1.3]. Liver function tests are otherwise normal. Vitamin D is <2 nmol/L [25-108], and parathormone 27.5 pmol/L [1-8].
Blood film is shown:

Which of the following tests is most likely to be diagnostic?
questions.explanation: Combination of severe malabsorption affecting the proximal small bowel and functional hyposplenism (Howell Jolly bodies in RBCs) suggests coeliac disease; here presenting as coeliac crisis.

questions.stem:
A 50-year-old male is referred to IBD clinic with a 20-year history of extensive ulcerative colitis in clinical remission on oral 5-ASA therapy previously managed by his GP. Routine blood tests demonstrate the following abnormalities.

Laboratory results: Alanine aminotransferase Gamma-glutamyl transferase Alkaline phosphatase Total bilirubin Albumin INR

45 IU/L 230 IU/L 322 IU/L 15 umol/L 40 g/L 1.1

[5­30] [10-55] [30-110] [3­20] [33­46] [0.9-1.3]

He has no hepatobiliary symptoms. IgM, IgG4 and CA-19.9 titres are all within normal limits. MRCP demonstrates multiple short intra- and extra-hepatic duct biliary strictures with normal intervening biliary segments. He has normal liver parenchyma and a smooth liver contour alongside a normal appearing gallbladder.
Which is the next best investigation?
questions.explanation: Patients with PSC are at increased risk of cholelithiasis and cholangitis, dominant benign strictures, cholangiocarcinoma, gallbladder carcinoma, cirrhosis and death. Patients with PSC and concurrent IBD are also at increased risk of colonic dysplasia and colorectal cancer. They require colonoscopy at the time of PSC diagnosis and annual colonoscopy thereafter for dysplasia screening. A liver biopsy is not necessary for diagnosis when the history, biochemistry and imaging are all consistent.

questions.stem:
Monica's brother and maternal uncle were affected with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD).
What is her risk of having a child affected with DMD?
questions.explanation: Monica's mother is an obligate carrier for DMD (affected brother and son). Therefore, Monica has a 1 in 2 chance of being a carrier and a 1 in 4 chance of passing on a faulty DMD gene to each of her children. Only her sons would be at risk of being affected, so there is a 1 in 8 chance of an affected son.

questions.stem:
47-year-old Maria is having neoadjuvant chemotherapy for triple negative breast cancer. She presented to the emergency department on day 7, feeling unwell. On examination, her temperature was 39°C, her pulse was 112 beats per minute and her blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg. Her bloods have been taken for full blood count, urea and electrolytes and liver function tests. Blood cultures have been drawn from her port-a-cath and from a peripheral site.
What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text:
Intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics and granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-
CSF)

questions.explanation: Fever in neutropenic patients is a medical emergency. Empiric broad-spectrum antibacterial therapy should be started immediately after blood cultures have been drawn and before any other investigations have been completed. Fever may not be present in neutropenic patients, particularly if the patient is elderly or taking corticosteroids. For the greatest survival benefit, start antibiotics as early as possible. Start antibiotics within 1 hour of the patient presenting to medical care or, if there are signs of sepsis or septic shock start antibiotics within 30 minutes. Reference eTG- Therapeutic guidelines

questions.stem:
A 46-year-old woman presents 2 hours after the onset of acute left arm weakness. CT perfusion imaging reveals an acute thrombus in her middle cerebral artery, and she undergoes thrombolysis and endovascular clot retrieval.
Subsequent duplex ultrasound imaging of both carotid arteries reveals no stenosis, and no atrial fibrillation is identified on cardiac monitoring. On day 4 of her admission, the following blood test results are returned:

Lupus anticoagulant: Beta2 glycoprotein IgG: Anti-cardiolipin IgG:

positive 122 U/mL 84 U/mL

[<20 U/mL] [<40 U/mL]

Which of the following is the most appropriate agent to prevent subsequent thrombotic events?
questions.explanation:
There is a high probability that this patient has antiphospholipid syndrome (APLS). The presence of a venous or arterial thrombotic event, plus one or more of a positive lupus anticoagulant, beta-2 glycloprotein IgG &/or anti-cardiolipin IgG (confirmed with a repeat positive result 3 months apart) satisfies the criteria for APLS.
Patients with APLS are at high risk for recurrent venous and arterial thromboembolic events, and this risk is highest in patients who test positive on all three assays ("triple positive APLS"). Regardless of the site of thrombosis, anticoagulant therapy provides superior secondary thromboprophylaxis rather than antiplatelets alone.
In triple positive APLS patients, the use of rivaroxaban was found in a randomised controlled trial to be inferior to warfarin in preventing recurrent thrombosis, particularly arterial thromboses.

questions.stem: All of the following are potential treatments in hereditary angioedema, except:

A 27-year-old female presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of dyspnoea and malaise. She is experiencing haemoptysis, headaches, and fever. Her bloods show a WCC of 15.0 x 10^6 cells/L and CRP is 125 mg/L. She is otherwise fit and well. She and her partner recently moved home to a building where she states the water piping wasn't quite ready yet.
What is the most likely causative organism of this presentation?
questions.explanation:
Legionella pneumophila is found in natural water supplies and soil. It is also found in many recirculating and water supply systems. It causes haemoptysis and pleuritic chest pain. This patient likely contracted the infection from her water piping. Table from TG 2019 Community acquired pneumonia
Legionella species Legionella species are acquired from environmental sources. Patients may present with nonrespiratory symptoms, including confusion and diarrhoea. Hyponatraemia may be present. Risk factors for Legionella disease include chronic lung disease, smoking, diabetes, end-stage kidney disease, malignancy, and immune compromise, including chronic corticosteroid use. Of the 424 confirmed cases of Legionella pneumonia in Australia in 2014, Legionella pneumophila caused 46% (98% of which were serogroup 1) and Legionella longbeachae caused 39%. L. pneumophila serogroup 1 is the serogroup most commonly associated with outbreaks, whereas L. longbeachae causes sporadic infections (eg after exposure to potting mix). L. pneumophila causes more severe CAP, whereas L. longbeachae usually causes less severe CAP.

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old man with known T2 incomplete spinal cord injury, following a motor vehicle accident 3 years earlier, complains of sudden new onset severe 8/10 throbbing headache, bilateral and frontally predominant, associated with facial flushing and sweatiness, blurred vision and nasal congestion, apparently unprecipitated. On examination, BP 150-90, HR 45 regular, afebrile. Pupils constricted, minimally reactive to light. No new focal neurological signs.
What is the most likely contributor to this presentation?
questions.explanation:
Autonomic dysreflexia in spinal cord injury. From Lakra et al Relatively common in people with spinal cord injuries above the level of T6. Three times more common in those with complete spinal cord injury than in those with incomplete injury (91% versus 27%) noxious stimulus sends signals via afferent sensory nerves to the spinal cord. This triggers a sympathetic response from T6 to L2 that is pathologically exaggerated, due to intrinsic cord hyper excitability below the lesion and lack of descending modulation - akin to motor spasticity - with sympathetic response causing massive vasoconstriction of the muscular, splanchnic and cutaneous vascular beds the increase in blood pressure stimulates the carotid and aortic baroreceptors, increasing brainstem parasympathetic outflow, with resulting descending drive causing relative bradycardia and peripheral vasodilatation. The vagal drive cannot pass below the level of the lesion, such that unopposed sympathetic activity below that level can lead to lifethreatening rising blood pressure. Patients with spinal injuries below T10 rarely experience autonomic dysreflexia, as the splanchnic innervation remains intact and allows compensatory parasympathetic dilatation of the splanchnic vascular bed. Ordinarily dysreflexia is defined as an acute increase in systolic blood pressure above 20 mmHg from baseline with headache: patients with spinal cord injury above T6 tend to have systolic blood pressure between 90 and 110 mmHg, so sometimes the degree of seemingly moderate hypertension does not seem to match the symptoms. Most common causes bladder over distension, accounting for 75 to 85% of episodes stop the second most common causes over distension of the bowel. Common causes can be remembered as: bladder (infection, retention, stones, blocked catheter), bowel (constipation, impaction), boils (skin damage), bones (fractures), back passage (haemorrhoid or fissure), babies (pregnancy, sexual intercourse, breastfeeding).



2

questions.stem:
63-year-old Sheila presented with increased abdominal distention and constipation. Physical examination revealed ascites. Her investigations are as follows
Hb 120 g/L (120-170 g/L) Platelets 700 (150-400 x109/L) White cells count 11 (4-12 x109/L) Neutrophils 9 (2-8 x109/L Serum Bilirubin 30 (< 34 micromol/L) ALT 45 (5-50 Units/L) Total protein 63 (50-80 g/L) Serum Albumin 33 (35- 52 g/L) Serum globulin 30 (25-40 g/L) Serum carcinoembryonic antigen 18 g/L (<10) Serum CA-125 2130 U/mL (<35) CT scan of chest, abdomen, and pelvis ­ Splenic metastasis, ascites and omental caking.
What is the most important risk factor for ovarian cancer?
questions.explanation:
Family history of ovarian cancer · Early menarche (before age 12) has been associated with a slightly increased risk of ovarian cancer in some, but not all, studies · A patient who has one first-degree relative with ovarian cancer has an approximate 5% risk of ovarian cancer, a 3.5% risk if the patient has one second-degree relative, and a 7% risk if the patient has two affected relative. Much of this risk may be due to pathogenic variants in BRCA and, to a lesser extent, in other genes associated with ovarian cancer (eg, RAD51C, RAD51D, BRCA1-interacting protein 1 [BRIP1], as well as the mismatch repair genes associated with Lynch syndrome). It is estimated that genetic mutations account for up to one-quarter of ovarian cancer cases. · Infertility treatment do not appear to be independent risk factors for ovarian cancer. · Parous patients appear to be at decreased risk for ovarian cancer compared with nulliparous patients, and the risk appears to decrease with increasing parity. In one study, risk of ovarian cancer decreased by 8 % for each additional pregnancy.
In two systematic reviews, high body mass index appeared to modestly increase ovarian cancer risk (odds ratios 1.3 and 1.1), but another systematic review concluded available data were limited and inconsistent, precluding a clear assessment of risk.

questions.stem:
200mg of the new anitviral `Novid' is administered to a 100kg man. The volume of distribution is 0.5L/kg and the half-life is 4 hours.
What is the best estimate of the clearance of `Novid'?
questions.explanation: Vd = 2L/kg * 100kg = 50L. Clearance = (Vd*0.693)/t1/2. Clearance is the volume of plasma cleared of drug per unit time.

questions.stem:
Two months post deceased donor kidney transplantation, a patient maintained on tacrolimus, mycophenolate and steroids develops dysuria, notes cloudy urine and candida is grown from their MSU. Upon ID team advice, fluconazole 200mg daily is commenced. After 1 week, the patient feels well; however, an increase in serum creatinine from 120 to 170umol/L is noted.
What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: Fluconazole predictably inhibits p450 metabolism and commonly increases tacrolimus exposure. Other causes are all possible, but much less likely in this case.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements explains the mechanism of action of omalizumab in severe asthma?

options.text: Binds blood circulating free IgE molecules

questions.explanation: Omalizumab was the first biological treatment for severe asthma. It is a humanised monoclonal antibody that binds blood-circulating free IgE molecules, inhibiting the activation of mast cells and basophils (answer A is correct).

questions.stem: In patients with rheumatic diseases, use of which biologic disease modifying antirheumatic drugs is associated with an increased risk of hospitalisation and death as a result of COVID-19?
questions.explanation:
Reference: Avouac J, Drumez E, Hachullka E et. al. COVID-19 outcomes in patients with inflammatory rheumatic and musculoskeletal diseases treated with rituximab: a cohort study. Lancet Rheumatology 2021; 3: e419 ­ 426.
Rationale: Several studies have highlighted the risk of more severe COVID-19 outcomes in patients with rheumatic diseases on rituximab. This study found that rituximab use was associated with more severe disease (death or admission to ICU) as well as a markedly longer hospital stay.

questions.stem: Which of the following endocrine changes are NOT typical of pregnancy?

options.text: A fall in serum parathyroid hormone (PTH)

questions.explanation: Pregnancy is a state of hyperreninaemic hyperaldosteronism due to vascular underfilling. The placenta produces PTH-related peptide (hence fall in PTH), CRH and ACTH, hence, increase in cortisol (further increased by estradiol-mediated increase in CBG). Prolactin increases up to 10-fold.

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old male presents to outpatients with active ileocolonic inflammatory Crohn's disease despite 6 months of optimized azathioprine therapy and a previous course of prednisolone 2 months ago. He is passing four loose stools per day and has an elevated faecal calprotectin of 400 µg/g (<50 µg/g). He is particularly troubled by arthropathy. He reports 8 weeks of pain, 45 minutes of early morning stiffness and subtle swelling of his right knee, left ankle and left hip. Examination confirms mild synovitis but with full range of motion and retained weight-bearing ability. He is afebrile and otherwise well. Rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP, ANA, dsDNA, ENA and HLA-B27 are negative.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next management step?



5
questions.explanation: This patient has a type I enteropathic arthritis. Type I is an acute pauci-articular inflammatory arthropathy, affecting fewer than five large usually lower limb weightbearing joints and is usually associated with active IBD. Type II is polyarticular, often symmetrical, affects a larger number of peripheral joints, and is independent of IBD activity. This patient requires step up in his maintenance therapy due to active, steroiddependent luminal Crohn's disease despite optimised azathioprine. Whilst either infliximab or ustekinumab are reasonable options, the presence of convincing extraintestinal manifestations and the more rapid onset of infliximab makes this the most appropriate choice. Whilst methotrexate has some evidence in peripheral arthopathy, it is unlikely to adequately address his luminal disease given he has failed optimised thiopurine.

questions.stem:
A couple have a child affected with Tuberous Sclerosis (TS). Genetic testing reveals a germline mutation in the TSC1 gene in the child. Both parents are tested via a blood sample and neither carries the mutation identified in the child.
What recurrence risk would you give the parents regarding having another child affected with TS?

options.text: 0% because neither parent carries the TSC1 mutation identified in the child.

1% because of the risk of one of the parents being gonadal mosaic for the TSC1
mutation identified in the child.
25% because TSC is inherited in an autosomal recessive manner.
50% because TSC is inherited in an autosomal dominant manner.

questions.explanation: Although the parents have both tested negative for the TSC1 mutation, there is still a small residual risk that one of the parents is mosaic for the mutation within their germ cell tissue (gonadal or germinal mosaicism). Empiric data suggests this risk is approximately 1% for TS.

questions.stem:
An 82-year-old man has been admitted following a fall at the local shopping centre. X-ray in the Emergency department shows a left intertrochanteric hip fracture. His wife reports a history of Type 2 diabetes mellitus, Alzheimer dementia, hypertension and benign prostatic hypertrophy.
The following are all appropriate management options EXCEPT:
questions.explanation: The rest are all appropriate pre-op measures in a hip fracture. There is no given indication for the TTE in this case, which may delay his time to surgery.

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old woman experiences relapse of diffuse large B cell lymphoma (DLBCL) 18 months after being treated to complete remission with 6 cycles of R-CHOP chemotherapy. Following 2 cycles of the 2nd line chemotherapy regimen R-ICE, a PET scan shows persistent disease in a conglomerate of retroperitoneal lymph nodes. She then receives combination chemotherapy with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide, followed by an infusion of axicabtagene ciloleucel.
24hrs after infusion, she develops a fever of 39.2 C and is admitted for investigation and is treated with IV piperacillin/tazobactam. 24hrs after admission she develops hypotension (SBP 85mmHg) and hypoxia (SaO2 85%). The hypotension is refractory to resuscitation with IV fluids and her SaO2 rises to 96% with the application of 6L/min of O2 via face mask. Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral infiltrates.
Alongside supportive care, what agent should next be administered to treat the most likely underlying diagnosis?
questions.explanation:
This patient most likely has cytokine release syndrome (CRS) which occurs in ~40-90% of patients with lymphoma following treatment with a CD19 targeting CAR T cell therapy. Fever >38.0 C is a universal feature of CRS with a median time of onset of 48hrs post infusion. Whilst all organ systems can be affected, the overall severity CRS is determined by the presence and severity of hypotension and hypoxia.
Alongside supportive care, the interleukein-6 inhibitor tocilizumab is the first line treatment for CRS and is highly efficacious in preventing the progression to severe CRS, particularly when instituted early.
Whilst corticosteroids can be used for severe and refractory CRS, their use can be associated with impaired anti-tumour CAR T cell efficacy and are thus used in highly selected patients only.



4





Whilst highly pre-treated lymphoma patients are at risk for PJP, it would not typically be associated with hypotension and its incidence is much lower than that of CRS (particularly with near-universal primary prophylaxis)

questions.explanation:
Reference: Arnold, M. How to treat Rheumatoid arthritis. AusDoc 4 June 2021.
Rationale: While all of the above infections are possible with immunosuppression, Herpes Zoster infection is higher with oral JAK inhibitors than the other listed. It is postulated that this may be due to its suppression of interferon, which is required for the early clearance of viral infections.

questions.stem:
Three weeks after a meal at a fast-food café, a 21-year-old man develops malaise, fatigue, and loss of appetite. He notes passing dark urine. On examination, he has scleral icterus and right upper quadrant tenderness. Investigations show serum AST of 62 U/L and ALT of 58 U/L. The total bilirubin concentration is 3.9 mg/dL, and the direct bilirubin concentration is 2.8 mg/dL. His symptoms improve gradually over the next 3 weeks. On returning to the café, he finds that is has been closed by the Department of Health.
Which of the following serologic test results is most likely to be positive in this patient?
questions.explanation: This patient's clinical presentation is consistent with an acute hepatitis with jaundice and RUQ tenderness. With the context given, it suggests this is due to a viral hepatitis contracted from the faecal-oral route, which would be due to acute hepatitis A compared with the other options given which are blood borne viruses (and hepatitis D requires superinfection with hepatitis B). Therefore, the correct answer is a positive IgM hepatitis A antibody. https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/hepatitis-a

questions.stem:
A 25-year-old man is seen 2 months post motorcycle accident with traumatic right brachial plexopathy. On examination, there is upper trunk predominant involvement, with right finger and wrist movement relatively preserved; though he is unable to lift the right arm in shoulder abduction or flexion, and with weakness of elbow flexion. In detail, the right upper limb motor findings show: initial shoulder abduction present lying prone, though not against gravity; no shoulder abduction beyond around 10 degrees; no apparent shoulder external rotation; shoulder adduction reduced, though present with moderate resistance; elbow flexion in supination present against minimal resistance; elbow flexion in mid-prone position present with moderate resistance; preserved normal wrist flexion and extension, finger extension, finger abduction, thumb abduction.
Which summary of motor power by MRC scoring is apt?

options.text: Deltoid 0; Suprapsinatus 0; Infraspinatus 1; biceps 3; brachioradialis 4; hand intrinsics 4+
Deltoid 1; Suprapsinatus 1; Infraspinatus 0; biceps 4-; brachioradialis 4; hand intrinsics 5
Deltoid 0; Suprapsinatus 2; Infraspinatus 0; biceps 4-; brachioradialis 4; hand intrinsics 5

Deltoid 1; Suprapsinatus 3; Infraspinatus 1; biceps 2; brachioradialis 4; hand intrinsics 5



7

questions.explanation:
From Up to date: Grading strength -- The most common convention for grading muscle strength is the 0 to 5 Medical Research Council scale:
0 = no contraction 1 = visible muscle twitch but no movement of the joint 2 = weak contraction insufficient to overcome gravity 3 = weak contraction able to overcome gravity but no additional resistance 4 = weak contraction able to overcome some resistance but not full resistance 5 = normal; able to overcome full resistance
The most compelling feature of this scale is its reproducibility; an examiner is unlikely to assign a score of 1 to a muscle that another examiner graded 3 or stronger. A major limitation of the scale is that it is insensitive to subtle differences in strength. In particular, grade 4 covers a wide range, so that in most clinical situations the scale does not allow precise differentiation of the severity of weakness from one muscle to the next. Similarly, it is not a sensitive tool for documenting moderate changes in strength over time.

questions.stem:
54-year-old Janet is having treatment for metastatic colorectal cancer. She presented to you with a rash covering her face, chest and back.

For her next chemotherapy cycle, which drug you will withhold until the rash is improved?
questions.explanation:
Cetuximab
Cetuximab is an EGFR inhibitor used for treatment of KRAS wild type colorectal cancer. The most common side effects of EGFR-targeted therapies are dermatologic toxicities and diarrhea. At present, it is believed that the cutaneous side effects induced by EGFR inhibitors are due to inhibition of EGFR in basal keratinocytes and hair follicles, which express high levels of EGFR similar to tumour cells.



5





https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4905346/
For management of Cetuximab induced rash see EviQ guidelines https://www.eviq.org.au/clinical-resources/side-effect-and-toxicity-management/hairskin-and-nails/1241-acneiform-rash-associated-with-egfr-inhibitor#management

questions.stem:
The usual dose of a drug given orally is 100mg daily for people with normal renal function. Its oral bioavailability is 0.50 and its fraction excreted unchanged is 0.60.
In a person with renal impairment whose eGFR has halved, what is the ideal renallyadjusted dose for once daily oral dosing?
questions.explanation: 60% of the usual dose that is renally excreted (60mg) needs to be halved. Clearance of other 40% (40mg) is unaffected. Hence the renally-adjusted dose = (60mg*50%) + 40mg = 70mg.

questions.stem: Regarding SGLT2 inhibitors, which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.text: Drug should be withheld if the patient is fasting
Are relatively weak hypoglycaemic agents for people with eGFR<45ml/minute

Should not be discontinued if eGFR drops by 10ml/minute within 1 week of
commencement

Are indicated to delay progression of diabetic nephropathy in patients with T1D



8

questions.explanation: Contra-indicated in T1D (at present). SGLT2i should be withheld if sick or fasting to avoid ketoacidosis. The capacity of SGLT2i to cause glycosuria is GFR dependent, and SGLT2i predictably cause an early fall in eGFR, average 5ml/min, which does not progress and long-term benefits are maintained regardless of magnitude of initial fall.

questions.stem: Which of the following cystic fibrosis mutation types is the most frequent?
questions.explanation:
There are over 2100 mutations for cystic fibrosis that have been identified. The most common mutation type with a frequency of 38% is missense mutation, followed by frameshift (16%), splicing (10%), nonsense (8%), in frame insertion/deletion (2%) and promoter (<1%).
Reference: Cystic Fibrosis Genetic Analysis Consortium Database (available from http://www.genet.sickkids.on.ca/cftr/StatisticsPage.html).

Reference: None specifically provided for this question

questions.stem: The use of ACE inhibitors in pregnancy is associated with which complication?
questions.explanation: ACE inhibitors are associated with renal agenesis when used in pregnancy. Beta blockers are associated with hypoglycemia.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an expected effect of testosterone treatment in men?
questions.explanation: Exogenous testosterone treatment suppresses FSH and does not achieve sufficiently high intratesticular testosterone, both factors leading to reduced spermatogenesis and over time reduced testicular size (note that testosterone is not a reliable contraceptive).

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old male is referred with chronic hepatitis B infection for further management. They are otherwise well, take no medications, and have no significant family history.

Laboratory results: Platelet count Alanine aminotransferase Gamma-glutamyl transferase Alkaline phosphatase Total bilirubin Albumin INR

278 x10^9/L 26 IU/L 50 IU/L 100 IU/L 10 umol/L 40 g/L 1.0

[150­400] [5­30] [10-55] [30-110] [3­20] [33­46] [0.9-1.3]

Hepatitis B surface antigen Hepatitis B surface antibody Hepatitis B core antibody Hepatits B eAg

Positive Negative Positive Negative

Hepatitis B VL Hepatitis B VL (6 months ago)

1822 IU/ml 1734 IU/ml

Liver ultrasound: mild hepatosteatosis with smooth liver contour. 8mm portal vein diameter, no recanalization of ligamentum teres and normal spleen size.

What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Review in 6 months with repeat LFTs and HBV DNA

questions.explanation:
This patient has treatment naïve, non-cirrhotic eAg-negative chronic hepatitis B without biochemical evidence of liver inflammation (normal ALT). They do not qualify for treatment and a viral load <2000 IU/mL can be safely monitored in 6 months, with



6





consensus agreement from AASLD, APASL and EASL. In eAg-negative chronic Hepatitis B, if the HBV DNA VL is > 20 000 IU/ml and the ALT is >2x ULN, treatment is indicated with consensus agreement from AASLD, APASL and EASL. If the HBV DNA VL is between 2000 ­ 20 000 IU/ml, international treatment guidelines vary according to the degree of ALT elevation (1-2x vs. 2x ULN) and any equivocal values can be further clarified with a liver biopsy to look for moderate-severe hepatic inflammation, advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis.

questions.stem:
Tom presents with a paraganglioma on the left side of his neck. Subsequent genetic investigation reveals he carries a germline heterozygous mutation in the SDHD gene (familial paraganglioma syndrome type 1). SDHD is subject to maternal imprinting.
Which of the following statements about the risk of transmission of the condition to his children is most correct?

options.text: Only his sons are at 50% risk of inheriting the SDHD mutation.
Only his daughters are at 50% risk of inheriting the SDHD mutation.
Each of his children are at 50% risk of inheriting the SDHD mutation.
None of his children are at risk of inheriting the SDHD mutation.

questions.explanation: SDHD is subject to maternal imprinting; this means the copy you inherit from your mother is silenced. A man who carries a SDHD mutation will still have a 50% chance of passing on the mutation to each of his children (sons or daughters); his copy will not be imprinted and so any of his children that inherit the SDHD mutation would be at risk of developing paragaglioma.

questions.stem:

Which of the following statements is FALSE?

options.text: There is a dropout of atrial pacemaker cells leading to a decrease in heart rate.
There is decreased responsiveness to beta-adrenergic receptor stimulation.

Reduced arterial elasticity causes a decrease in systolic blood pressure.



10

questions.explanation: Reduced arterial elasticity and associated increase in vascular stiffness leads to increased SBP.

Which of the following investigations will determine the appropriateness of instituting therapy with bortezomib, lenalidomide and dexamethasone (VRd)?

options.text: Karyotype and fluorescent in situ hybridisation of bone marrow aspirate
Next generation sequencing of bone marrow aspirate
Combination therapy is not indicated, regardless of subsequent results

questions.explanation:
This question tests knowledge of the criteria with which to classify patients with plasma cell dyscrasias into one of three categories (monoclonal gammopathy of uncertain significance (MGUS); smouldering myeloma; symptomatic multiple myeloma), and the indications for commencement of therapy in these disorders.
With the results initially available, this patient has a diagnosis of smouldering myeloma ­ they have >10% bone marrow infiltration but no CRAB features.
Combination therapy is not indicated for the treatment of MGUS or smouldering myeloma (treatment of smouldering myeloma with single agent therapy is highly controversial, but not standard practice), and thus the key to this question is identifying the investigation that will "upstage" this patient from smouldering myeloma to symptomatic myeloma.
Whilst this patient does not have CRAB features, the International Myeloma Working Group (IMWG) includes the following criteria as "Myeloma Defining Events": · >60% clonal plasma cells on bone marrow examination · Serum involved/uninvolved free light chain ration >100 · >1 focal lesion on MRI that is >5mm in size
Thus, MRI spine is the investigation that will allow definitive classification of this patient. Whilst cytogenetic and molecular studies can help predict the outcome of patients treated with VRd, they do not influence the classification of patients with plasma cell dyscrasias or the decision to institute active therapy.

questions.explanation: Benzathine penicillin 2.4 mega units IM is the first line treatment for syphilis and doxycycline can be used as second-line if there is anaphylaxis to penicillin. Ceftriaxone and azithromycin are used as a combination for gonorrhoea. Azithromycin or doxycycline are options for treatment of chlamydia. For rectal chlamydia, doxycycline 100mg BD for 7 days was found to be superior to azithromycin 1 gram in an Australian RCT in MSM. Lau A et al. Azithromycin or Doxycycline for Asymptomatic Rectal Chlamydia trachomatis. N Engl J Med 2021; 384:2418-2427. June 24, 2021

questions.stem:
A 56-year-old female presents with onset over 3 days of worsening numbness and paraesthesiae in feet and hands. Over the previous day she has noted worsening weakness, particularly of proximal muscles. Bulbar function is normal. She is areflexic. EMG shows multifocal conduction block, focal slowing of conduction velocities, with mild associated loss of amplitude of motor unit potentials. There is no evidence of active denervation. Cerebrospinal fluid examination shows raised protein with no increase in cell count. Diagnosis of acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy is considered.
Which of the following is most likely to have contributed?

options.text: Incidence is rare and comparable with each of these exposures

questions.explanation:
Neurological complications after first dose of COVID-19 vaccines and SARS-CoV-2 infection. Patone M et al, Nat Med. 2021;27(12):2144. Epub 2021 Oct 25.
Emerging reports of rare neurological complications associated with COVID-19 infection and vaccinations are leading to regulatory, clinical and public health concerns. We undertook a self-controlled case series study to investigate hospital admissions from neurological complications in the 28days after a first dose of ChAdOx1nCoV-19 (n=20,417,752) or BNT162b2 (n=12,134,782), and after a SARSCoV-2-positive test (n=2,005,280). There was an increased risk of Guillain-Barré syndrome (incidence rate ratio (IRR), 2.90; 95% confidence interval (CI): 2.15-3.92 at 15-21days after vaccination) and Bell's palsy (IRR, 1.29; 95% CI: 1.08-1.56 at 1521days) with ChAdOx1nCoV-19 (AstraZeneca). There was an increased risk of hemorrhagic stroke (IRR, 1.38; 95% CI: 1.12-1.71 at 15-21days) with BNT162b2 (Pfizer). An independent Scottish cohort provided further support for the association between ChAdOx1nCoV and Guillain-Barré syndrome (IRR, 2.32; 95% CI: 1.08-5.02 at 1-28days). There was a substantially higher risk of all neurological outcomes in the 28days after a positive SARS-CoV-2 test including Guillain-Barré syndrome (IRR, 5.25; 95% CI: 3.00-9.18). Overall, we estimated 38 excess cases of Guillain-Barré syndrome per 10million people receiving ChAdOx1nCoV-19 and 145 excess cases per 10million people after a positive SARS-CoV-2 test. In summary, although we find an increased risk of neurological complications in those who received COVID-19 vaccines, the risk of these complications is greater following a positive SARS-CoV-2 test.

A patient receives an orthotopic liver transplant because of alcohol-induced cirrhosis, and develops AKI requiring dialysis immediately post-op. Pre-op creatinine was 64umol/L.

A major intraoperative bleed was likely to have contributed to his AKI

His risk of early mortality is now significantly increased compared to the average liver

questions.explanation: He has AKI, and given a normal creatinine pre-transplant, this will likely recovery sufficiently to enable him to cease dialysis. questions.stem: Which of the following is a contraindication for performing spirometry?

options.text: Myocardial infarction within the last 3 months

questions.explanation:
The forced expiratory manouvre used in spirometry increases intrathoracic, intraabdominal, and intracranial pressure. Relative contraindications for spirometry are due it increases in myocardial demand or changes in blood pressure, increases in intracranial/intraocular pressure, increases in sinus and middle ear pressures, increases in intrathoracic and intraabdominal pressure and infection control issues. Myocardial infarction within 1 week is a relative contraindication (answer A is



8





incorrect). Stress incontinence is not a contraindication (answer B in incorrect). Eye surgery within 1 week is a contraindication (answer D is incorrect). Late term pregnancy is a contraindication (answer C is correct).
Reference: Graham et al. Standardization of Spirometry 2019 Update. An Official American Thoracic Society and European Respiratory Society Technical Statement. AJRCCM 20(8) Oct 15 2019.



9

questions.stem:
Juan, 30, has a new diagnosis of dilated cardiomyopathy.
A cardiac MRI will be most useful for which of the following?

options.text: Ascertaining the aetiology of the cardiomyopathy
Identifying if he needs an upfront ICD emergently
Guiding which order guideline-directed medical therapy should be commenced in

questions.explanation: A cardiac MRI gives enormous information regarding aetiology of cardiomyopathy, depending on location of scar (delayed gadolinium enhancement) or inflammatory changes. It is often diagnostic, and almost always also helps rule out relevant differentials even when it doesn't definitively rule in an aetiology. MRI findings would not determine need for upfront ICD ­ this would depend on clinical history, for example a VT arrest. If no ventricular arrhythmia history, GDMT would always be started before ICD placed. LVEDP is determined at coronary angiography. GDMT is always commenced as per protocol, with nuances according to clinical history (for example beta-blocker intolerance, renal failure) not MRI findings.

questions.stem: Diltiazem will have a pharmacodynamic drug-drug interaction with oral digoxin via which mechanism?
questions.explanation: A pharmacodynamic drug-drug interaction directly affects drug actions, while a pharmacokinetic drug-drug interaction affects drug absorption or metabolism.

questions.stem:
A 24-year-old Asian man presents with acute onset weakness after eating a large pizza the night prior. In the emergency department he appears anxious, heart rate is 90 bpm, and he has symmetrical lower limb weakness [3/5]. Serum potassium is 2.1 mmol/L [3.55.0].
What is the most important next step?
questions.explanation: History, presentation, and ethnicity are typical for thyrotoxic periodic paralysis. In TTP, thyroid hormone and adrenergic stimulation stimulate the Na-K-ATPase, contributing to intermittent hypokalaemia. Carbohydrate load and exercise can be precipitators. Treatment includes cautious potassium supplementation to prevent rebound hyperkalaemia (no total body deficit), beta-blockade and restoring euthyroidism.

questions.stem: Which of the following is associated with the highest risk of progression from Barrett's oesophagus to oesophageal adenocarcinoma?

options.text: Increased age, female sex and short length of Barrett's oesophagus
Increased age, long length of Barrett's oesophagus and uncontrolled acid reflux

Use of a proton pump inhibitor and H pylori infection with strains that express that
cytotoxin associated gene A
Male sex, increased age and short tongue of Barrett's oesophagus

questions.explanation: Male sex, increased age, uncontrolled reflux and long segment of Barrett's oesophagus are risk factors for progression to adenocarcinoma. PPI use and H pylori with CAG- A expressing strain are protective factors.

questions.stem:
A couple presents to find a cause for their 18-year-old son's intellectual impairment. They wish to determine if their intellectually normal daughter is at risk of having a child similarly affected to their son. Chromosome microarray is performed on a blood sample from the son and a microduplication at Xp13 is found.
What investigation should be performed next to best interpret the microarray finding and predict the risk of recurrence for the couple's daughter?



1

options.text: Karyotype in the son to validate the microarray finding

Microarray analysis in the daughter to determine her carrier status for the Xp13
duplication

Microarray analysis in the mother to determine her carrier status for the Xp13
duplication

Microarray analysis in both parents to determine their carrier status for the Xp13
duplication

questions.explanation: Microarray has confirmed the duplication. Karyotype is not needed for validation and would likely not detect this duplication. First step would be to test parents to see if either of them carry the duplication to determine the daughter's risk. As the duplication is on the X chromosome it cannot have been inherited from the father, so testing in him is unnecessary. Maternal testing is the next step.

questions.stem: Which diagnostic tool is often used to assess delirium in the elderly?
questions.explanation: B and C are cognitive screens and will likely be impaired in delirium but not often used to assess for delirium per se. GCS may also be impacted by delirium but not specific for causes of impaired LOC.

questions.stem:
A 50-year-old woman is reviewed in haematology clinic after being referred for splenomegaly with symptoms of abdominal discomfort. Her FBE showed a Hb 90, WCC 12, and platelets of 90. The blood film showed mild to moderate red blood cell teardrops, occasional nucleated red cells, and some rare blasts <1%. Biochemistry demonstrated increased LDH twice the upper limit of normal. She undergoes a bone marrow biopsy which demonstrated significant fibrosis and abnormal megakaryocytes.
Pathogenic variants (mutations) in which of the following genes is the most likely to be found?
questions.explanation: JAK2. The mostly likely diagnosis in this patient is primary myelofibrosis. Her peripheral blood film is leucoerythroblastic (containing blasts and nucleated red cells) and the marrow demonstrates marked fibrosis. This condition is one of several myeloproliferative neoplasms that include polycythaemia vera and essential thrombocytosis. These disorders are characterised by a high prevalence of the JAK2 V617F variant. This mutation is observed in 95% of polycythaemia vera patients, and ~60% in both essential thrombocytosis and primary myelofibrosis. Ruxolitinib is a targeted inhibitor of JAK2 than can be used to decrease symptoms and decrease spleen size.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of the SARS-CoV-2 antiviral nirmatrelvir/ritonavir?

options.text: Inhibits fusion of viral and host phospholipid bilayers
Inhibits complementary DNA synthesis from a viral RNA template

questions.explanation:
Option A: Molnupiravir and remdesivir ­ nucleoside analogues that are incorporated by the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase into the new RNA strand, ultimately halting further replication



1





Option B: Paxlovid (nirmatrelvir/ritonavir) is a boosted protease inhibitor that targets the protease enzyme responsible for cutting the long polypeptide strand that is translated from the virus RNA into smaller, functional proteins ­ stuck in long form, cannot carry out functions necessary for viral replication C and D ­ not current mechanisms of action for COVID-19 antivirals
