questions.stem:
A 20-year-old female, with consanguineous parents, is admitted with her second presentation of Strep. pneumoniae pneumonia within the last 12 months. She has a history of systemic lupus erythematosus diagnosed at age 15. Her lupus is currently inactive with hydroxychloroquine treatment. Functional complement assays are performed.
Which of the following results are in keeping with her likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This patient likely has C2 deficiency (autosomal recessive) with recurrent pyogenic infections and early onset SLE. C2 deficiency affects the classical complement pathway resulting in reduced CH50 and normal AH50. Her lupus is inactive ­ if she had active lupus this could result in low C3 and C4 leading to reduced AH50 and reduced CH50 (due to consumption).

questions.stem:
A 37-year-old man presents with abdominal pain and a 6kg weight loss over the past 4 months. His father was diagnosed with papillary thyroid cancer at the age of 50. He is married with no children. Computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis revealed thickening of left ascending colon with high suspicion of malignancy. Colonoscope revealed an ulcerating mass, with biopsy confirming a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma.
Which of the following features suggest a stronger association with Lynch syndrome?

options.text: Tumour testing showing loss of MLH1 expression with a positive BRAF V600E mutation
Diagnosis of a left sided colorectal cancer
First degree family history of papillary thyroid cancer diagnosed at the age of 50
Diagnosis of colorectal cancer at the age of 37

questions.explanation:
The diagnosis of Lynch syndrome is autosomal dominant, with clinical diagnosis suspected when a patient history and family history fulfill the Amsterdam criteria. The Bethesda guidelines were developed later to identify more patients to test for microsatellite instability (MSI), but currently it is a standard practice to test all patients with newly diagnosed colorectal cancer for MSI 6.
Why not A - Detection of a somatic BRAF V600E mutation is closely associated with MLH1 hypermethylation, which is the most common cause of deficient mismatch repair, and the presence of either of these alterations in a tumor indicates a sporadic origin and rules out Lynch syndrome Why not B ­ Lynch syndrome is more associated with synchronous colorectal cancer, metachronous colorectal cancer or other lynch syndrome associated tumors. The side of colorectal cancer is not related to Lynch syndrome. Why not C ­ thyroid cancer is not a Lynch syndrome associated tumors

questions.stem: You have a patient with CKD stage 5 who is receiving erythropoietin. They report feeling tired and lethargic. Current tests show Hb 79 g/L, MVC 91, ferritin 790µg/L and transferrin saturation 10%. Regarding their management, you should ...

options.text: Take a history and examination looking for a source of chronic inflammation

questions.explanation: Chronic inflammation is an important and common cause of erythropoietin resistance, here suggested by the high ferritin but low TSat and Hb. Finding and addressing the cause is the correct answer. Iron deficiency is unlikely with normal MCV and high ferritin despite the low TSAT. Increasing erythropoietin dose is unlikely to help until the cause of inflammation is corrected.

A 55-year-old gentleman with no significant past medical history is admitted under the Respiratory service with a 48-hour history of fevers, productive cough and chest pain. Initial bloods demonstrate CRP of 150, WCC of 18.5 and neutrophils of 14.2. Sputum has been sent for routine microscopy and culture. Preliminary gram stain shows large numbers of squamous epithelial cells and some gram-positive cocci. A chest x-ray and CT chest are organised with representative images below.



3





The investigation that would most likely change the acute management (< 24 hours) of the patient is...

options.text: Sputum culture for MCS, AFB and Fungal culture
Extended respiratory viral PCR, including COVID-19 PCR
Pleural fluid pH, microscopy/gram stain and culture
Urinary antigens for legionella and Streptoccus pneumoniae

questions.explanation:
The diagnosis is most likely empyema. Further microbiological investigations may assist in identifying a pathogen and rationalising antibiotic therapy, but would take time to return, may not be positive (eg: sputum MCS) and would not change acute management. A pleural fluid pH (if < 7.2) would mean an ICC is indicated and would certainly change the acute management of the patient (eg: large volume thoracocentesis for complicated parapneumonic effusion vs ICC drainage for empyema).

While axial pain and stiffness remains the main clinical feature in patients with axial spondylarthritis a variety of other clinical manifestations are increasingly recognised.
Which extra-axial manifestation would be most likely seen in a patient with ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation:
Rationale: All of these clinical features are seen in patients with ankylosing spondylitis however, the most common is enthesitis seen in nearly 30% of patients. Anterior uveitis is seen in around 23% of patients, IBD in around 7%, peripheral enthesitis in around 29% and finally skin psoriasis in around 10%.

A 78-year-old man from a low socioeconomic group presents with progressively worsening dysphagia to solid foods. He is a current heavy smoker, has a preference for hot beverages and regularly chews areca nuts wrapped in betel leaves. He has lost 20kg and also reports haematemesis and retrosternal chest pain. He is of a low BMI and does not have a history of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease.
What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Barrett's oesophagus with progression to adenocarcinoma of the oesophagus

Squamous cell carcinoma of the oesophagus



5

questions.explanation:
GORD is associated with Barrett's oesophagus and adenocarcinoma of the oesophagus. Smoking, alcohol, hot liquids, areca nuts and N-nitroso compounds are associated with squamous cell carcinoma.

questions.stem:
An autosomal recessive condition has an incidence of 1 in 90,000.
What is the carrier frequency in the general population?
questions.explanation: Incidence = 1 in 90,000 Mutated allele = q, wildtype allele = p. So, q x q (q2) = 1 in 90,000, so q = 1 in 300. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium states that the frequency of carriers (pq) is double the frequency of wildtype and mutated alleles (p: 2pq: q). So, the carrier frequency is double that of q = 1/300 x 2 = 1 in 150.

questions.stem: What is the most common type of hip fracture in the elderly?
questions.explanation: Femoral neck and intertrochanteric are the most common (~ 90% fall into these 2 categories). Femoral head is quite rare and usually the result of a high-velocity event.

questions.stem:
A 70-year-old man is admitted into hospital for a neck of femur fracture post fall. On day 3, he develops a swollen right leg with a subsequent ultrasound showing deep vein thrombosis at the level of the right common iliac vein. He is commenced on therapeutic dose enoxaparin. On day 5, he develops large volume PR bleeding and becomes hypotensive. The last dose of enoxaparin was 2 hours ago.
What is the most appropriate anticoagulant reversal agent?
questions.explanation: Protamine. Enoxaparin functions by inhibiting factor Xa more so than thrombin. In contrast to protamine reversal of unfractionated heparin, only about 60% of the anticoagulant activity is reversed by protamine in the setting of low molecular weight heparin. Nonetheless, protamine is the most appropriate agent if reversal of bleeding is required (e.g. life-threatening bleeding, not stabilised by fluids and transfusions). Indarucizumab is used for dabigatran reversal. Prothrombinex is used for reversal of warfarin and direct factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban, rivaroxaban).

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of the antiviral zanamivir for treatment of influenza virus infection?
questions.explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors include oseltamivir, peramivir and zanamivir, that act by inhibiting the neuraminidase enzyme to prevent viral budding and release of daughter virions from the host cell to infect other cells. Peramivir is available in intravenous formulation. Other options: A ­ M2 inhibitors include amantadine, rimantadine ­ effective only against influenza A, block the M2 H+ channel leading to incomplete viral uncoating and disassembly. C- ie remdesivir, molnupiravir ­ not used for influenza. D ­ ribavirin, some evidence of benefit in RSV in immunocompromised patients to prevent progression to LRTI (in particular HSCT, lung transplant recipients).

questions.stem: X-linked hyper IgM syndrome results in...

A defect in CD40 expression on B cells
A defect in CD40L expression on CD4+ T cells

A defect in CD40L expression on B cells

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old man presents with three-week history of paraesthesia in the right hand. He describes onset on waking of dense paraesthesia involving the right fifth finger, medial half of the fourth finger, extending proximately through the medial right hand, including dorsum, to just above the wrist, unchanged since onset. There is no associated weakness.
Where is the likely lesion?

options.text: Ulnar nerve in Guyon's canal at wrist

questions.explanation:
4





C8 radiculopathy and medial cord plexopathy give sensory distribution extending to medial forearm, in distribution of medial cutaneous nerve of forearm. Ulnar neuropathy at elbow gives deficit as described, including the dorsal hand, and proximal medial hand. Sparing of the dorsal surface of ulnar dermatome (dorsal medial hand and two fingers) points to Guyon canal syndrome. This occurs because this region is innervated by the dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch which comes off about 5 cm proximal to the Guyon canal entrance. The hypothenar eminence is also commonly spared because the palmar ulnar cutaneous branch ­ arising proximal to the wrist - is not affected.

The presence of gross ankle oedema

He has not had any exacerbations of COPD in the last 12 months. He is mildly breathless on exertion. Examination records resting oxygen saturations of 87%. His chest is clear to auscultation, with globally reduced breath sounds. There are no clinical features of cor pulmonale. An ABG collected on room air demonstrates the following results.



7





FiO2 pH PaCO2 PaO2 HCO3 Base excess Na K Cl Ca Glucose Lactate Hb FCOHb FMetHb

0.21 7.45 50 53 35 6 137 4.1 105 1.2 5.0 1.5 135 1.5% 0.8%

Normal range
7.35-7.45 35-45mmHg 75-105mmHg 22-28mmol/L -3 to +3 135-145mmol/L 3.7-4.7mmol/L 101-110mmol/L 1.15-1.3mmol/L 3.5-5.4mmol/L 0-2mmol/L 120-150g/L 0.3-1.8% 0.4-1.2%

What is the next most appropriate management option?

Addition of an ICS could be considered if he had suffered from frequent exacerbations (> 2 moderate or 1 severe exacerbation). ICS is therefore not currently indicated. questions.stem:
A young couple from Thailand is seen in haemoglobinopathy clinic for antenatal planning and counselling. They have been referred by their family doctor after screening identified microcytosis (MCV 70 fL) in the woman. Genetic tests were subsequently performed in both individuals which identified the -/- alpha genotype in both the man and the woman.
What is the probability of their child having haemoglobin H disease?
questions.explanation: 0%. Haemoglobin H (HbH) disease is an alpha thalassemia disorder that generally results from deletion of three out of the four alpha gene loci. In contrast, alpha thalassemia trait is caused by deletion of two alpha genes (in cis or trans) while hydrops fetalis is a lifethreatening disorder caused deletion of all four loci. In general, HbH is nontransfusion dependent but is characterised by haemolysis and occasional periods requiring RBC transfusions during stress. The genotype of HbH disease is a deletional --/- (note that nondeletional forms also exist). Alpha thalassemia trait can be caused by either -/- (homozygous two gene deletion) or --/ (heterozygous two gene deletion) genotypes where the two alpha gene deletions can occur in trans or cis, respectively. In this scenario, both the man and woman have the alpha thalassemia trait in trans. The genotype of the child will therefore always be -/-, meaning the child will have a 100% risk of alpha thalassemia trait but 0% risk of hydrops fetalis or HbH disease.

questions.stem:

On examination, he looks well, without fever, headache or any neurological signs. His GCS is 15. He also notices some mild bilateral hand swelling and joint pain. Pertinent investigations include FBE: haemoglobin 159 g/L, platelets 61 x 10^0/L (normal 150-450), lymphocytes 0.8 x 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.6 x 10^9/L, COVID-19 and other respiratory virus PCR negative, blood cultures no growth, CK 846 U/L (normal 45-250). LFTs ALT 85 U/L (normal 5-40), AST 143 U/L (normal 10-40), bilirubin normal. CRP is 1 mg/L. Urea, electrolytes and creatinine are normal. Blood film reveals marked neutropenia and occasional atypical lymphocytes with moderately reduced platelets. On further history, he did have some mosquito bites but did not go swimming and only stayed in his resort.
What is your next best test for diagnosis?

options.text: Thick and thin blood film x 3 and malaria ICT

questions.explanation: This patient has quick onset fever after returning from overseas. Longer incubation periods are usually associated with other infections including malaria or typhoid fever. Dengue should be high on your list of differentials in this setting (fever within 7-10 days of returning home) and can cause a viral induced myositis. The blood film can be very useful in helping to work out etiology of infectious diseases ­ this suggests viral infection. Commonly, LFTs are mildly deranged with dengue (D). Other options ­ (B) there is negligible risk of malaria transmission in the resort areas of Bali, the patient's headache and fever has improved, if persistent, of course a lumbar puncture should be considered (C). https://wwwnc.cdc.gov/travel/yellowbook/2020/preparing-international-travelers/yellowfever-vaccine-and-malaria-prophylaxis-information-by-country/indonesia#seldyfm948

questions.stem: Which of the following is sufficient for diagnosis of epilepsy?

options.text: First seizure on day 6 post right middle cerebral artery ischaemic infarction

One unprovoked seizure and a probability of further seizures similar to the general
recurrence risk after two unprovoked seizures (80 percent) occurring over the next 10 years
Second seizure in 12 months in a 16-year-old male, each in the setting of alcohol intoxication and sleep deprivation
Two unprovoked seizures occurring 3 days apart

questions.explanation:
REF Up to date: evaluation and management of the first seizure in adults
Acute symptomatic seizure -- Acute symptomatic seizure refers to a seizure that occurs at the time of a systemic insult or in close temporal association with a documented brain insult. Such insults include metabolic derangements, drug or alcohol withdrawal, and acute neurologic disorders such as stroke, encephalitis, or acute head injury. The time window within which a seizure may be considered acute symptomatic has not been clearly defined and may vary based on the type of insult. One consensus panel recommendation suggests the following ranges: Within one week of stroke, traumatic brain injury, anoxic encephalopathy, or intracranial surgery At first identification of subdural hematoma During the active phase of a central nervous system infection Within 24 hours of a severe metabolic derangement In population-based studies, acute symptomatic seizures make up to 40 percent of first seizures. Acute symptomatic seizures may recur during the index illness but generally carry a low risk for future epilepsy compared with unprovoked seizures. That said, some patients will go on to develop remote symptomatic seizures or epilepsy related to a prior stroke, hemorrhage, or head injury.
Unprovoked seizure -- Unprovoked seizure refers to a seizure of unknown etiology as well as one that occurs in relation to a preexisting brain lesion or progressive nervous system disorder. Unprovoked seizures that are determined to be due to an underlying brain lesion or disorder are also referred to as remote symptomatic seizures. They carry a higher risk of future epilepsy compared with acute symptomatic seizures.
Epilepsy -- Epilepsy is defined as when any of the following exist [7]: At least two unprovoked seizures occurring more than 24 hours apart. One unprovoked seizure and a probability of further seizures similar to the general recurrence risk after two unprovoked seizures (eg, 60 percent) occurring over the next 10 years. This may be the case with remote structural lesions such as stroke, central nervous system infection, or certain types of traumatic brain injury. Diagnosis of an epilepsy syndrome. The second criterion was added by the International League Against Epilepsy (ILAE) working group in 2014 and emphasizes the importance of neuroimaging and electroencephalography (EEG) in the evaluation of patients with a first-time seizure, as some of these patients will meet criteria for epilepsy at the time of a first seizure.
Alcohol intoxication or withdrawal and drugs of abuse should not be overlooked as a potential cause of seizure. Of note, however, a history of alcohol does not preclude other causes of seizure. In a study of 259 patients with first seizure suspected to be due to alcohol withdrawal, head computed tomography (CT) identified 16 patients (6 percent) with a clinically significant lesion (eg, subdural hematoma), and the presence of altered mental status or focal deficits did not correlate with abnormal head CT results

questions.stem: What is the most common type of breast cancer associated with BRCA2 mutation?
questions.explanation:
This came from a landmark paper from 2011 that analysed 3797 BRCA1 mutation carriers and 2392 BRCA 2 mutation carriers diagnosed with invasive breast cancer17. It shows that a majority proportion of BRCA2 mutation carriers have ER-positive breast cancer.



9





(Adapted from Mavaddat et al, "Pathology of breast and ovarian cancers among BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutation carriers: results from the Consortium of Investigators of Modifiers of BRCA1/2 (CIMBA)", Cancer Epidemiol Biomarkers Prev 201117)

questions.stem: Which of the following is a contra-indication to kidney transplantation?

options.text: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with FEV1 0.75 L in an ex-smoker

questions.explanation: Kidney transplantation is contra-indicated in those with cancer except for early-stage non-melanoma skin cancer, early-stage prostate cancer and superficial bladder cancer. For other cancers, transplantation should not be undertaken until potentially curative therapy has been completed and remission achieved, with consensus between transplant clinicians and oncologists that cure is likely, and that transplantation is justified. Obesity and substance abuse are relative contraindications and should be assessed on a case-by-case basis, but do not necessarily preclude transplantation. COPD with FEV1 < 1.0 is associated with a high risk of mortality post-Tx and is a contraindication to kidney alone transplant.

questions.stem:
Suvorexant and lemborexant are novel medications for management of insomnia.
These medications are contraindicated in which sleep disorder?
questions.explanation: Orexin antagonists block the action of orexin. This is likely to worsen symptoms of narcolepsy. There is currently limited data on safety of orexin antagonists in patients with OSA, however lemborexant appears to be safe and well tolerated in patients with severe OSA. RLS and parasomnia disorders are not contraindications. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4702197/ https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37677076/

questions.stem:
While axial involvement is seen in all patients with ankylosing spondylitis, peripheral joint involvement can also occur.
Which peripheral joint is most commonly affected in patients with ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation:
Although axial involvement is seen in all patients with ankylosing spondylitis, peripheral arthritis is also common in around 30% of patients. The hip is the most common joint affected and is a marker of worse disease / a poorer prognosis.
Reference: Dr Michael Gingold's DeltaMed Lectures Golder V and Schachna L. Ankylosing spondylitis: An update. Australian Family Physician 2013; 42(11): 780 ­ 784.



10

questions.stem: What is the most important side effect to beware of in patients on mavacamten for HCM?
questions.explanation: Mavacamten is a first-in-class drug that acts via myosin inhibition to reduce LVOT gradient in obstructive HCM. As such, it is technically cardiotoxic and frequent echocardiograms are required at initiation of mavacamten. LV dysfunction is an absolute contraindication to commencing mavacamten, and if LV dysfunction develops on mavacamten it must be ceased.

questions.stem: Why is the primary reason for blinding (masking) in clinical trials?
questions.explanation:
If study investigators or subjects are aware of the group to which subjects are allocated in a clinical trial, then it is possible that prejudice about the potential efficacy or otherwise about interventions may affect the onset or assessment of the trial's outcome. Blinding (masking) reduces this bias.
Information bias is that which affects data, while selection bias is that which affects subjects.

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old woman with type with type 2 diabetes is admitted with left foot osteomyelitis and scheduled for surgical debridement the next morning. Her usual insulin regimen is twice daily premixed insulin (Novomix 30) 30 units at breakfast and 20 units at dinner. Recent HbA1c was 8.5%.
What is the best management plan for this patient's hospital stay?

options.text: Continue Novomix 30 but decease the dose by 30% insulin with short acting top up insulin as required
Continue Novomix 30 at current dose insulin with short acting top up insulin as required
Stop Novomix 30 and change to twice daily gliclazide with short acting top up insulin as required
Start daily basal insulin with short acting top up insulin as required and add TDS short acting insulin TDS pre-meals when full died is ordered

questions.explanation: Patients with changing oral intake on the background of poorly controlled insulin requiring diabetes are best managed with basal insulin and a top upscale (and prandial insulin once oral intake is commenced). A+B can lead to hypoglycaemia and premixed insulin is less flexible. C will lead to hyperglycaemia given her established insulin requirement.

questions.stem:
A 78-year-old man with ulcerative colitis is on mesalazine 4.8g daily and optimally dosed azathioprine for over 6 months. His bowels are opened 4-5 times per day with mucus but no blood. A colonoscopy showed moderate severity inflammation with erythema, oedema and reduced vascular markings to the transverse colon. He meets the criteria for escalation of therapy. He has a history of melanoma excised 3 years ago, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction of 40% with NYHA III heart failure and he is also current smoker with a smoking history of over 50 pack years.
Which biologic agent/small molecule is the most suitable for this patient given his comorbidities?
questions.explanation: Vedolizumab is a gut-selective alpha4beta7 integrin and would be the agent of choice for an elderly patient with heart failure at risk of infections and malignancies. Infliximab is associated with an increased melanoma risk. Infliximab and ozanimod can both worsen heart failure NYHA III or IV and are contraindicated. Tofacitinib is associated with an increased risk of lung cancer.

questions.stem: Which of the following mechanisms best describes the way in which oncogenes exert their effect on the cell?
questions.explanation: Oncogenes provide the cell with a growth advantage by enhancing function, for example, a constitutionally active tyrosine kinase receptor. Haploinsufficiency, dominant negative effect and loss of heterozygosity all result in loss of function.

questions.stem: With regards to pharmacological treatment for urinary incontinence, which of the following statements is true?

options.text: Oxybutynin may be associated with an increased risk of dementia

Topical vaginal oestrogen tends to worsen the genitourinary syndrome of menopause
(GSM)

questions.explanation: Anticholinergics more commonly cause TACHYcardia. Anticholinergic antimuscarinic drugs are a/w an increased risk of dementia (OR ~1.65). Topical oestrogen can help with incontinence a/w vaginal atrophy due to GSM. Hypertension is common SE of mirabegron.

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old man is admitted for surgery of a suspected bowel cancer. His FBE and biochemistry tests are normal. The patient does not have any significant past medical history other than the suspected cancer. The screening coagulation studies are shown below.
INR: 1.0 PT: 13 seconds (12-15) APTT: 50 seconds (25-37) Fibrinogen: 2 g/L (1.5-4)
Additional tests are performed including a mixing study and factor levels. The 50:50 mix did not correct the prolonged APTT (mixing resulted in APTT of 48 seconds). Factor assays were performed for factors 8,9,11, and 12 and were all above 50%.
The surgeons are worried about the risk of bleeding during the surgery and ask for your consultation.
What is the most appropriate advice/management?

options.text: Advise that additional factor testing for factors 2, 5, 10 is required

Advise them that she is at increased risk of thrombosis and requires early
commencement of thromboprophylaxis
Reassure them that surgery is safe to proceed
Delay surgery pending lupus anticoagulant testing

questions.explanation: Reassure them that surgery is safe to proceed. This patient has an isolated prolonged APTT that does not correct on mixing. Mixing studies will distinguish whether the problem is due to factor deficiency (prolonged APTT will correct on mixing) or due to an inhibitor (specific factor inhibitor or lupus anticoagulant). The additional tests demonstrates that the patient does not have a specific factor deficiency (low factor activity levels). The most likely reason for prolonged APTT is a lupus anticoagulant which is not uncommon in patients with malignancy. These patients do not have an increased risk in bleeding or thrombosis (above that of malignancy) and should receive normal pharmacologic thromboprophylaxis.

questions.stem: What feature below is a distinct characteristic of airborne transmission compared to droplet spread?

options.text: Direct contact with mucous membrane of recipients

questions.explanation:
Reference ­ 2023 Delta Med Notes, ID overview part 1 ­ A/Prof Leon Worth



11

questions.stem: In an individual with an average risk of cancer, screening of which of the following cancers has been shown to significantly decrease mortality rate?
questions.explanation:
In ovarian cancer ­ a large, randomized trial of women (N=50623) age 50-74 years who did not have increased risk of familial ovarian cancer found that screening with transvaginal ultrasound did not reduce mortality24.
In lung cancer ­ a systematic review and meta-analysis of 7 randomized controlled trials (N=84558) in patients with smoking history of more than 15 pack years revealed a reduction in lung cancer mortality of 17% (RR 0.83, 95% CI 0.76-0.91), with relative reduction of overall mortality of 4% (RR =0.96, 95% CI 0.92-1.00), by screening using low dose CT25. However, there is no evidence in screening an average risk patient without smoking history.
In prostate cancer, 3 largest randomized controlled trials conducted to assess the effect of PSA screening on prostate cancer among men 55 to 69 years of age found at most a small benefit in reducing prostate cancer mortality. Screening should be a shared decision-making between clinicians and patient after assessing the risk and benefits 26. (Reference for reading: NEJM review article on "Screening for Prostate Cancer" https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMcp2209151)
In colorectal cancer, randomized controlled trials of FOBT has shown to reduce colorectal cancer incidence and identifying earlier staged lesions 27. (Reference for reading: AJGP "Colorectal cancer screening in Australia: an update" https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2018/december/colorectal-cancer-screening-inaustralia)

(Adapted from AJGP "Colorectal cancer screening in Australia: an update")



12

questions.stem: The risk of acute kidney transplant rejection is greater...

options.text: With a negative (versus positive) virtual crossmatch
After induction with thymoglobulin rather than basiliximab
In recipients who have had a previous kidney transplant

questions.explanation: Having had a previous transplant typically induces development of anti-HLA antibodies, and PRA is typically high. Having been "sensitized" by the previous organ, the patient will be more likely to develop acute rejection to a subsequent transplant as compared to a patient receiving their first kidney transplant. A negative virtual crossmatch indicates the absence of donor specific antibodies prior to transplant, and hence lower risk of rejection. Thymoglobulin is a more potent induction agent than basiliximab. Acute rejection is most commonly experienced during the first 3 months after transplantation.

questions.stem:
An ABG is collected from a 60-year-old gentleman admitted for further investigation of breathlessness. The results are below.

FiO2 pH PaCO2 PaO2 HCO3 Base excess Na K Cl Ca Glucose Lactate Hb FCOHb FMetHb

0.21 7.43 40 65 26 +2 137 4.2 106 1.1 4.5 0.9 149 1.2% 0.9%

Normal range
7.35-7.45 35-45mmHg 75-105mmHg 22-28mmol/L -3 to +3 135-145mmol/L 3.7-4.7mmol/L 101-110mmol/L 1.15-1.3mmol/L 3.5-5.4mmol/L 0-2mmol/L 120-150g/L 0.3-1.8% 0.4-1.2%

The most likely cause for his breathlessness is...
questions.explanation:
The A-a gradient is calculated according to the following formula: A-a gradient = PAO2 ­ PaO2
PAO2 is estimated from the alveolar gas equation. PAO2 = 150 ­ (PaCO2 x 1.25)
Therefore, the A-a gradient is calculated by... A-a gradient = [150 ­ (PaCO2 x 1.25)] ­ PaO2
The expected A-a gradient for age is calculated according to the following formula... A-a gradient (for age) = (Age + 10) / 4
The A-a gradient is elevated (35) compared to the A-a gradient expected for age (19). The only cause of a wide A-a gradient hypoxaemia in the list above is COPD and emphysema. Metabolic acidosis can immediately be excluded based on the ABG.

questions.stem:
An 80-year-old man presents to hospital with a 2-week history of right sided temporal headache and jaw claudication. While waiting to be seen in the emergency department, they describe a 10-minute episode of right eye visual loss that has now resolved. Laboratory tests show a CRP of 95 and an ESR of 110 mm/Hr.
Patients from which geographic region are at greatest risk of this condition?
questions.explanation:
Please see the reference below.

questions.stem: Which of the following are characteristics of a `vulnerable coronary plaque' most likely to rupture and cause an acute coronary syndrome?
questions.explanation: Options A, C and D are features of a `stable' plaque (see below). Option B is characteristic of a thin-cap fibroatheroma, considered to be a high-risk plaque.

questions.stem:
The following figure was taken from the publication of the `EMPA-REG' study, which randomised 7020 people with type 2 diabetes at high cardiovascular risk to empagliflozin or placebo. (New Engl J Med 2015;373:2117-28.) Death from cardiovascular causes was one of the secondary outcomes of the study.

Over 48 months, what is the best estimate of the number needed to treat for empagliflozin versus placebo in terms of preventing death from cardiovascular causes?
questions.explanation: Number needed to treat (NNT) = 1 / absolute risk difference = 1 / (9%-5%) = 1/4% = 25 25 people need to be treated with empagliflozin over 48 months in order to prevent one cardiovascular death.

questions.stem:
A 22-year-old male presents with hyperglycaemia on routine health check. HbA1c is 7.5%, BMI is 22 kg/m2. There is a history of diabetes in the father, paternal grandfather and a paternal uncle. Insulin autoantibodies are negative, and C-peptide is 500 pmol/L [200-800].
What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Low BMI young age argue against (A). Negative antibodies and preserved C-peptide against (B) and to a certain extent (C). With clinical characteristics and family history suggesting autosomal dominant inheritance his risk of MODY is ~75%. See MODY risk calculator: (https://www.diabetesgenes.org/exeter-diabetes-app)

questions.stem:
A 40-year-old woman with chronic hepatitis B is 20 weeks pregnant. Her e-Antigen status is negative, and she is treatment naïve. Her last fibroscan from two years ago shows a median liver stiffness of 5.6kPa, IQR 0.19. Her latest blood test is below: Bili 7, ALT 20, AST 15, ALP 120, GGT 48, albumin 33
What is the most appropriate next step in her management?

options.text: Start entecavir in the third trimester to reduce the risk of mother to child transmission
Obtain an ultrasound liver and repeat fibroscan to re-assess fibrosis status

Counselling regarding the hepatitis B vaccine and immunoglobulin to be given to the
newborn

Check the hepatitis B viral load in the post-partum period, if the viral load is above
200,000cp/ml, to start tenofovir to prevent mother to child transmission

questions.explanation: The hepatitis B viral should be checked early in the third trimester to determine if treatment is required to prevent mother to child transmission, not in the post-partum period.

questions.stem:
The following pedigree is obtained from a family with Huntington's disease.

Individual A is 40 years old and has developed symptoms of chorea, depression, personality change and some cognitive impairment. His father started to develop symptoms at the age of 50 and his grandmother developed symptoms at the age of 65. Individual B is also 40 years old and remains asymptomatic, but the diagnosis was confirmed on genetic testing. His mother also has the disease but did not develop symptoms til the age of 60.



16





Which of the following best explains why individual B is asymptomatic compared to individual A?

options.text: As a polygenetic disease, individual A is likely to have inherited other genes which modify the expression of the Huntington gene
Individual A is likely to be a homozygote while individual B is likely be a heterozygote
Individual B is likely to develop symptoms within the next 12 months and progress at the same rate as individual A
Intergenerational instability is much greater with paternal inheritance

questions.explanation:
Paternal transmission of a CAG repeat expansion in HD appears to be more unstable that maternal transmission. This means that the offspring of a male CAG repeat expansion carrier are at greater risk of inheriting a further expanded allele resulting in the potential for earlier onset of disease. This phenomenon is known as anticipation. An inherited the expansion from his father, whereas B inherited his expansion from his mother, so A's expansion may well have expanded further to explain his more severe disease and B is likely to have inherited a similar size expansion to his mother and present with less severe disease. HD is not a polygenic disease, although polymorphisms in other genes may modify expression. This remains unknown to date. Homozygous CAG expansions are not thought to cause any earlier onset of disease than heterozygotes. Based on the CAG repeat size, we have no way of predicting if individual B will develop symptoms in the next 12 months.



14

questions.stem:
A 97-year-old man with end-stage Alzheimer dementia has been brought into hospital with reduced level of consciousness. He usually resides in a nursing home, is bedbound and non-verbal.
Thorough examination reveals a large painful sacral pressure ulcer, with likely underlying osteomyelitis. Blood tests reveal multiorgan failure. As he has no surrogate decision-maker, the treating team decide to commence an Endof-life care pathway, with morphine and midazolam charted as needed.
Which of the following ethical principles has been applied in this patient's current care?
questions.explanation: A and D do not apply when there is lack of capacity (and no given care directives or surrogate decision maker). Maleficence is not an ethical principle. NON-maleficence would also be applicable here and the physician's obligation and intention to relieve suffering overrides the foreseen but unintended harmful effects or outcome of (in this case) administering palliative care.

questions.stem:
A 70-year-old man is admitted to the cardiothoracic unit for a bioprosthetic valve replacement. Three days later he developed a right sided iliofemoral DVT and is commenced on unfractionated heparin. His platelet count begins to fall relative to the normal platelet count of 250 x10^9 cells/L he had at the end of surgery. His day 5 results are shown below.
Platelet: 30 x10^9 cells/L INR: 1.1 PT: 13 seconds (12-15) APTT: 70 seconds (25-37) Fibrinogen: 2 g/L (1.5-4)
His 4T score indicates a high probability and HIT testing is sent off.
What is the most appropriate anticoagulant to change the patient to?
questions.explanation: The immediate management is to stop the unfractionated heparin and change anticoagulant therapy. In the acute perioperative period, an infusional anticoagulant with a short life such as bivalirudin is preferred. Another option would be argatroban. Enoxaparin should be avoided due to being a heparin. Warfarin should not be used in the acute period as it can worsen thrombosis and cause skin necrosis (initial fall in protein C). However, warfarin is an option after the platelets have normalised if other anticoagulants are not appropriate. Rivaroxaban can also be used for HITT; however, given the immediate perioperative period in this scenario, a shorter acting agent would be preferred.

questions.stem:
A 53-year-old female from Vietnam is being assessed in the outpatient clinic for aetiology of chronic kidney disease. She has normal liver function tests and no drug allergies and a previous HIV serology in the work up is negative. Her general practitioner has also performed a QuantiFERON gold test which is positive.
What is the next most appropriate step?

options.text: Ignore it and leave for the GP to manage

Counsel the patient and start isoniazid 300mg daily and vitamin B6 25mg daily for 9
months with follow up in 1 month
Check drug-drug interactions and start rifampicin 600mg daily for 4 months
Assess for and exclude active tuberculosis

questions.explanation: Latent TB section in TG, 2020 Other options A ­ she will need specialist follow up if LTBI treatment is started, B and C ­ not until D is performed. If she does not have active TB, either option is probably fine ­ chronic kidney disease can increase risk of TB reactivation, if she is to be immunosuppressed for a kidney transplant then treatment of LTBI becomes more important. There is no recommended dose adjustment for kidney dysfunction for either medication ­ however, it does require close monitoring for AEs (https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJM197307122890208). Equally, you could watch and wait if she does not meet other criteria for consideration of LTBI treatment (recent converter in last 2 years, close contact with smear + pulmonary TB case, HCW, currently immunocompromised).

questions.stem:
A 50-year-old male has an out of hospital cardiac arrest following a bee sting requiring 5 minutes of CPR. He is reviewed in ICU where he is intubated, on vasopressor support and piperacillin/tazobactam for hospital-acquired pneumonia. A serum tryptase measured 48 hours post-admission is elevated at 150ug/L (normal <11.1ug/L). His renal function is normal. On examination the following rash is noted on his trunk.

What is the most likely explanation for his raised tryptase?
questions.explanation: Mastocytosis is the most likely cause for this patient's raised tryptase, which has remained very elevated 48 hours post-anaphylaxis. The rash is also typical of urticaria pigmentosa as is the presentation of anaphylaxis. Tryptase elevation post-mast cell degranulation in IgE-mediated reactions is transient and should normalise by 24 hours (particularly in the context of normal renal function). CPR can increase serum tryptase levels, but it would be unusual for such high levels to persist after 5 minutes of CPR.

questions.stem:
An 88-year-old man presented to the emergency department with acute back pain. He has a significant medical history, including ischemic cardiomyopathy with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 15%, poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus, and is a heavy smoker with more than 100 pack-year history. Over the last 6 months, he has increasingly depended on his family for most activities of daily living, including showering and cooking. A chest x-ray showed a large spiculated mass in the left upper lobe. An urgent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine was performed, revealed the following findings.

What would be the most appropriate next step in managing this case?

options.text: Organise an urgent biopsy of the lesion

questions.explanation:
Spinal metastases are common in cancer, with breast, prostate and lung cancers being the most frequent causes of metastasis to vertebral column. Treatment of malignant spinal cord compression would be either radiotherapy or surgical decompression but are partly palliative. Surgery (laminectomy) is the most rapid method for relief of acute spinal cord compression and is necessary if there is spinal instability, followed by post operation radiation. However, in patients with poor underlying performance status, careful discussion is often required to determine if patient is fit for surgery and the expected neurology recovery. Option for surgery is not included in the options to avoid confusion. In this case of a likely metastatic lung cancer with spinal cord compression, poor performance status/ unlikely surgical candidate due to significant co-morbidities, urgent palliative radiation is recommended.
Figure above adapted from Ropper et al. "Acute Spinal Cord Compression", NEJM review article 201737)



15

questions.stem:
Delayed graft function (DGF) is defined by the requirement for dialysis within 1 week of kidney transplantation due to inadequate function of the transplanted kidney.
Regarding DGF, which of the following statements is correct?



19

options.text: DGF is more frequently seen following living donor transplantation than deceased donor transplantation
DGF is less likely to occur if balanced crystalloids are used for peri-operative IV fluid therapy, rather than normal saline
Fluids containing potassium should be avoided peri-operatively to reduce the risk of hyperkalaemia
25% of patients with DGF will experience transplant failure within 1 year of transplant surgery

questions.explanation: Use of Plasmalyte, a balanced solution containing physiological concentrations of both chloride and potassium, was associated with a 25% reduction in the incidence of DGF as compared to normal saline in a recent multicenter trial conducted in ANZ (Collins M, et al, Lancet 2023). DGF is rare in living donor kidney transplantation, but affects 3040% of deceased donor recipients and >50% if the kidney is from a donor after circulatory determination of death (DCDD). Although DGF is associated with increased risks of acute rejection, prolonged hospitalization, higher costs and reduced long-term outcomes, the vast majority of patients with DGF (>95%) regain transplant function and remain free from dialysis at 12 months.

questions.stem:
A chest x-ray requested for investigation of cough in an 85-year-old gentleman is shown below.

These findings are most likely in keeping with previous exposure to which of the following?
questions.explanation:
This chest x-ray demonstrates multiple calcified irregular pleural plaques, in keeping with previous asbestos exposure. Silica exposure is usually associated with fibrosis (and in some cases, progressive massive fibrosis). Aspergillus and wood fibre exposure do not lead to development of pleural plaques. Pleural plaques can also occur in cases of previous tuberculosis, haemothorax, talc pleurodesis, early mesothelioma, myeloma and rib fractures.
Ref: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5873627/#:~:text=Although%20bilateral %20calcified%20pleural%20plaques,mesothelioma%2C%20myeloma%20and%20rib%2 0fractures. https://radiopaedia.org/articles/pleural-plaque

questions.stem:
Symmetrical deforming polyarthropathy is one of the hallmark features of untreated rheumatoid arthritis.
Which of the following x-ray changes would be most likely to be seen in rheumatoid arthritis?
A. Distal interphalangeal joint sparing B. Juxta articular erosions with a sclerotic margin C. Resorption of the distal phalangeal tips D. Subchondral sclerosis at the MCPs

Extended MCQ
Answer Options for Q69 & Q70

options.text:
Mycophenolate Mofetil F. Rituximab
Lead-in statement: For each of the listed first-line immunosuppressant agents used in neurological disease, select the most likely matching descriptions of mechanism of action and immune consequences.

questions.explanation: The classic x-rays changes seen in rheumatoid arthritis are juxta-articular osteopaenia, peri-articular erosions, soft tissue swelling leading to joint space narrowing. The DIPs are usually spared in rheumatoid arthritis but involved in psoriatic arthritis, gout, scleroderma and osteoarthritis. Juxta-articular erosions with a sclerotic margin are seen in gout. Subchondral sclerosis is a feature of osteoarthritis. Reference: Michelle Leech's DeltaMed lectures.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a well-established anatomical or clinical contraindication to transfemoral TAVI?

options.text: Peripheral arterial disease precluding vascular access
History of coronary artery bypass grafting

questions.explanation: Severe peripheral arterial disease that limits vascular access is a contraindication to transfemoral TAVI. Alternatives like transapical or transaxillary approaches may be considered. While bicuspid valves and low coronary heights are relative challenges, they are not absolute contraindications in contemporary practice.

questions.stem: Some drugs are available as `slow release' or `controlled release' formulations. Such formulations are required because ...

options.text: The drugs are toxic in high doses
The drugs are susceptible to the first pass effect
The half-life of the drugs is too short
The half-life of the drugs is too long

questions.explanation: Drugs with short half-lives need to be given more frequently than those with longer halflives to maintain therapeutic concentrations. Slow-release preparations allow for less frequent dosing of drugs with short half-lives.

questions.stem:
A large clinical trial randomised over 5000 patients with a left ventricular ejection fraction 40%, coronary artery disease, and no atrial fibrillation to either a new anticoagulant or placebo. The primary outcome was the composite of death from any cause, myocardial infarction and stroke. Over a median follow-up period of 20 months, the primary end point occurred in proportionally fewer subjects in the anicoagulant group compared to the placebo group; hazard ratio, 0.94; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.84 to 1.05.
Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: There was 95% confidence that the true hazard ratio lied in the range 0.84 to 1.05
The hazard ratio was statistically significant (at the 0.05 level of significance)
The anticoagulant was superior to placebo in terms of the primary outcome

Compared to placebo, the relative risk reduction for the primary outcome associated
with the anitcoagulant was 6%

questions.explanation: Response A correctly describes the interpretation of a confidence interval. Other responses are incorrect given the non-statistically significant finding.

questions.stem: Which of the following effects of testosterone treatment in men has been observed in randomised clinical trials?

options.text: Increased risk of developing type 2 diabetes mellitus

questions.explanation: Despite beneficial effects of testosterone on bone density, a recent RCT (PMID: 38231621) has reported that testosterone increases fracture risk compared to placebo. Fractures occurred at sites associated with trauma (e.g., ribs) and are most likely due to pleiotropic effects on testosterone on behaviour. Therefore, men at high fracture risk should be treated with osteoporotic drug therapy irrespective of whether they receive testosterone treatment or not. Testosterone has been reported to be neutral for cardiovascular events (no increased risk relative to placebo), to increase haematocrit (and reduce anaemia risk), and to reduce risk of type 2 diabetes mellitus in high-risk men.

questions.stem:
A 57-year-old man with metabolic dysfunction associated steatotic liver disease presents with haematemesis and melaena, his comorbidities include ischaemic heart disease, peripheral vascular disease, type 2 diabetes, stroke and severe COPD.
Which of the below management strategies is the most appropriate for this patient?

options.text: Resuscitation to restore haemodynamic stability, ceftriaxone and terlipressin

Resuscitation to restore haemodynamic stability, tranexamic acid, ceftriaxone and
terlipressin.
Resuscitation to restore haemodynamic stability, lactulose and terlipressin.

Resuscitation to restore haemodynamic stability, ceftriaxone and octreotide.



1

questions.explanation: Octreotide is preferred as terlipressin can cause vasoconstriction and is contraindicated in ischaemic heart disease and should be used with caution in patients with cardiac arrhythmias, cerebral vascular disease, peripheral vascular disease or uncontrolled HTN. It can also cause bronchospasm and dyspnoea, therefore may worsen asthma/COPD.

questions.stem:
A 28-year-old female presents with a history of 2 early miscarriages. Investigation reveals that both miscarriages had Trisomy 21. She undergoes normal chromosome testing via banded karyotype.
What investigation should be performed next, to best investigate the cause of recurrent pregnancies with Trisomy 21?

options.text: Microarray analysis in the female to ensure she carries no chromosomal abnormality that predisposes her to recurrent pregnancies with Trisomy 21
Microarray analysis in the male partner to ensure he carries no chromosomal abnormality that predisposes her to recurrent pregnancies with Trisomy 21
Banded karyotype in the male partner to exclude him carrying a balanced chromosomal translocation involving chromosome 21
Whole genome sequencing in both parents to exclude an underlying genetic cause for their recurrent miscarriages

questions.explanation: The most likely cause here is one of the parents carrying a balanced chromosome translocation involving chromosome 21 that predisposes to recurrent trisomy 21. Karyotype has excluded a translocation in the female. Microarray will not detect a balanced translocation. Whole genome sequencing will not detect a translocation either. What's missing here is whether the father carries a balanced translocation. This is best assessed via karyotype in him.

questions.stem: Which of the following clinical features is most characteristic of Dementia with Lewy Bodies (DLB) and helps distinguish it from Alzheimer's disease?
questions.explanation: Explanation: REM sleep behaviour disorder (vivid dreams, dream enactment) is a core clinical feature of DLB and often occurs early in the disease course, helping differentiate it from Alzheimer's disease.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a feature of the innate immune response?

options.text: Receptors diversity is generated by somatic mutation
Recognition of foreign structures is via molecular motifs present on microorganisms

questions.explanation:
Recognition of foreign structures is via pathogen associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) and damage associated molecular patterns (DAMPs). Innate immune responses are rapid (A incorrect), does not have memory (D incorrect) and recognition receptors are encoded in the germline (C incorrect).



1

questions.stem: Which of the following is true regarding syphilis serology?

options.text: Treponemal tests (e.g., TPPA or TPHA) indicate disease activity and are used for treatment monitoring, while non-treponemal tests (e.g., RPR) confirm the diagnosis and typically remain positive for life
A negative test prior to pregnancy is adequate to ensure that the risk of congenital syphilis is eliminated
False negative results can occur early after acquisition of syphilis and require either repeat testing or using alternative tests such as PCR to make the diagnosis

Failure to see the serological titre decline within six months of therapy indicates treatment failure and the need to re-treat with higher dose intravenous therapy



2

questions.explanation: Serology is not always positive early on when the primary chancre first appears. Answer A is the wrong way around. Answer B is wrong as syphilis can be acquired when pregnant so repeat testing throughout pregnancy is indicated, especially in higher risk mothers. Answer D is wrong as the RPR serology can take longer than six months to fall, especially if the infection has been present for years.

questions.stem:
A 65-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled hypertension presents after a newonset seizure. Witnesses report bilateral tonic extension of the arms followed by clonic jerking, lasting one minute. He was confused for several minutes afterward. No aura was noted. MRI shows a small infarct in the left frontal cortex, and EEG reveals epileptiform activity localized to the left frontal region.
Which seizure classification best describes this episode?
questions.explanation: This is a focal onset seizure with secondary generalisation, supported by unilateral imaging and EEG. No clear generalised epilepsy features are present. · B. Generalised tonic-clonic seizure ­ would be symmetric, typically without a focal lesion. · C. Myoclonic seizure: these are brief jerks without tonic-clonic evolution. · D. Unknown onset tonic-clonic seizure: onset is known (left frontal on imaging and EEG). Reference Scheffer IE et al. ILAE classification of the epilepsies: Position paper of the ILAE Commission for Classification and Terminology. Epilepsia. 2017;58(4):512­521.

questions.stem:
Kevin is a 55-year-old gentleman with metastatic melanoma, currently receiving first-line treatment with ipilimumab and nivolumab. He has no significant past medical history. He presented to the emergency department with increasing confusion, one week after his third cycle of immunotherapy. His wife reports a gradual decline in cognition over the past three days, initially with poor memory recall, now progressing to an inability to communicate meaningfully. On examination, he opens his eyes to speech, has inappropriate verbal responses, and localises to pain. He is afebrile, with normal blood pressure and heart rate. A septic screen was negative. MRI of the brain revealed no evidence of metastatic disease. You suspect autoimmune encephalitis secondary to immunotherapy.
What is the most appropriate agent to initiate in this scenario?
questions.explanation:
It is important to recognise an immune toxicity. For patients receiving double agent immunotherapy, the risk of immune toxicity also increases compared to single agent immunotherapy. The estimated incidence of neurological immune related adverse effects (irAEs) is approximately 1-5%, with time of onset varies from 6-13 weeks. A range of neurological irAEs have been described, including irAEs involving the central nervous system (CNS; encephalitis and aseptic meningitis) and those involving the peripheral nervous system (acute immune demyelinating polyneuropathy, chronic immune demyelinating polyneuropathy, cranial nerve neuropathies, myasthenic syndromes and myositis). Methylprednisolone at 1-2mg/kg is the first-choice agent to use in grade 3 to 4 irAEs. For grade 2 toxicity oral prednisolone is sufficient.

Reference Management of toxicities from immunotherapy: ESMO Clinical Practice Guideline for diagnosis, treatment and follow-up. Haanen, J. et al. Annals of Oncology, Volume 33, Issue 12, 1217 - 1238



2

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old male with diabetes mellitus and hypertension is on peritoneal dialysis for end stage renal disease. He presents with cloudy dialysate, abdominal pain, and fever.
What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer intraperitoneal antibiotics and initiate peritoneal dialysis catheter exchange
Administer intravenous antibiotics and obtain peritoneal dialysate culture
Administer intraperitoneal antibiotics and obtain peritoneal dialysate culture
Discontinue peritoneal dialysis and start haemodialysis immediately

questions.explanation: Intraperitoneal antibiotics are the preferred treatment for peritoneal dialysis-associated peritonitis. The next step is also to obtain a peritoneal dialysate culture to guide targeted therapy based on the specific causative organism.

questions.stem:
A 67-year-old man with severe COPD (FEV 35% predicted) has frequent exacerbations despite maximal inhaled therapy including a LAMA, LABA, and ICS. He has had two hospital admissions in the past year. He is a non-smoker and adherent to therapy.
Which of the following biologic agents would be potentially beneficial in reducing the frequency of exacerbations?
questions.explanation:
Dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor) has shown significant reduction in exacerbations in eosinophilic COPD in recent studies. Other biologics have had mixed or modest results in COPD compared to their efficacy in asthma.
Ref: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMoa2303951

questions.stem: Which histopathological pattern is generally considered the most common in Connective Tissue Disease-associated Interstitial Lung Disease (CTD-ILD), with the exception of Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA)?
questions.explanation:
NSIP is the most common histopathological pattern found in CTD-ILD, with the notable exception of Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA), where Usual Interstitial Pneumonia (UIP) predominates. While other patterns like Organizing Pneumonia (OP) and Diffuse Alveolar Damage (DAD) can occur, they are not identified as the generally most common pattern across CTD-ILD types.
Reference Hong, M., Veitch, E., & Limaye, S. (2025). Connective tissue disease-associated interstitial lung disease: an approach to treatment amidst an expanding evidence base. Internal Medicine Journal, 55, 885­894.

questions.stem:
Bruno is a 50-year-old male who presents with progressive dyspnoea and orthopnoea. Echocardiography reveals left ventricular dilation with reduced systolic function (LVEF 30%). Coronary angiography shows normal coronary arteries.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: DCM is characterized by ventricular dilation and impaired systolic function in the absence of significant coronary artery disease or other identifiable causes. It commonly presents with heart failure symptoms and reduced LVEF. In a 50-year-old presenting with new-onset DCM, a full family history is very important, and investigations such as cardiac MRI and genetic testing would typically be offered.

questions.stem:
A 100kg man is given a single IV dose of 100mg of Drug X. The volume of distribution is 0.5L/kg. One hour later, the plasma concentration is 1.0mg/L. Two hours later, the plasma concentration is 0.5mg/L.
What is the best estimate of the clearance of Drug X?
questions.explanation: Clearance = (Vd*0.693)/t1/2 (Volume of plasma cleared of drug per unit time.) Vd for Drug X = 50L t1/2 for Drug X = 1hr

questions.stem: Which of the following is a source of information bias in clinical trials?

options.text: Lack of standardised, objective criteria to define outcomes

questions.explanation: Information bias in clinical trials occurs if outcome data are collected differently between or among the groups being compared. This leads to the possibility that the outcome is more likely to be assessed to have occurred in one group compared to the other group(s).

questions.stem:
A 68-year-old woman presents with a fractured right neck of femur after tripping on an uneven footpath. She reports breaking her left wrist after a fall 12 months prior. She has a history of premature menopause (aged 30) following bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy but has never been on hormone replacement therapy. She also had received repeated courses of oral glucocorticoids for asthma but, for the past decade, has been well managed on inhaled glucocorticoids. Metabolic bone screen including PTH and coeliac serology is normal and serum 25-hydroxy-vitamin D is 75 nmol/L. DXA shows T-scores: lumbar spine -3.8, femoral neck -3.0. She had a left below knee amputation for a `bone tumour' 20 years ago the details of which are unclear, and previous renal stones. She is otherwise well without any additional past medical history, and there is no history or clinical features suggesting cardiovascular disease.



4





Which is the best initial treatment for her osteoporosis?
questions.explanation:
Anabolic therapy should be strongly considered as first line therapy in people at very high risk of fractures and are recommended by current guidelines. Romosozumab is now PBS listed as initial therapy for patients at high risk of fractures. To qualify for PBS subsidy, the patient must have a bone mineral density (BMD) T-score of -2.5 or less, AND the patient must have had a symptomatic fracture due to minimal trauma, AND the patient must have had at least 1 hip or symptomatic vertebral fracture in the previous 24 months; OR the patient must have had at least 2 fractures including 1 symptomatic new fracture in the previous 24 months. Cardiovascular disease is a contraindication. A 12month course needs to be followed with an anti-resorptive to consolidate gains. History of a bone tumour constitutes a contraindication for the anabolic agent teriparatide, as would be renal stones especially if related to hypercalciuria. While head-to-head trials especially for fracture outcomes are lacking, teriparatide is likely weaker than romosozumab and has only evidence for vertebral fractures. Topical oestradiol is less appealing as > 10 years of menopause and in any case needs concomitant progestins



3





in women with a uterus to prevent endometrial hyperplasia. She is relatively young for denosumab, and it is a pure antiresorptive, also needs an exit strategy.

questions.stem: What is the best measurement of nutritional status in patients with cirrhosis?
questions.explanation: Hand grip strength is a functional measure of arm/upper body strength. A reduction in muscle strength precedes poor physical performance or loss of muscle mass. Hand grip strength responds rapidly to changes in nutrition status. Values are indicative of sarcopenia (<18kg for women and <26kg for men). BMI does not distinguish between muscle and adipose tissue, as sarcopenic obesity can exist. Albumin is affected by liver function and inflammation. Subjective global assessment has no objective measure of muscle mass or strength and is not sensitive to change.

questions.stem: An autosomal recessive condition has an incidence of 1 in 6,400. What is the carrier frequency for the condition in the general population?
questions.explanation: Incidence = 1 in 6,400 Mutated allele = q, wildtype allele = p. So, q x q (q2) = 1 in 6,400, so q = 1 in 80. Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium states that the frequency of carriers (pq) is double the frequency of wildtype and mutated alleles (p: 2pq: q). Also: p + q = 1. As q is very small, p very close to 1. So, the carrier frequency is 2 x q = 1/80 x 2 = 1 in 40.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a typical change in pulmonary function with ageing?
questions.explanation: Explanation: Ageing is associated with reduced elastic recoil, leading to increased air trapping and higher residual volume. Vital capacity decreases, chest wall compliance declines, and dead space increases.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with bruising. FBE shows Hb 120 g/L, WCC 4 x10^9/L, and platelets 22 x10^9/L. The film is unremarkable. Coagulation profile is normal. LDH is normal. There is no splenomegaly. What is the most likely cause?
questions.explanation: ITP is common in SLE and results from autoantibodies directed at platelet glycoproteins (often anti-GPIIb/IIIa). There are no features to suggest a microangiopathic process such as TTP given the normal blood film and LDH.

questions.stem: Which of the following pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) is found on bacteria?
questions.explanation: A is correct because lipopolysaccharide is typically found on gram negative bacteria and can result in shock. B, D are seen in fungal pathogens. C is seen with viral pathogens.

questions.stem:
A 43-year-old female with relapsed B cell lymphoma is receiving Chimeric Antigen Receptor T-cell therapy. 48 hours after the CAR T-cells are infused, she develops fevers, rigors, headaches, myalgias, hypotension and hypoxia. Blood tests (including cultures) are obtained and show ferritin 2950 mg/L (RR 20 ­ 150 mg/L), CRP 481 mg/L (RR < 5 mg/L), LDH 875 U/L (RR 120 ­ 250 U/L), procalcitonin 2.0 micrograms/L (RR < 0.05 micrograms/L). Chest X-ray does not show any consolidation, but she does have small pleural effusions.
Which of the following is most correct?

options.text: The highly elevated ferritin indicates haemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis is the diagnosis and therapy with etoposide should be started

Even though this is a classic picture of cytokine release syndrome (CRS), no test can reliably differentiate this from sepsis, so she should be managed with broad-spectrum antibiotics as well as either anti-IL-6 therapy (tocilizumab) or corticosteroids
Procalcitonin and CRP levels can help guide whether this is more likely to be febrile neutropenia versus CRS
Therapy for CRS should only be commenced when treatment for sepsis has been started and there is neutrophil recovery

questions.explanation: Correct answer is B (but other differentials such as tumour lysis syndrome need to be considered too). Answer A is "less correct" as although HLH may be present and may be caused by CAR T-cell therapy, you would not be jumping in with etoposide quickly. Answer C is wrong as neither procalcitonin nor CRP can reliably differentiate CRS and sepsis. Answer D is wrong as treatment for CRS should not be delayed for that time period, as neutrophil recovery can take at least a week (or even over a month) and failure to treat for CRS in that time is likely to worsen the outcome.

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old woman was diagnosed with an unprovoked deep venous thrombosis (DVT). She is commenced on apixaban therapy. She is reviewed in clinic, where it is noted that her coagulation profile (taken at the time of presentation prior to apixaban) demonstrated a prolonged APTT that did not correct with mixing. Subsequent testing identified a positive anti-beta2 glycoprotein and anti-cardiolipin antibodies.
What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Add in aspirin in addition apixaban

questions.explanation:
This patient has antiphospholipid syndrome (APLS) and meets the revised Sapporo criteria (strictly speaking, the positive antibodies need to be confirmed 12 weeks apart). It is likely this patient has triple positive APLS (positive for both antibodies, and lupus anticoagulant). In this situation, the LAC is likely to be positive suggested by the APTT that does not correct with mixing. However, it is possible to test for LAC in the presence of apixaban therapy. The optimal management for triple positive APLS is warfarin. Rivaroxaban has been demonstrated to be inferior to warfarin in APLS patients with triple positive disease, arterial thromboses, and microvascular manifestations. In practice, this extends to all DOACs. The clinical situation where you could consider DOAC is in a patient with low risk APLS (DVT only, single or double antibody positivity).



4

questions.stem:
A 46-year-old Aboriginal man presents after a minor ischaemic stroke. He has untreated hypertension, a 20 pack-year smoking history, and drinks 4­5 standard drinks daily. His fasting glucose is 7.1 mmol/L. His father had a stroke at age 50. You discuss long-term prevention and modifiable risks with him.
Which of the following modifiable risk factors is most strongly associated with stroke in younger adults (age <50) in the Australian context?
questions.explanation:
Among younger adults, hypertension is the strongest and most consistently associated modifiable risk factor for both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke. It is often undiagnosed or undertreated in this age group, especially among Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander populations. · A. Diabetes mellitus ­ Also important but has a stronger association with coronary artery disease than early-onset stroke. · B. Dyslipidaemia ­ Contributes to atherogenesis but has a smaller independent effect on early stroke risk. · D. Obstructive sleep apnoea ­ Increasingly recognised, but less well established as a dominant risk in young-onset stroke in the Australian context.
Regarding smoking: o Highly prevalent in young stroke populations, especially in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander communities. o A potent modifiable risk factor, particularly for ischaemic stroke, due to its effects on vascular inflammation, atherogenesis, and thrombosis. o Often co-occurring with other risks (e.g. hypertension, alcohol use), making it difficult to isolate its independent contribution in some datasets.
Would smoking ­ if an option ­ be the correct answer in this case? In large-scale, multivariable-adjusted analyses (e.g. INTERSTROKE), hypertension consistently emerges as the strongest independent modifiable risk factor, even in younger age groups. Smoking is next in line or closely follows, but the statistical strength of association for hypertension is higher.
Reference Kilian A et al. Epidemiology and risk factors for stroke in young adults: A regional perspective. Med J Aust. 2022. O'Donnell MJ et al. Global and regional effects of potentially modifiable risk factors associated with acute stroke in 32 countries (INTERSTROKE): a case-control study. Lancet. 2016.

questions.stem:
A 62-year-old woman presents with abdominal bloating and altered bowel habits. CT imaging reveals a large pelvic mass involving the left adnexa and segmental thickening of the sigmoid colon. Tumour markers show an elevated carcinoembryonic antigen



6





(CEA), while CA-125 is within normal limits. Colonoscopy reveals a suspicious sigmoid lesion, and biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma.
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Primary epithelial ovarian carcinoma with bowel involvement
Primary colorectal adenocarcinoma with ovarian metastasis
Metastatic breast cancer involving ovary and colon

questions.explanation: An adnexal mass with elevated CEA and normal CA-125, especially with colonic wall thickening, suggests a gastrointestinal primary with ovarian metastasis (Krukenberg tumour). Colorectal cancer is the most likely source. Primary ovarian cancer typically presents with elevated CA-125 and peritoneal involvement.

questions.stem:
A 55-year-old male with stage 4 chronic kidney disease presents for routine follow-up.
His recent laboratory results are as follows: Serum phosphate: 2.2 mmol/L (0.8­1.5 mmol/L) Corrected calcium: 2.38 mmol/L (2.2­2.6 mmol/L) Parathyroid hormone (PTH): 30 pmol/L (1-7 pmol/L)
Which of the following interventions is most appropriate to manage his phosphate levels?

options.text: Start calcium carbonate 600mg TDS with meals
Start calcitriol 0.25microg three times per week

questions.explanation: Starting a phosphate binder is the first step in managing phosphate and reducing PTH. Calcitriol and cinacalcet are used for PTH control but are not first-line for phosphate reduction.

questions.stem:
A 25-year-old non-smoker with poorly controlled asthma and frequent exacerbations requiring multiple courses of oral prednisolone presents for review. He is currently taking budesonide/formoterol 400/12 microg 2 puffs twice daily, montelukast 10 mg daily, tiotropium Respimat 2.5microg 2 puffs daily and salbutamol 100 microg 2­4 puffs PRN. He reports persistent daytime and nocturnal symptoms, frequent reliever use, and limitation in daily activities despite good adherence and correct inhaler technique.
Spirometry demonstrates a moderate obstructive ventilatory defect with significant bronchodilator reversibility. Measurement of the fraction of exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) is 65 ppb (< 25 ppb). His blood eosinophil count (off prednisolone) is 0.7 × 10/L. Total serum IgE is 35 IU/mL (< 100 IU/mL), with negative specific IgE to common aeroallergens.
What is the next most appropriate step in management?

options.text: Addition of low dose prednisolone 5mg daily
Addition of theophylline SR 200 mg daily

questions.explanation:
This patient has severe asthma with frequent exacerbations and persistent symptoms despite high-dose inhaled corticosteroids (ICS), long-acting beta-agonist (LABA), and montelukast, with good adherence and inhaler technique. He demonstrates markers of type 2 (T2) inflammation, including elevated FeNO (65 ppb) and peripheral blood eosinophilia (0.7 × 10/L), despite being off oral corticosteroids. These features suggest a steroid-responsive, eosinophilic phenotype, which is likely to respond to biologic therapy targeting the IL-5 pathway.
Benralizumab is an anti­IL-5 receptor monoclonal antibody that reduces eosinophilic inflammation by inducing apoptosis of eosinophils and basophils via antibody-dependent



5





cell-mediated cytotoxicity. It is indicated in patients with severe eosinophilic asthma and frequent exacerbations who remain symptomatic despite high-dose ICS-LABA therapy.
Further increasing ICS (option A) or adding theophylline (option D) is less effective and not preferred given the option of a biologic agent. There is no role for low dose prednisolone.

questions.stem: What is the generally recommended initial pharmacological treatment approach for psoriatic arthritis (PsA) according to recent guidelines?

options.text: Immediate use of biologic drugs like TNF inhibitors for all patients
A "step-up" approach, starting with simple therapies and escalating if needed
Oral steroids as a primary treatment to rapidly settle inflammatory disease

Methotrexate as the sole and definitive therapy for all patterns of PsA



7

questions.explanation:
A "step-up" approach, starting with simple therapies and escalating if needed. Both the European League Against Rheumatism (EULAR) and the Group for Research and Assessment of Psoriasis and Psoriatic Arthritis (GRAPPA) updated recommendations from 2015 broadly suggest a "step up" approach to therapy. This strategy involves using therapies sequentially, beginning with simple options such as non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) or topical psoriasis treatments, progressing to single disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drugs (DMARDs), then combinations of standard DMARDs, and finally biologic drugs if patients do not respond to previous treatments. Oral steroids are generally "not recommended" due to the possibility of skin 'rebound' upon withdrawal. While methotrexate is the most common first-line DMARD, it is part of this sequential approach, not a sole definitive therapy for all PsA patterns. Biologics are typically reserved for patients who have failed to respond to at least two standard DMARDs.
Reference Coates, L. C., & Helliwell, P. S. (2017). Psoriatic arthritis: state of the art review. Clinical Medicine, 17(1), 65­70.

questions.stem:
Jack, a 55-year-old man, is undergoing CPR in ED for cardiac arrest due to VF. After 3 defibrillation attempts at 200 J biphasic, 3 rounds of adrenaline, and one dose of amiodarone, VF persists. Your team prepares for dual sequential defibrillation.
Which of the following is an essential safety consideration before proceeding?

options.text: Verifying both defibrillators deliver shocks simultaneously to prevent competing rhythms
Placing both sets of pads in identical anterior-lateral positions
Administering lidocaine prior to the DSD attempt

questions.explanation: For DSD to be effective and safe, both shocks should be delivered nearly simultaneously (within milliseconds) to avoid asynchronous depolarizations that could worsen arrhythmias or cause injury.

questions.stem:
The concentration-time curve for Drug X is illustrated below, after a single 10mg dose is administered intravenously.

Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: The elimination of Drug X follows linear kinetics
The elimination of Drug X follows first-order kinetics
The concentration of Drug X is not predictably changed by changing its dose and timing
A loading dose of Drug X would normally be required

questions.explanation: The elimination of Drug X follows zero-order kinetics, meaning a proportional relationship between concentration and dose/timing is not present (as opposed to drugs that follow firstorder elimination kinetics). NB: the term `linear kinetics' refers to first-order kinetics, referring to the proportional relationship between concentration and dose/timing.

questions.stem:
100 people with osteosarcoma were selected for a study and matched 1:2 to 200 people without osteosarcoma. The study compared the 2 groups in terms of previous exposure to suspected risk factors for osteosarcoma.
What type of study is this?
questions.explanation: Case-control studies first recruit people with a condition of interest (cases) and a matched group without the condition of interest (controls). Prior exposure to risk factors are then compared between cases and controls.

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of hypoparathyroidism?
questions.explanation: More than 80% are due to previous neck surgery; conventional treatment is with calcium and calcitriol; palopegteriparatide has been approved in Australia but is not yet on the PBS.

questions.stem:
A 33-year-old man with ulcerative colitis presents to the emergency department with 8 bloody bowel actions per day for the past 3 days. He is managed on mesalazine 4.8g PO daily. His heart rate was 90bpm and abdomen was soft and non-tender. Bloods showed a haemoglobin of 105, white cell count of 4.3 and CRP of 37.
What is the most appropriate next step in the management?

options.text: AXR, surgical review, flexible sigmoidoscopy, high dose steroids and enoxaparin for DVT prophylaxis
AXR, surgical review, flexible sigmoidoscopy, high dose steroids and CT abdomen to assess for microperforations
AXR, surgical review, flexible sigmoidoscopy, high dose steroids and DVT prophylaxis with TED stockings instead of enoxaparin as the patient is actively bleeding
AXR, surgical review, flexible sigmoidoscopy, high dose steroids and aspirin for DVT prophylaxis

questions.explanation: Acute severe ulcerative colitis is a pro-thrombotic state and DVT prophylaxis is indicated even if the patient is having bloody bowel actions.

questions.stem:
