A karyotype of a genetic condition is shown below.

Which of the following phenotypic features would you expect this person to have?
questions.explanation:
This is Turner syndrome (45, X0), characterised by short stature, ovarian dysgenesis, infertility, heart and renal defects, with normal intellect.



6

questions.stem: In elderly patients, which of the following is a known risk factor for developing late-onset depression?
questions.explanation: Left hemispheric strokes, particularly in the frontal and basal ganglia regions, are associated with post-stroke depression, often presenting as late-onset depression. While female gender and childhood trauma are risk factors for depression in general, left-sided cerebrovascular events are particularly linked to late-life depression.

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old man is diagnosed with acute myeloid leukaemia. He had previously received adjuvant chemotherapy for colorectal cancer 4 years earlier. His cytogenetic analysis reveals a complex karyotype and deletion of chromosome 5q. Bone marrow morphology shows trilineage dysplasia with 4% blasts.
Which of the following is most consistent with the pathogenesis of his disease?

options.text: FLT3 internal tandem duplication following prior radiotherapy

questions.explanation: Therapy-related AML (t-AML) can be broadly divided into two categories based on the type of prior treatment. Alkylating agents and radiation typically lead to t-AML 5 years after exposure and are associated with complex karyotypes, including monosomy 5, monosomy 7, or deletion 5q/7q, and preceding myelodysplasia. Topoisomerase II inhibitors (e.g. etoposide) are associated with balanced translocations involving genes like MLL (KMT2A) at 11q23, and onset occurs earlier (within 1­3 years) without a preceding MDS. This patient's history and cytogenetics are consistent with alkylating agent-related t-AML.

questions.stem: Defects in oxidative burst can cause which clinical immunodeficiency?
questions.explanation: Oxidative burst is used by neutrophils to destroy pathogens, and deficiency of this pathway results in chronic granulomatous disease. CVID, Hyper IgE and SCID are forms of T/B cell defects, and those cells do not use an oxidative burst.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism of action of the antibiotic Colistin?

options.text: Binds to a site on the bacterial 23S ribosomal RNA of the 50S subunit, preventing the formation of a functional 70S initiation complex and interfering with translation
Binds to lipopolysaccharides and phospholipids leading to disruption in outer cell membrane, leakage and cell death
Binds to cell membrane and inhibits DNA, RNA, protein synthesis

questions.explanation: Binds to lipopolysaccharides and phospholipids leading to disruption in outer cell membrane, leakage and cell death

questions.stem:
A 72-year-old retired schoolteacher with a 10-year history of Parkinson's disease is brought to the emergency department by his wife. Over the past 10 days, he has become increasingly agitated in the evenings and describes seeing "children in the house," (which his wife confirms are not real). He was started on pramipexole 2 weeks ago due to worsening motor fluctuations. On examination, he is alert, cooperative, oriented to time and place, and performs well on a digit span test and serial sevens. Blood work, urinalysis, and chest X-ray are unremarkable.
Which of the following features best distinguishes this presentation from delirium?
questions.explanation:
· Preserved attention ­ In delirium, impaired attention is a core diagnostic feature. Preserved attention in the context of visual hallucinations and alertness suggests dopaminergic psychosis, not delirium. Incorrect options: · A. Agitation ­ Occurs in both delirium and PD psychosis; not distinguishing. · B. Insight into hallucinations ­ Can vary in both conditions and is often lost in PD psychosis. · D. Visual hallucinations ­ Common in both; particularly well-formed in PD psychosis, but not exclusive.
Reference Aarsland D, et al. Parkinson disease-associated psychosis: epidemiology, pathophysiology, and management. Nat Rev Neurol. 2020;16(9):479­497.

questions.stem:
Germline mutations in BRCA1 and BRCA2 are associated with an increased risk of several solid organ malignancies.
Which of the following cancers is most strongly associated with BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations?
questions.explanation:
BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations are best known for increasing the risk of breast and ovarian cancers. However, they are also associated with an elevated risk of other solid tumours, most notably pancreatic and prostate cancers. Colorectal, lung, renal, and gastroesophageal cancers have not been strongly linked to BRCA mutations in the general population.
Reference UpToDate: "Cancer risks in BRCA1/2 carrier"

questions.stem: Which of the following statements is true regarding the relationship between chronic kidney disease and cardiovascular disease?

options.text: Chronic kidney disease is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease

Chronic kidney disease is a risk factor for cardiovascular disease due to the increased
prevalence of hypertension

Chronic kidney disease is not associated with cardiovascular disease until a patient
reaches stage 3 chronic kidney disease

Chronic kidney disease is not associated with cardiovascular disease until a patient
reaches stage 4 chronic kidney disease

questions.explanation:
Chronic kidney disease is an independent risk factor for cardiovascular disease.



7

questions.stem:
A 72-year-old retired office worker presents with progressive dyspnoea and dry cough over 6 months. He does not report any infective symptoms or weight loss. He reports keeping a number of cockatiels in an indoor aviary at home. He is a never smoker and does not consume other recreational substances. On examination, there are fine inspiratory crackles. Serological testing identifies antibodies to avian antigens. Baseline lung function is shown below.

A representative image from his CT chest is shown below:



11





Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence long-term (eg: 12 months) prednisolone with a gradual wean thereafter
Recommend antigen avoidance with close monitoring of lung function and symptoms

questions.explanation:
The likely diagnosis is hypersensitivity pneumonitis. For patients with mild or intermittent symptoms, and minimal to no abnormalities on pulmonary function tests (PFTs), avoidance of the culprit antigen will usually lead to resolution of symptoms and lung function abnormalities. For patients with persisting symptoms, reduced lung function, and diffuse lung disease on highresolution computed tomography (HRCT), but no evidence of infection, in addition to antigen avoidance, a course of oral glucocorticoids can be considered, based on limited evidence that therapy will shorten the duration of symptoms. This is usually tapered over a number of weeks. A 12-month initial course would not be appropriate in the first instance. Maintenance therapy is rarely required, usually only when a patient is unable to avoid the culprit antigen.
Ref: https://publications.ersnet.org/content/errev/31/163/210169#s6

questions.stem: Which of the following autoantibodies is described as highly specific for SLE, despite having low sensitivity?
questions.explanation:
Anti-Sm antibodies are specifically highlighted as being highly specific for Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE). This article describes that anti-Sm reactivity is not described in other diseases, making them a very specific marker for SLE. However, their sensitivity is low, meaning they are only detectable in a smaller percentage of SLE patients (e.g., 20% in white patients, 30­ 40% in black and Asian patients)
Reference Cozzani, E., Drosera, M., Gasparini, G., & Parodi, A. (2014). Review Article Serology of Lupus Erythematosus: Correlation between Immunopathological Features and Clinical Aspects. Autoimmune Diseases, 2014, Article ID 321359. http://dx.doi.org/10.1155/2014/321359

questions.stem: You are at Chadstone Shopping Centre when you see a man collapse, and you begin CPR immediately. His wife tells you he has a defibrillator in place, but he remains unresponsive. As you start CPR, he receives a shock from the ICD but he remains unresponsive. Which of the following is the best course of action?

options.text: Immediately stop CPR to prevent interference with the ICD
Apply a magnet over the ICD to disable defibrillation

Continue CPR, avoiding direct contact with the ICD site, and proceed with
defibrillation if indicated
Administer antiarrhythmic drugs and reassess rhythm in 30 minutes

questions.explanation: If an ICD is firing during CPR, it likely indicates a persistent shockable rhythm. Continue CPR and manage according to usual protocols. Avoid direct pressure on the ICD but chest compressions remain safe and necessary. External defibrillation can still be applied if indicated.

questions.stem: Which is the most common cause of hirsutism in premenopausal women?
questions.explanation:
B accounts for 80-90%, (A) for 5-10% and (C) depending on ethnicity for 1-10%. (D) is rare.



8

questions.stem: Which of the following is an extraintestinal manifestation of Crohn's disease that is associated with disease activity?
questions.explanation: Erythema nodosum may worsen with increased intestinal inflammation.

questions.stem:
A family tree is shown below for a condition.
!



!!

!!!
Which of the following best explains the inheritance of the condition seen in this family tree?
questions.explanation: X-linked Inheritance

questions.stem: Which of the following is the primary pathological hallmark of Alzheimer's disease?
questions.explanation: Alzheimer's disease is characterized by extracellular beta-amyloid plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein.

questions.stem:
A 29-year-old man presents with several weeks of painless cervical lymphadenopathy and intermittent night sweats. Excision biopsy of a lymph node reveals nodular sclerosis classical Hodgkin lymphoma. PET-CT staging demonstrates bilateral cervical (2 nodal sites) and mediastinal involvement without infra-diaphragmatic disease. The mediastinal involvement is not bulky. He has no weight loss, fever, or other systemic symptoms. ESR is 18 mm/hr and serum LDH is normal.
What is the most appropriate initial treatment approach?
questions.explanation: This patient has early-stage (stage II) classical Hodgkin lymphoma. He does not have B symptoms, bulky disease, and high ESR and would be staged as favourable stage II. Standard first-line treatment for early-stage Hodgkin lymphoma is ABVD chemotherapy (adriamycin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine). R-CHOP is not used in Hodgkin lymphoma. Radiotherapy alone is inadequate due to the systemic nature of the disease. One of the goals of Hodgkin lymphoma treatment is limit long term side effects. For this patient with favourable early-stage disease, treatment would be limited cycles of ABVD (2 cycles) followed by radiation. This contrasts with more unfavourable or advanced disease where more chemotherapy cycles are required.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true regarding innate lymphoid cells (ILCs)?

options.text: Helper ILCs sense `distress signals' and produce type 1, 2 or 3 cytokines
ILCs develop from the common myeloid progenitor
ILCs have specific antigen receptors developed through VDJ recombination

ILCs require exposure to IL-1 for development and maintenance



13

questions.explanation: ILCs are generated in the bone marrow (and foetal liver), do not have specific receptors (VDJ recombination occurs in B/T cells). ILCs come from the common lymphoid progenitor and require IL-7 for development and maintenance.

questions.stem:
A 24-year-old recent immigrant from Russia, HIV positive on ART, describes a long history of weight loss, night sweats and persistent cough. Imaging and biopsy of his lungs reveals apical granulomas with central caseous necrosis. Surrounding the necrotic areas are large cells with abundant pale cytoplasm.
Which of the following surface markers is most specific for these cells?
questions.explanation: The caseating granulomas of TB almost always contain large epithelioid macrophages with pale pink granular cytoplasm and surface CD14 at the periphery. CD14 is a surface marker of the monocyte-macrophage cell lineage. CD4 and CD8 are surface markers of T helper and cytotoxic T cells, respectively. CD20 is a B cell surface marker.

questions.stem:
A 29-year-old woman with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RRMS) is planning to conceive. She is currently stable on ocrelizumab, with no relapses or new lesions on MRI in the past 18 months. She asks about the course of MS during and after pregnancy and whether she should continue treatment.
Which of the following statements best reflects current evidence regarding MS disease activity during pregnancy and the postpartum period?

options.text: Disease activity typically increases in the second and third trimesters of pregnancy
Ocrelizumab is considered safe and routinely continued during pregnancy
Relapse risk decreases during pregnancy and increases postpartum
There is no association between breastfeeding and postpartum relapse rates

questions.explanation:
Relapse risk decreases during pregnancy and increases postpartum
· Pregnancy, particularly the second and third trimesters, is associated with a reduction in relapse rates, likely due to immunological shifts toward a more anti-inflammatory (Th2-dominant) profile. However, relapse rates rebound in the first 3­6 months postpartum, often returning to or exceeding pre-pregnancy levels.
· Incorrect Options o A. Disease activity generally decreases, not increases, during later pregnancy. o B. Ocrelizumab is not routinely continued during pregnancy due to limited data and potential foetal B-cell depletion; guidelines advise stopping it several months prior to conception. o D. While earlier studies were inconclusive, recent evidence suggests exclusive breastfeeding may reduce relapse risk postpartum, particularly when disease-modifying therapy (DMT) is delayed.



9





Reference: Hughes SE, Spelman T, Gray OM, et al. "Predictors and dynamics of postpartum relapses in multiple sclerosis: A prospective study." Neurology. 2022;98(4):e389-e398. Dobson R, Dassan P, Roberts M, et al. "UK consensus on pregnancy in multiple sclerosis: `Association of British Neurologists' guidelines." Pract Neurol. 2019;19(2):106-114.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about melanoma is correct?

options.text: Superficial spreading melanoma is the most common melanoma subtype
Lentigo maligna is most common on the limbs

Superficial spreading melanoma is the subtype of melanoma most strongly
associated with cumulative UV exposure

Acral lentiginous melanoma is an uncommon subtype of melanoma that is associated
with significant UV-exposure

questions.explanation: Superficial spreading melanoma accounts for 70% of melanomas. Lentigo maligna is strongly associated with large amounts of cumulative UV exposure and most commonly arises in the sun-exposed face and neck. Acral melanoma only accounts for around 2% of melanoma cases in Australia, it is more common in individuals with darker skin and is not caused by UV radiation.

questions.stem:
A 62-year-old female with acute kidney injury secondary to sepsis is being monitored in the intensive care unit.
Her laboratory results are as follows: Urea: 36 mmol/L (2.5-7.5 mmol/L) Potassium: 5.9 mmol/L (3.5-5.0 mmol/L) Creatinine: 500 µmol/L (60-120 µmol/L)
She is becoming increasingly lethargic and reports feeling nauseous. On examination, she is confused.
Which of the following is the most appropriate reason to start dialysis in this patient?
questions.explanation: In patients with acute kidney injury, the presence of uraemic symptoms (such as nausea, confusion, lethargy, or pericarditis) is a strong indication for starting dialysis, even if lab results like urea and creatinine are elevated but not at critical levels.

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old man is newly diagnosed with bronchiectasis. He has a history of rheumatoid arthritis and is managed with methotrexate and low dose prednisolone.
What is the next step in his initial management?
questions.explanation:
Airway clearance aims to mobilise secretions and aid expectoration, provide control of cough and reduce exacerbations. It is an essential part of initial management (answer A is correct). There is no role for ceasing DMARD therapy in all patients with CTDassociated bronchiectasis unless there are significant infective complications. DMARDs are required for controlling disease activity (answer C is incorrect) which has been associated with worse lung disease. If the patient has repeated infections/exacerbations, then introduction of a macrolide could be considered (answer B is incorrect). There is no role for admission for `tune up' antibiotics in bronchiectasis (answer D is incorrect).



10

questions.stem: In a patient newly diagnosed with axial spondyloarthritis, which of the following agents is most likely to improve their symptoms?
questions.explanation:
Axial symptoms respond poorly to synthetic DMARDs such as Methotrexate or Sulfasalazine (although these do work for peripheral arthritis). Interestingly while Prednisolone works well for many forms of arthritis, it also has a limited role for axial symptoms, and over very high doses are needed even to provide a modest benefit (and hence they are avoided). NSAIDs (either COX-1 or COX-2) have good efficacy in spondyloarthritis and are first line therapy. IL-6 inhibitors such as Tocilizumab work for rheumatoid arthtitis and GCA but don't have efficacy in spondyloarthritis.
Reference Navarro-Compán V, Sepriano A, El-Zorkany B, et al. Axial spondyloarthritis. Ann Rheum Dis 2021;80:1511­1521

questions.stem: Which of the following clinical or imaging findings is most specific for chronic diastolic dysfunction rather than an acute elevation in filling pressures?
questions.explanation: Left atrial enlargement reflects chronic elevation of left ventricular filling pressures and is a hallmark of longstanding diastolic dysfunction. It is more specific for chronicity than transient markers like elevated TR velocity or NT-proBNP.

questions.stem: Which postmenopausal woman has the highest risk of fracture?

options.text: Ostoepaenia by DXA, no prior fracture
Osteoporosis by DXA, no prior fracture
Normal bone density by DXA, prior fracture

questions.explanation: A person who has suffered a fragility fracture is 3-5x more likely to have another fracture within the next year, compared to someone who has never had an osteoporotic fracture

questions.stem:
A 48-year-old vasculopath presented with severe abdominal pain was found to have a long segment of intestinal ischaemia. He required a small bowel resection to remove the segment of ischaemic bowel.
Which of the below scenarios would result in the highest rate of bowel adaptation?

options.text: Removal of a section of the jejunum with an anastomosis between the remaining jejunum and ileum. The ileum, ileocaecal valve and colon are intact
Removal of the entire ileum, ileocaecal valve and part of the colon
Removal of the entire small bowel including the ileocaecal valve

Removal of just the ileum and ileocaecal valve



15

questions.explanation:
The ileum is better able to adapt after bowel resection compared to the jejunum. Having residual ileum is advantageous as it preserves it's functions including vitamin B12 and bile acid absorption. Resection of the ileum disrupts the enterohepatic circulation resulting in bile acid deficiency and malabsorption of fat as well as fat soluble vitamins. Loss of the ileocaecal valve is a poor prognostic factor as it helps regulate the passage of fluid and nutrients from the ileum to the colon. In short gut syndrome, the colon can adapt to absorb fermented carbohydrates which can provide up to 50% of the daily energy requirements.



11

questions.stem:
Scott and Lucy are planning to have a child but are concerned about having a child with spinal muscular atrophy (SMA). Lucy had a daughter with a previous partner that died from SMA. Scott has no family history of SMA. The carrier frequency of SMA in the general population is 1 in 50.
What is the risk of the couple having a child affected with SMA?
questions.explanation: Lucy is an obligate carrier. Scott is considered same risk as population as being a carrier: 1 in 50. Each parent has a 1 in 2 chance of passing on mutation if a carrier, so combined risk is 1 in 4. So, risk of affected child is 1 x 1 in 50 x 1 in 4 = 1 in 200.

questions.stem:
A 90-year-old woman with advanced dementia, bedbound and non-verbal, is admitted with aspiration pneumonia. She has previously been hospitalised for the same reason. Her carer asks whether a feeding tube should be inserted to prevent future aspiration.
What is the most evidence-based advice?

options.text: PEG feeding reduces the risk of aspiration and prolongs life
Nasogastric tube feeding is less invasive and should be trialled
Oral assisted feeding is preferred; tube feeding does not prevent aspiration
Feeding tubes should always be offered to avoid starvation

questions.explanation: Tube feeding in advanced dementia does not reduce the risk of aspiration or prolong life and may increase discomfort. Best practice supports careful hand feeding, which is associated with improved comfort and dignity at end of life.

questions.stem:
(Relates to the patient in Question 63) Over the course of the day, the patient's condition deteriorates, with increasing respiratory distress and secretions. She is no longer opening her eyes to voice and only withdrawing to pain.
Her carer, who has known the patient for over 20 years and sees her daily, has insisted on a trial of non-invasive ventilation (NIV) and intensive care unit (ICU) admission. Several other interested parties have arrived to the ward, with differing opinions.
Which of the following is the most appropriate party to act as the Person Responsible?

options.text: The patient's son, her Enduring Guardian, who lives interstate
The patient's carer, a paid employee of her Homecare provider
The family lawyer, who has known the patient the longest
The family general practitioner, who sees the patient on a monthly basis

questions.explanation: The appointed Enduring Guardian sits at the top of the hierarchy of surrogate decision makers in the event of incapacity. This overrides time length of relationship and frequency of contact.

questions.stem:
A 58-year-old man with a 20-year history of Crohn's disease presents with worsening fatigue over several weeks. He is currently experiencing a moderate flare with increased bowel frequency and elevated inflammatory markers. Laboratory results show haemoglobin 88 g/L, mean corpuscular volume 74 fL, ferritin 15 µg/L, transferrin saturation 7%, and CRP 52 mg/L.
What is the most appropriate treatment for his anaemia?
questions.explanation: This patient has anaemia due to absolute iron deficiency, as evidenced by a low ferritin and low transferrin saturation. However, the presence of active inflammation, as indicated by a raised CRP and symptomatic Crohn's flare, significantly impairs intestinal iron absorption and reduces the efficacy of oral iron therapy. Current guidelines recommend intravenous iron as the treatment of choice in inflammatory bowel disease patients with absolute iron deficiency and active inflammation. Oral iron is poorly tolerated and less effective in this context.

questions.stem: Natural Killer (NK) cells recognise and kill target cells primarily by...

options.text: Detecting antigen presented via MHC class II
Binding to pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) via TLRs
Recognising cells lacking MHC class I expression

questions.explanation: NK cells identify cells that are missing MHC Class 1 (which should be on all nucleated human cells). CD4 T cells bind to MHC Class II (A incorrect), TLRs are receptors on macrophages and dendritic cells that help to activate the innate immune system (B incorrect). B cells and plasma cells make antibodies (C incorrect).

questions.stem:
A 42-year-old cattle farmer presents with a five-day history of fever, headache, myalgia and conjunctival suffusion. Bloods reveal high inflammatory markers, with mild hyperbilirubinemia and acute kidney injury. Multiple blood cultures are reported as no growth to date at 48 hours. You make a clinical diagnosis of leptospirosis and commence the patient on IV benzylpenicillin on morning ward round. They are stable at this time of review. He has previously had penicillins on multiple occasions without issue. Later that day, you are called to see the patient due to development of fever, rigors and hypotension. There is no rash or wheeze or other features.
What is the most likely explanation for the clinical change?
questions.explanation: Reference ­ 2022 Delta Med Notes, ID overview part 1 Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction can occur after first few doses of antibiotic in leptospirosis in particular with penicillin. This can be severe. This is a non-allergic reaction and occurs within 24 hours of antibiotic treatment of spirochete infections, including syphilis, leptospirosis, lyme disease. It is likely a reaction to endotoxin like products released by the death of infecting micro-organisms, resulting in a stimulation of proinflammatory cytokines and a systemic inflammatory response. The reaction begins within 2 hours of treatment, peaks at approximately 8 hours, and disappears in 24 to 36 hours. Other options A ­ seems unlikely with history given of multiple prior times the patient has tolerated penicillin; B ­ you are told multiple blood cultures are no growth, and you have a plausible diagnosis with compatible risk factor; C ­ potentially but you have just started treatment so does not seem likely and the patient was stable in the morning.

questions.stem:
A 60-year-old woman with no medical comorbidities is diagnosed with metastatic breast cancer that is strongly HER2 positive. Her treating oncologist offers her Trastuzumab (Herceptin) + Pertuzumab + Taxane chemotherapy.
Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: Taxane (such as paclitaxel or docetaxel) based chemotherapy is commonly associated cold-induced peripheral neuropathy
Trastuzumab and Pertuzumab are generally not prescribed together

Trastuzumab has been associated with cardiac toxicity and requires regular
surveillance with transthoracic echocardiography or MUGA

questions.explanation:
Cold induced neuropathy is very well described with the chemotherapy agent oxaliplatin. Taxanes are associated with a peripheral neuropathy. Trastuzumab and pertuzumab are often prescribed together for metastatic HER2 positive breast cancer. These agents are associated with cardiac toxicity (left ventricular dysfunction/heart failure) and require monitoring throughout treatment. Trastuzumab does not have great CSF penetration ­ it is a large protein molecule and does not easily cross the blood-brain barrier.



12

questions.stem: Which of the following types of glomerulonephritis is most likely to cause graft failure in a kidney transplant?
questions.explanation: Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS) is the glomerulonephritis most likely to cause graft failure in kidney transplant recipients. FSGS has a high recurrence rate in transplanted kidneys, and its recurrence is often associated with rapid deterioration of renal function and graft loss.

questions.stem: When given to people with cystic fibrosis, azithromycin 500mg, three times per week, has been associated with which of the following?

options.text: Improved forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV1)

questions.explanation:
Azithromycin at a dose of 500mg three times per week has been shown to improve FEV1 at up to 6 months compared to placebo, increase time to exacerbation (answer A and C are correct). Prolonged corrected QT interval is a potential adverse effect (answer B is correct).
Reference: Southern KW, Solis-Moya A, Kurz D, Smith S. Macrolide antibiotics (including azithromycin) for cystic fibrosis. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews 2024, Issue 2. Art. No.: CD002203. DOI: 10.1002/14651858.CD002203.pub5. Accessed 20 April 2025.

questions.stem:
Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic autoimmune disease that targets synovial joints.
According to modern guidelines, the most appropriate initial therapy is...

options.text: NSAIDs and pain medications for symptomatic relief

questions.explanation:
The 2016 EULAR management recommendations for RA suggest starting treatment with methotrexate plus short-term glucocorticoids. Methotrexate should be rapidly escalated to its optimal dose, with folate substitution to reduce adverse events without compromising effectiveness. Glucocorticoids are typically given for a limited period (a few weeks to 4­5 months) to provide rapid anti-inflammatory activity until the methotrexate reaches full effectiveness. Monotherapy with biological DMARDs is generally not the first-line strategy, as it often leads to overtreatment without added benefit, and alternatives like sulfasalazine or leflunomide are used when methotrexate cannot be tolerated. NSAIDs primarily provide symptomatic relief and do not modify the underlying disease process or prevent joint damage.
Reference Smolen, J. S. et al. Rheumatoid arthritis. Nat. Rev. Dis. Primers 4, 18001 (2018)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly identifies the cell type primarily responsible for the formation of foam cells in atherosclerotic lesions?

options.text: Macrophages that engulf oxidized LDL via scavenger receptors

questions.explanation: Macrophages infiltrate the intima and ingest oxidized LDL via scavenger receptors (e.g., CD36, SR-A), becoming foam cells -- a hallmark of the fatty streak in early atherosclerosis.

questions.stem: A patient presents with thyrotoxicosis; thyroid uptake scan is shown below. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Factitious thyrotoxicosis due to levothyroxine ingestion

questions.explanation:
With factitious thyrotoxicosis (in contrast to other conditions with decreased scan uptake, thyroglobulin is low). A, B and D all others have increased uptake.



13

questions.stem:
A 45-year-old obese woman has been getting persistent diarrhoea after having a cholecystectomy. She finds her symptoms are worse after consuming foods high in fat. A stool infectious screen was negative, and her faecal calprotectin was not raised.
Which of the following medical therapies is the most appropriate for management of her bowel symptoms?

options.text: Rifaximin for small bowel bacterial overgrowth

questions.explanation: Bile acid malabsorption can occur after a cholecystectomy and can be managed with bile acid sequestrants such as cholestyramine.

questions.stem:
In a family with Huntington Disease it appears that in each successive generation there is earlier age of onset of symptoms and faster progression of disease.
Which of the following terms best explains this phenomenon?
questions.explanation: Anticipation (Inheritance)

questions.stem:
A 35-year-old man recovers from anaphylaxis after eating shellfish. He is observed for 4 hours and discharged with two adrenaline autoinjectors.
What is the best advice regarding preventing future reactions?

options.text: Take daily antihistamines to prevent reactions
Avoid shellfish completely and carry adrenaline autoinjectors at all times
Gradually increase shellfish exposure to build tolerance
No further precautions are necessary after recovery

questions.explanation: Strict allergen avoidance and carrying adrenaline are essential to prevent and manage future reactions. Regular antihistamines is not a suitable treatment. Desensitisation to food (e.g. peanut) is undergoing research but is not current standard practice. Referral to an allergist/immunologist would be appropriate with this clinical presentation.

questions.stem: Which of the following malignancies is most strongly associated with smoking?
questions.explanation: Lung squamous cell carcinoma is the most strongly associated with smoking from the options provided. It is one of the most common types of lung cancer in smokers, as smoking directly damages the airway epithelium leading to carcinogenesis. Lung adenocarcinoma, whilst still common in smokers, has a higher association with nonsmokers and is the more common histology seen in people with no smoking history. Lung adenocarcinoma is more likely to harbour driver mutations such as EGFR mutations or ALK rearrangements.

questions.stem:
A 58-year-old man presents with fever, malaise, and a non-productive cough. He is 6 months post kidney transplant and is on tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and 5mg prednisolone daily. Chest imaging reveals bilateral ground-glass opacities. Bronchoalveolar lavage is pending.
Which of the following is the most likely cause of his presentation in the context of recent kidney transplantation?
questions.explanation: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia typically presents with fever, dry cough and bilateral interstitial infiltrates and can effect immunocompromised hosts.

questions.stem: Which statement is true regarding treatment for severe obstructive sleep apnoea?

options.text: Treatment with continuous positive airway pressure increases Apnoea-Hypopnoea Index (AHI)
Treatment with continuous positive airway pressure normalises glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c)
Treatment with continuous positive airway pressure reduces motor vehicle accident risk

Treatment with a mandibular advancement splint is as effective as continuous positive airways pressure



19

questions.explanation:
Treatment with CPAP decreases AHI (answer A is incorrect). Treatment with CPAP has been demonstrated to improve HbA1c by 0.24% (answer B is incorrect as it stated `normalises' rather than improves). Treatment with CPAP reduces accident rate in people with OSA (answer C is correct). Overall, there are fewer treatment responders with a MAS oral device is than CPAP, particularly for severe OSA (answer D is incorrect).
References: Herth J, Sievi NA, Schmidt F, Kohler M. Effects of continuous positive airway pressure therapy on glucose metabolism in patients with obstructive sleep apnoea and type 2 diabetes: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Eur Respir Rev. 2023 Sep 6;32(169):230083. doi: 10.1183/16000617.0083-2023. PMID: 37673425; PMCID: PMC10481331.
Risk of traffic accidents in patients with sleep disordered breathing: reduction with nasal CPAP. ERJ 1996 9(12): 2606-2611; DOI: https://doi.org/10.1183/09031936.96.09122606

questions.stem: What are the earliest, though non-specific, signs of systemic sclerosis often reported by patients?

options.text: Gastric antral vascular ectasias and dysphagia
Renal crisis and pulmonary arterial hypertension

questions.explanation:
Raynaud phenomenon and fatigue The earliest signs of systemic sclerosis include Raynaud phenomenon and fatigue, although these are non-specific and can have several alternative causes. While skin thickening and joint contractures are manifestations of the disease, they are typically later developments due to collagen deposition. Renal crisis and pulmonary arterial hypertension are serious complications and involve specific organ systems, and gastric antral vascular ectasias and dysphagia are manifestations of gastrointestinal involvement.
Volkmann, E. R., Andréasson, K., & Smith, V. (2023). Systemic sclerosis. Lancet, 401, 304­318.



14

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most common cardiac arrhythmia observed in endurance athletes, such as marathon runners?
questions.explanation: Endurance athletes, especially those involved in long-term, high-intensity training such as marathon running, are at increased risk of developing atrial fibrillation (AF). This is thought to be due to atrial remodeling, increased vagal tone, and low-grade chronic inflammation.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a cause of secondary hyperparathyroidism?
questions.explanation: See the conditions below. These conditions also need to be excluded before so-called normocalcaemic hyperparathyroidism is diagnosed, a somewhat controversial entity

questions.stem:
A 26-year-old woman with ulcerative colitis is well controlled on 8 weekly infliximab. She is planning to conceive with her partner.
Which of the following statements is correct regarding infliximab?

options.text: Infliximab should be stopped prior to conceiving

Infliximab is safe to continue throughout pregnancy and live vaccines can be given to
the newborn

The infliximab dose should be doubled during pregnancy as uncontrolled disease
activity is associated with spontaneous miscarriage and stillbirth

Infliximab is safe to continue throughout pregnancy and live vaccines should be
avoided in the newborn in the first year of life

questions.explanation: Infliximab is safe to continue throughout pregnancy and live vaccines should be avoided in the first year of life (Australian IBD consensus statement). Although there is an increased risk of miscarriage and stillbirth if IBD disease activity is not controlled, there is currently no evidence to empirically escalate medical therapy during pregnancy.

questions.stem:
Denis and Trudy are having their first child together. They are unrelated. Trudy's sister recently had a child with Tay-Sachs disease. The carrier frequency of Tay-Sachs disease in the general population is 1 in 100.
What is the risk to their child of developing Tay-Sachs disease?
questions.explanation: Trudy's risk of being a carrier is 1 in 2. Her sister is a carrier and so one of their parents must be a carrier too. Denis' risk of being a carrier is the general population risk which is 1 in 100. So, the couple's risk of having an affected child is (1 in 2) x (1 in 100) x (1 in 4) = 1 in 800.

questions.stem:
A 50-year-old man is referred for investigation of persistent lymphocytosis noted on routine blood testing. A peripheral blood film shows numerous small mature-appearing lymphocytes with scant cytoplasm. Flow cytometry reveals a clonal B-cell population expressing CD5, CD19, CD23, and kappa light chain restriction.
What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The two main differentials here are CLL and mantle cell lymphoma. The immunophenotype of CD5+ and CD23+ is most characteristic of chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL). Mantle cell lymphoma may also express CD5 but typically lacks CD23. Splenic marginal zone lymphoma does not express CD5 or CD23 and often presents with splenomegaly and cytopenias. Hairy cell leukaemia is characterised by CD11c, CD103, and annexin A1 expression, with unique morphology (lymphocytes have villous projections, hence are called hairy cells) and typically causes pancytopenia rather than isolated lymphocytosis.

questions.stem:
A fit 60-year-old gentleman presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of jaundice, lethargy, and pruritus. On examination, he is jaundiced with a palpable upper abdominal mass and no signs of ascites. A CT abdomen/pelvis reveals a 3 cm pancreatic mass with common bile duct dilatation. A subsequent biopsy confirms pancreatic adenocarcinoma.
Which of the following tumour markers is most useful for monitoring treatment response in this patient?
questions.explanation:
Serum CA19.9 is most commonly elevated in patients with upper gastrointestinal malignancies, including pancreatic cancer, biliary cancer, hepatocellular cancer, and less commonly gastric cancer. It is also frequently elevated in patients with cancers other than pancreatic cancer; hence, it is not recommended as a screening test for pancreatic cancer, but useful in monitoring treatment response and disease progression.



15





Reference UpToDate "Clinical manifestations, diagnosis and staging of exocrine pancreatic cancer ­ section on
carbohydrate antigen 19-9)"

questions.stem:
A 50-year-old man with end stage renal disease on haemodialysis presents with a serum potassium of 7.5 mmol/L (3.5 ­ 5 mmol/L) after missing a few dialysis sessions. His ECG shows peaked T waves.
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: 30g resonium and repeat testing of serum potassium

questions.explanation: The reason being is calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane and reduces the risk of arrhythmias due to hyperkalemia. Immediate dialysis should also be done but the next step would be calcium gluconate

questions.stem:
A 59-year-old woman is referred for evaluation of chronic fatigue, morning headaches, and excessive daytime sleepiness. She has a BMI of 47 kg/m² and a neck circumference of 44 cm. Her Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is 18/24. She does not smoke and has no known lung disease. Apart from a resting SpO2 of 89%, cardiorespiratory examination is unremarkable.
A chest X-ray shows normal lung fields and heart size. Spirometry is within predicted normal limits. An arterial blood gas on room air is collected (below).

Parameter pH PaCO PaO

Result 7.38 55 mmHg 64 mmHg

Normal Range 7.35 ­ 7.45 35 ­ 45 mmHg 75 ­ 100 mmHg



21





Parameter SaO HCO Lactate Na K Cl

Result 90% 31 mmol/L 1.2 mmol/L 138 mmol/L 4.1 mmol/L 100 mmol/L

Normal Range 94 ­ 98% 22 ­ 28 mmol/L 0.5 ­ 2.2 mmol/L 135 ­ 145 mmol/L 3.5 ­ 5.0 mmol/L 98 ­ 107 mmol/L

Which of the following is the most likely explanation for the ABG result?
A. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) B. Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) C. Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome D. Pulmonary embolism

Extended Question Questions 89 & 90 are about stroke and cerebral vascular territories.

Which vascular territory best corresponds with the described clinical scenarios (below)?

Answer options:

options.text: Left Anterior Cerebral Artery - Deep Branches
Left Middle Cerebral Artery - Superior Division

Left Middle Cerebral Artery - Deep (Lenticulostriate)

questions.explanation: This ABG demonstrates compensated type 2 respiratory failure with an elevated PaCO (55 mmHg) and hypoxaemia (PaO 64 mmHg), as well as compensatory metabolic alkalosis (elevated HCO 31 mmol/L). Furthermore, the A-a gradient is narrow (17, expected A-a gradient for age 18.8) in keeping with hypoventilation as the cause of hypoxaemia. This, in the context of severe obesity and absence of other causes is most in keeping with OHS. COPD is unlikely given the absence of a smoking history or obstructive ventilatory defect on spirometry. Congenital central hypoventilation syndrome (CCHS) is a rare childhood-onset disorder and very unlikely to present for the first time in a 59-year-old. PE typically causes hypoxaemia with low or normal PaCO due to hyperventilation. Chronic CO retention and bicarbonate compensation are not features of acute PE.
